title: Batching Defaults (SLO Tiers) — Error Budget Impact Analysis + Guardrails (SRE)
preview:
Doc intent
This doc estimates the expected error-budget impact of shipping “workload-aware batching defaults” (fast / standard / cost-efficient latency SLO tiers), and defines SRE guardrails + alert routing for the staged rollout.

Audience: SRE on-call, Runtime on-call, Observability, Release Engineering, Hosted API PM rollout owners.
Owner: SRE (Sean). Runtime implementation owner: Sofia/Lena team.

TL;DR
- If we keep fast-tier tail protections strict and maintain overload backpressure behavior, the change should be neutral-to-positive on error budget for standard traffic (better queue stability due to consistent defaults, fewer customer misconfigs).
- Primary error-budget risks are (1) fast-tier p99 regressions under mixed workloads, (2) queue blowups in cost-efficient tier during bursty concurrency, and (3) telemetry/label cardinality incidents masking real performance signals (already seen once in early canary).
- We will treat this as a “latency SLO impacting” launch: progressive rollout with hard abort criteria and paging on fast-tier p99 breaches.

Definitions and scope
- The “tier” is a policy selection that maps to scheduler knobs (e.g., max_wait_ms, max_batch_tokens, admission control settings, prefill/decode balancing, overload mode behavior).
- This analysis covers Hosted API and Dedicated default behavior changes. Private deployments follow later and are out of this rollout gate set.
- SLO tier numeric targets are defined in the canonical Confluence doc (linked). Here we use those targets via derived SLI thresholds used in alerting/gates.

SLIs/SLOs that consume error budget (what we will page on)
We model error budget consumption across two classes:

A) Availability SLO (request success)
- SLI: “successful completion rate” (HTTP 2xx + model-level success) excluding customer-caused invalid requests.
- Primary failure modes relevant to batching: overload-induced 429/503, scheduler timeouts, OOM kills, watchdog resets.

B) Latency SLO (tier-specific)
We track two latency SLIs (because batching changes affect them differently):
- Streaming: TTFT (time to first token) and stream jitter (token gap p99) for fast tier.
- Non-streaming: end-to-end request latency (prefill + decode) for standard and cost-efficient.

Error budget policy note
- We count tier-SLO compliance separately from global API availability. A tier regression that violates fast-tier p99 consumes fast-tier budget even if overall platform availability stays healthy.
- For Dedicated: we still enforce guardrails but route alerts primarily to the owning account SRE + runtime (less noise to global on-call); Dedicated customer SLAs are enforced per-cluster.

Baseline (pre-change) and expected impact (post-change)
Baseline assumptions (based on last 30d + previous batching experiments; validate during canary):
- Majority of Hosted traffic defaults to “standard-like” behavior today, but actual knob values vary due to historic model-specific configs and customer overrides.
- Fast-tier users today are largely self-selecting via manual batching knobs and/or smaller max_wait budgets.

Expected positive effects
1) Fewer foot-gun configs reaching production
- Standardized defaults reduce the long tail of “bad batching” configs (over-aggressive wait budget, batch token caps too high, missing backpressure) that create latency spikes.
- Expect fewer incident tickets like “p95 suddenly up after config change” attributable to customer batching params.

2) Better overload behavior consistency
- With tier-defined overload modes, load shedding thresholds and queue admission become more predictable. SRE can tune alert thresholds on stable behavior, rather than per-model configuration drift.

3) Improved throughput per GPU at steady-state
- Higher throughput under standard/cost-efficient should reduce saturation time during bursts (indirectly preventing p95/p99 from falling off a cliff).

Primary risk factors (error budget consumers)
R1) Fast-tier p99 regression under mixed workloads
- Mechanism: continuous batching + mixed prompt lengths can cause long-prefill requests to delay short decode work if scheduler fairness slips.
- Symptom: TTFT p99 and/or token-gap p99 increase while median looks OK (classic tail regression).
- Risk level: High (fast tier is explicitly a tail-latency promise; small regressions are visible to customers).

R2) Cost-efficient tier queue blowups under burst (backpressure too late)
- Mechanism: more aggressive batching improves throughput but increases queueing delay if concurrency spikes and we over-admit.
- Symptom: queue depth climbs, p95 balloons, then 429/503 if the system sheds late; can also induce GPU OOM resets if batch tokens spike.
- Risk level: Medium-High. We accept higher latency in cost-efficient, but still need bounded tail and controlled degradation.

R3) Starvation / fairness bugs (correctness-adjacent)
- Mechanism: if anti-starvation safeguards are incomplete, a class of requests may be perpetually deprioritized (especially long prompts or non-streaming behind streaming).
- Symptom: extreme p99.9, timeouts, sporadic “request stuck” support cases.
- Risk level: Medium but treated as SEV-2 if it causes timeouts.

R4) Telemetry cardinality / observability cost spikes
- Mechanism: adding “tier” plus other labels at too-high cardinality increases metrics volume; worst case we lose visibility due to pipeline throttling.
- Symptom: metrics ingestion latency, dropped series, missing dashboards; may also manifest as “all green” while reality regresses.
- Risk level: Medium; mitigated by cardinality controls/hotfix (see linked PRs).

Projected error budget impact (back-of-envelope)
Important: This section is directional. We will treat canary measurements as source of truth.

Scenario 0 (most likely): Neutral overall; fast tier slightly riskier, standard slightly better
- Standard: p95 improves modestly during peak due to better batching; fewer customer misconfigs reduces incident rate.
- Fast: p99 risk increases during early rollout due to new admission control and wait budgets (configuration bugs rather than fundamental). Expect to stabilize after 1–2 tuning iterations.
- Budget outlook: overall platform error budget unchanged; fast-tier error budget is the limiting factor and must be protected with gates.

Scenario 1 (good): Positive net improvement
- Standard throughput gains reduce overload incidence => fewer 429/503 during regional bursts.
- Budget outlook: availability budget improves; latency budgets stable.

Scenario 2 (bad): Fast-tier p99 regression in a single model family/region
- Typical pattern: one model family + one GPU class; regression only visible under mixed prompt length + streaming.
- Budget outlook: fast-tier p99 consumes budget quickly; can create customer-visible incidents even if global aggregate is fine.

Guardrail thresholds (hard gates) for rollout
We will gate progression with tier-specific pass/fail criteria. “Hard gate” means we do not expand rollout until resolved OR we roll back.

Guardrail set A — Fast tier tail latency (paging, hard gate)
- Trigger window: 10–15 minutes sustained + confirmed not due to upstream client behavior.
- Gate 1: TTFT p99 > (fast-tier target + 10%) in any top region for any of top N models (N=5 by traffic) during canary.
- Gate 2: Token-gap p99 (stream jitter) > (fast-tier target + 15%) sustained.
- Gate 3: Request timeout rate > baseline + 0.05% absolute (timeouts are the “red line”).
Actions:
- Freeze rollout expansion.
- Page Runtime on-call immediately (SEV-2 for sustained breach; SEV-1 if widespread customer impact).
- SRE to prepare rollback if not improving within 30 minutes or if budget burn rate exceeds 2x.

Guardrail set B — Standard tier latency + error rates (ticket + hard gate on severe)
- Gate 1: End-to-end latency p95 > (standard target + 15%) sustained 30 minutes on canary slice.
- Gate 2: 429/503 rate increases > 0.2% absolute over baseline for 15 minutes (indicates overload/backpressure issues).
Actions:
- Open incident ticket (no page unless > 0.5% absolute or multiple regions).
- Rollback if correlated with tiered defaults flag and persists > 60 minutes.

Guardrail set C — Cost-efficient tier queue stability (ticket, hard gate on queue blowup)
- Gate 1: Queue depth p95 > 2x baseline for > 20 minutes.
- Gate 2: Scheduler “admission rejects” or load-shed events > baseline + 50% (rate) while latency also increases.
- Gate 3: GPU OOM / worker restart rate > baseline + 2x.
Actions:
- Freeze expansion; consult runtime to tighten guardrails (max_batch_tokens cap, earlier shedding).
- Rollback if OOM/restarts spike (protect fleet health).

Guardrail set D — Observability pipeline health (hard gate)
- Gate 1: Metrics ingestion drop/delay for tier-labeled metrics > 5 minutes (missing panels is a launch blocker).
- Gate 2: Cardinality budget exceeded (as per telemetry platform thresholds) OR ingestion cost anomaly alert fires.
Actions:
- Disable tier label dimensions or apply cardinality controls (hotfix path).
- Do not expand rollout until dashboards stable for 24h.

Alert routing (who gets paged vs ticketed)
Principles
- Page only on customer-visible or budget-burning events that require immediate mitigation.
- Route “actionable but non-urgent” to tickets with clear ownership (Runtime vs SRE vs Observability).
- For Dedicated: avoid paging global on-call for single-tenant issues unless it indicates platform-wide regression.

Pager policy
- Fast tier p99 breaches: PAGE Runtime on-call; PAGE SRE on-call as secondary only if multi-region or unclear attribution.
- Elevated timeouts / errors (any tier): PAGE SRE on-call (availability first); Runtime added if scheduler indicators implicated.
- Queue blowups / OOM spikes: PAGE SRE on-call if impacting multiple hosts or causing cascading restarts; otherwise ticket Runtime.
- Metrics pipeline health: PAGE Observability on-call if panels/SLIs missing; SRE informed but not primary.

Ticket routing (examples)
- “Standard tier p95 drift, no errors, single region”: ticket Runtime Scheduling (Sofia) + FYI SRE.
- “Dedicated tenant override not taking effect”: ticket Platform Config (Ethan/Benji) + Dedicated support.
- “Dashboard missing tier breakdown labels”: ticket Observability-pack owner (Allison/Caleb) referencing dashboard spec.

Rollout stages and SRE go/no-go gates
Stage 0 — Internal canary (Redwood dogfood)
- Scope: internal traffic + small percent of hosted.
- Required to proceed:
  - All guardrails green for 48 hours.
  - Perf canary passing tier gates (ENG-18428).
  - No open SEV-2+ incidents attributable to tiered defaults.

Stage 1 — Hosted API subset (small regions/models)
- Start at low % (e.g., 1–5%) for standard tier only; then enable fast; then cost-efficient.
- Required to expand:
  - Fast-tier p99 within +5% of target (stricter than hard gate) for top models in the region.
  - 429/503 not worse than baseline.
  - Support readiness: runbook updated + support macro ready.

Stage 2 — Dedicated opt-in
- Enable only with explicit customer opt-in + per-tenant override confirmed.
- Required to proceed:
  - Verified precedence behavior (customer override > route policy > tier default) in a test tenant.
  - Dedicated cluster health checks (OOM/restarts stable).

Stage 3 — Broader default
- Expand region-by-region.
- Required to proceed:
  - Error budget burn rate not exceeding 1.2x baseline over 7 days for tiered-defaults cohort.
  - No unresolved telemetry/label issues.

Rollback criteria (explicit)
Immediate rollback (kill switch) if ANY of the following:
- Fast-tier: sustained TTFT p99 breach beyond hard gate in ≥2 regions OR confirmed widespread customer impact.
- Any tier: timeouts increase > 0.1% absolute sustained 10 minutes.
- Any tier: fleet instability (GPU worker restarts/OOM) > 2x baseline sustained 15 minutes.
- Observability: loss of tier SLI visibility for > 10 minutes during active rollout.

Rollback process notes
- Use feature flag kill switch (ENG-18431 / PR-28480). Ensure rollback is reversible and does not require runtime restart if possible.
- After rollback: preserve sampled traces + decision reason codes for postmortem (ENG-18426).

What dashboards/metrics SRE needs (minimum viable)
- Latency histograms broken down by tier (fast/standard/cost-efficient), model family, region.
- Streaming TTFT p50/p95/p99; token-gap p95/p99.
- Queue depth distribution; batch size distribution (tokens and requests).
- Load shedding / admission reject rates with reason.
- “Profile selection reason code” counters (default vs override vs model constraint vs overload mode).
- Telemetry pipeline health (ingestion lag, dropped series) with tier label cardinality monitoring.

Known issues / watchouts
- Metric cardinality: We already saw an early canary incident where tier labels combined with route/model labels created too many series. The fix is to constrain label combinations (prefer model_family over model_id; sample high-cardinality reason code events). Treat missing dashboards as a launch-stopper.
- Customer messaging: Some customers will interpret “fast tier” as a hard guarantee. Ensure status page and support messaging clarify “targets / best effort” wording consistent with PM/Docs copy.
- Dedicated override precedence: historically a source of confusion; confirm in staging and document with examples.

Open questions (need answers before Stage 1 expands)
1) Final numeric SLO thresholds per tier in alert rules: confirm with ENG-18421 outputs and update alert configs accordingly.
2) What is the official “top N models” list for gating? (Traffic-weighted per region vs global.) Proposed: per region top 5 by request count for the canary window.
3) How do we handle mixed streaming + non-streaming routes under a single route policy? Need a clear evaluation order for tier selection and metrics attribution.
4) Dedicated: do we page global SRE if a single high-profile tenant breaches fast-tier SLO? Proposed: page Dedicated on-call + Runtime; notify global SRE only if correlated with platform regression signals.

Appendix: Draft alert definitions (human-readable)
- ALERT: FastTierTTFTp99High
  - Condition: ttft_ms_p99{tier="fast"} > fast_ttft_p99_target * 1.10 for 10m
  - Action: Page Runtime on-call (primary), SRE (secondary)

- ALERT: TieredBatchingErrorsHigh
  - Condition: (http_503 + http_429 + timeouts)/requests > baseline + 0.2% for 15m and feature_flag_on
  - Action: Page SRE on-call

- ALERT: CostEfficientQueueBlowup
  - Condition: queue_depth_p95{tier="cost_efficient"} > baseline*2 for 20m AND latency_p95 increased
  - Action: Ticket Runtime; page SRE if multi-region or fleet instability

- ALERT: TierMetricsMissing
  - Condition: tier-labeled SLI panels have no data OR ingestion lag > threshold for 5m
  - Action: Page Observability on-call; freeze rollout

Next actions
- Rafael/Aisha: translate guardrails into concrete Prometheus/Datadog queries (match dashboard spec) and confirm paging routing.
- Sofia/Julian: ensure overload-mode signals are emitted with stable tags and low cardinality.
- Marissa/Connor: incorporate rollback criteria into rollout checklist and release train launch brief.
- Nikhil: confirm staged rollout cohort definitions and customer comms triggers (status page criteria).

Change log
- 2026-02-14: initial draft (baseline risks + guardrail skeleton).
- 2026-03-10: added observability hard gate and telemetry incident learnings.
- 2026-04-24: updated rollback criteria and Dedicated routing guidance after canary results + support feedback.

title: Oncall prep checklist — Optimize Quality Gates rollout (SRE)
preview:
Purpose
- Ensure SRE/oncall is ready for the limited production rollout of “Quality guardrails gating for Optimize” (hosted API subset of routes/accounts).
- This checklist is specifically for: new alerts, dashboards, runbooks, and rehearsals.
- Target window: limited prod rollout starting ~2026-02; 24/7 oncall coverage expected.

Rollout summary (what’s new for oncall)
- Optimize recommendations (batching, caching, quant profile changes) are now blocked unless quality gates pass.
- Gate runs depend on eval-harness GPU capacity + artifact storage + Optimize service orchestration.
- Rollout controller will halt/pause canary promotion if gates fail (defense-in-depth: enforcement in Optimize apply boundary + rollout promotion).
- New failure modes:
  - “False positive” gate failures due to flakiness or variance
  - Gate run timeouts / queue backlog (especially regional GPU scarcity)
  - Gate enforcement bugs blocking legitimate deploys (customer-visible if customers rely on Optimize one-click)
  - Artifact link failures (Console shows incomplete results; support burden)

Definitions (for paging + triage)
- Gate run: evaluation triggered by proposed config diff vs baseline, producing pass/fail + metrics deltas + artifacts.
- Smoke gate: fast tier (small prompt subset) used for quick feedback (should not page unless blocking widely).
- Full gate: comprehensive suite; higher cost/latency.
- Flake rate: percent of suites/runs requiring rerun or producing inconsistent outcomes beyond tolerance.

1) Access / permissions (must be done before rollout)
[ ] Confirm oncall has Console access to gate run results + artifacts drill-down
    - Who: SRE oncall rotation owners
    - Verify: can view gate run state machine + reasons (failed metric, timeout, validation reject)

[ ] Confirm oncall has access to:
    - Optimize service logs (prod + staging)
    - Eval-harness job logs (prod) incl. retries and GPU scheduling
    - Observability pack dashboards (quality gates folder)
    - Alert manager routes (paging policy)

[ ] Confirm break-glass procedure for “stuck rollout due to gate infra outage”
    - NOTE: break-glass should not bypass the quality requirement silently; it must route through override workflow with audit log events.

2) Dashboards (validate exist + readable + have runbook links)
Dashboards to pin for oncall
[ ] Quality Gates Overview
    - Gate run volume, pass/fail, failure reasons breakdown
    - Dimensions: config_type (batching|cache|quant), model_family, region, route_id, account_tier

[ ] Gate Execution Health
    - Gate execution latency p50/p95/p99
    - Queue time vs run time
    - Timeout rate
    - Retry rate

[ ] Flakiness / Variance
    - Flake score distribution
    - Rerun count per suite
    - “Quarantined prompt sets” and trend

[ ] Rollout Coupling
    - Count of canary promotions blocked by gate
    - Auto-rollback triggers tied to gate failures

[ ] Customer impact / support correlation
    - “Apply blocked” events rate
    - Console error rate for gate artifact fetch
    - Top accounts/routes impacted in last 24h

Dashboard acceptance checks
[ ] Each panel has correct tags (region, model_family, config_type, gate_tier)
[ ] Panels have sane default time ranges (last 6h, last 24h)
[ ] Runbook links are present in dashboards (top-right description or alert annotations)
[ ] Drill-downs work: click-through to logs for gate_run_id

3) Alerts (what pages vs what tickets)
Paging principles (agreed w/ #eng-sre thread)
- Page only on likely infra/systemic issues or widespread customer impact.
- Do NOT page on single-run failures (that’s expected); alert on spikes, correlated timeouts, or inability to run gates.
- Flakiness should produce a ticket unless it is blocking significant rollout or causing widespread false positives.

Pager alerts (P1/P2)
[ ] P1: Gate infrastructure outage / cannot run gates
    - Signal: gate_run_start_success_rate drops below threshold OR eval-harness trigger endpoint error rate spike
    - Symptom: many runs stuck in PENDING/RUNNING beyond max wall clock

[ ] P1: Gate run timeouts spike (region scoped)
    - Signal: timeout_rate > X% over 15m AND run volume > N
    - Notes: likely GPU scarcity; check eu-west known issue pattern (SUP-18487)

[ ] P2: Gate execution latency SLO burn
    - Signal: p95 gate_end_to_end_latency exceeds policy bounds for 30m
    - Notes: this is about blocking pipelines too long (release friction).

[ ] P2: Rollout controller blocked promotions spike
    - Signal: promotions_blocked_by_gate increases > baseline AND reasons indicate infra (timeouts/errors) not genuine regressions

[ ] P2: Artifact retrieval failures impacting Console
    - Signal: artifact_link_fetch_error_rate spike or 404/permission errors

Non-paging alerts (ticket/Slack)
[ ] Flake rate elevated for a specific suite/prompt set
    - Action: create ticket / ping Applied ML for quarantine workflow

[ ] Specific model family fails gates frequently post-change (possible real regression)
    - Action: triage per runbook; route to Runtime/Applied ML; likely not infra.

Alert configuration checklist
[ ] Alert annotations include: gate_tier, config_type, region, model_family; and “next steps” snippet
[ ] Alerts link to:
    - Overview dashboard
    - Runbook: “Optimize Quality Gate Failures”
    - Query to list top gate_run_ids

4) Runbooks (confirm complete, tested, and oncall-friendly)
Primary runbook
- Confluence: runbook-optimize-quality-gate-failures

Runbook checklist
[ ] Clear decision tree: infra vs real regression vs flake
[ ] Commands/queries included:
    - Find gate_run_id from alert
    - Fetch gate state + failure reason
    - Locate eval-harness job id(s)
    - Identify baseline config + proposed diff
    - Identify affected routes/accounts

[ ] Explicit remediation steps:
    - For timeouts/backlog: scale eval worker pool / shift region / increase quota / shed non-critical runs
    - For flake: quarantine suite/prompt set; rerun with increased sample; widen confidence interval only with Applied ML sign-off
    - For enforcement bug: disable new gating feature flag for affected tier/route (if available) + require manual override path with audit

[ ] Escalation paths are listed (names + Slack channels)
    - SRE: Sean Gallagher / Allison Grant
    - Applied ML: Jonas Weber / Selene Huang
    - Runtime: Sofia Ivanova / Grace Kim
    - Console: Chloe Martin
    - Release Eng / CI: Connor O’Brien / Sahana Iyer

[ ] “Customer support handoff” section exists
    - What to tell support when a change is blocked (expected behavior vs suspected false positive)
    - What artifacts to attach (gate_run_id, suite ids, region, timestamps)

5) Rehearsal scenarios (game day) — must complete before limited prod
Goal: ensure oncall can diagnose quickly and knows what knobs are safe.

Scenario A: Gate run timeouts in a region (eu-west)
- Inject: throttle eval-harness workers in eu-west or simulate GPU scarcity
- Expected:
  - Timeout alert fires (region-tagged)
  - Oncall identifies queue backlog vs runtime issue
  - Mitigation: reroute gate runs to alternate region for eval (if policy allows) OR scale workers
- Success criteria: time-to-mitigate < 30 min; no unnecessary paging to Applied ML.

Scenario B: Flaky suite causes false failures
- Inject: introduce variance in one prompt set / enable known flaky suite
- Expected:
  - Non-paging flake alert/ticket; optional Slack notification
  - Oncall follows quarantine workflow (or engages Applied ML)
  - Rerun policy behaves as documented; aggregated result stable
- Success criteria: quarantine procedure understood; prevents alert storms.

Scenario C: Optimize apply blocked due to gating service error
- Inject: make Optimize-to-eval-harness trigger return 5xx
- Expected:
  - P1 gate infra outage alert
  - Oncall confirms “apply blocked” rate rises
  - Temporary mitigation: pause new Optimize auto-apply (if enabled) and communicate status; do NOT bypass silently
- Success criteria: correct comms + safe mitigation path documented.

Scenario D: Real regression detected (quant profile)
- Inject: use known long-context prompts where quant profile degrades
- Expected:
  - Gates fail for quant changes; rollout promotion blocked
  - Oncall correctly categorizes as “real regression” and routes to Runtime/Applied ML (no infra action)
- Success criteria: right escalation; avoids disabling gates.

Scenario E: Artifact links broken in Console
- Inject: break artifact store permissions or return 404
- Expected:
  - Console errors show; artifact retrieval alert triggers (P2)
  - Oncall identifies that gating may still be working; customer-facing UI is degraded
- Success criteria: keep gates enforced; fix artifact path; provide workaround instructions.

6) Comms plan (who to notify and when)
[ ] Create #eng-sre pinned message with:
    - dashboards links
    - runbook link
    - known issues
    - escalation list

[ ] During limited prod: daily status post (Mon–Fri)
    - pass rate, timeout rate, median gate latency, flake rate, blocked applies count
    - top offenders by region/model_family

[ ] Incident comms template (internal)
    - What broke (infra vs regression)
    - Customer impact (routes/accounts)
    - Mitigation (what changed, flags toggled)
    - Follow-ups (tickets)

7) Known issues / watch-outs (as of last edit)
- EU-west gate runs occasionally time out under GPU contention; retry policy can amplify queue depth.
- Some prompt sets exhibit variance under long-context; flake scoring must be monitored to avoid rerun storms.
- Console artifact links depend on correct object TTL + permissions; failures increase support load even if gating is healthy.

8) Open questions (need answers before GA)
- What is the hard SLO for gate execution latency by tier (self-serve vs enterprise)?
- Under prolonged eval-harness capacity shortage, do we:
  - block all applies (safe but high friction), or
  - allow time-bound overrides for specific tiers with explicit approvals?
- What is the maximum acceptable flake rate before auto-quarantine triggers globally?

9) Sign-off checklist (rollout readiness)
[ ] Dashboards live and validated in prod
[ ] Paging alerts tested (synthetic) and routed to correct oncall
[ ] Runbook reviewed and dry-run completed by at least 2 oncall engineers
[ ] Game day completed (Scenarios A–D at minimum)
[ ] Support handoff doc snippet prepared (what to collect, what to tell customers)

Change log
- 2026-02-18: added artifact retrieval alert + Console degradation scenario; aligned terminology with “smoke vs full” gates.
- 2026-02-06: initial draft based on rollout plan + observability pack PR.

title: Support escalation runbook alignment notes (SRE)
preview:
Purpose
- Capture current state of SRE/Eng runbooks that Support touches during escalations.
- Identify gaps that cause slow triage / noisy paging.
- Propose concrete changes to paging + bridge practices that align with the refreshed Support playbook (P0–P4) and Engineering incident severity (Sev0–Sev3).

Scope / constraints
- This doc is internal working notes for SRE + Support Ops.
- Do not include customer-sensitive details (names, contract terms, specific IPs). Use ticket links if needed.
- Proposed processes should not bypass #incidents / formal incident command when the impact crosses thresholds.

Attendees / sources
- Inputs from:
  - Support Ops: Dev Patel (playbook refresh), Owen Phillips (escalation SME)
  - SRE: Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes
  - Platform: Ethan Park, Mina Haddad
  - Observability: Allison Grant
  - Tooling/Jira: Dylan Brooks
- Referenced discussions: eng-oncall + incidents channel conventions; multiple Support threads about required paging context.

Current runbook inventory (what exists today)
Note: “exists” does not mean Support can find it quickly or has access to the right dashboards.

1) Hosted API / Gateway / Runtime
- Streaming disconnects / truncated streams
  - Runbook exists (SRE + Runtime oncall). Focused on gateway timeouts, upstream resets, and regional edge issues.
  - Missing: customer-facing workaround snippet + explicit “what to ask for” checklist.
- Latency regression triage
  - Runbook exists. Uses golden dashboards + release diff checks.
  - Missing: Support-safe “quick view” dashboards and a minimal triage path when dashboards are restricted.
- 5xx spikes / partial region degradation
  - Runbook exists with incident thresholds, but does not map cleanly to Support P0/P1 definitions.

2) Routing / Fallback behavior
- Fallback routing activation & pinned model behavior
  - Partial docs exist across routing design notes and SRE incident notes.
  - Missing: single authoritative runbook section that explains:
    - What signals confirm fallback is active
    - What policies can override pins (and which cannot)
    - What Support should say to customers (and what not to claim)

3) Quotas / Rate limiting
- 429 investigation
  - Runbook exists in Platform/Control Plane notes.
  - Missing: clear explanation of header semantics and how they can differ by endpoint during edge cases (should be fixed, but Support needs short-term guidance).

4) Dedicated
- Autoscaling instability / oscillation
  - Runbook exists (SRE + Dedicated capacity). Good internal detail.
  - Missing: “customer-actionable knobs” section (what Support/CS can safely suggest without SRE) and a simple decision tree.

5) Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Installer upgrade failures / stuck migrations
  - Runbook exists but requires tribal knowledge and references internal cluster tooling.
  - Missing: Support-ready artifact list + a safe rollback checklist that’s consistent across versions.

6) Audit logs pipeline
- Audit log delivery latency
  - Basic runbook exists (check backlog, pipeline health).
  - Missing: defined customer expectation language + threshold that triggers paging vs async investigation.

7) Console / Observability UX
- Missing region breakdowns / telemetry gaps
  - Scattered notes exist.
  - Missing: “known telemetry delay vs incident” guidance and what evidence Support should collect.

Where Support gets stuck (recurring gaps observed in escalations)
1) Missing “minimum viable context” for pages
- On-call often receives: “customer is down” without region/model/request IDs.
- Result: back-and-forth in Slack, delayed mitigation, and sometimes duplicate investigation.

2) Dashboards are not discoverable (or not Support-safe)
- Support does not have a canonical list of dashboards for:
  - streaming health
  - region error rate
  - audit log lag
  - routing/fallback signals
- Result: Support escalates earlier than needed or cannot validate mitigation.

3) Threshold mismatch: Support SLA language vs SRE paging thresholds
- Support wants strict update cadence; SRE wants pages when SLO-impacting thresholds are crossed.
- Confusion when the ticket is “high importance” but not “incident-worthy.”

4) Bridge/channel confusion
- When to keep in #support bridge vs when to move to #incidents is inconsistent.
- Some escalations spin up ad-hoc channels without incident bot conventions; artifacts get lost.

5) “Waiting on Customer” status breaks SLA clarity
- Support pauses while awaiting logs, but customer perceives silence.
- Internally, pages are sometimes triggered just because the conversation is stalled.

6) No standard place to capture mitigation decisions
- Workarounds and “safe retries/timeouts” guidance live in Slack threads and don’t make it into KB/runbooks.
- Support repeats the same instructions across tickets.

7) Ownership boundaries not obvious at 2am
- Platform vs SRE vs Runtime ownership is clear to leads but not to the person triaging.
- Result: wrong paging target, then a handoff.

Proposed changes (process + tooling)
A) Standardize the “page template” (Support -> On-call)
- Required fields (must be in first message or page is re-requested):
  - Customer tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) + severity (Support P0–P4)
  - Region(s)
  - Model + version (or “latest”)
  - Endpoint (chat/generate/embeddings/rerank) + streaming yes/no
  - Request IDs (>= 3) + approximate timestamps (UTC)
  - Error sample (sanitized) + HTTP status
  - Recent customer changes (SDK version, timeout settings, proxy/CDN, retry behavior)
  - Business impact summary (approx % traffic, number of affected users, hard deadline)
- Non-required but useful:
  - Links: SUP ticket, Slack bridge, any dashboards available to Support

B) Clarify “when to move to #incidents”
- Proposed rule of thumb (to align with incident practices):
  - Move to #incidents when:
    - Multiple customers impacted OR
    - Regional error/latency crosses known SLO-impacting threshold OR
    - There is credible risk of broad impact (e.g., auth outage, routing misconfig, widespread 5xx)
  - Keep in #support bridge when:
    - Single-customer configuration/edge issue AND
    - No SLO/region signal AND
    - Mitigation likely customer-side or account-level
- Add explicit examples to the playbook (streaming disconnects: depends on scope; audit log lag: incident only when lag > enterprise threshold + multiple tenants affected).

C) Create Support-safe “Quick View” dashboards + links from runbooks
- Action: ENG-4823 (add canonical dashboard links to runbooks + expose a Support-safe quick view set).
- Minimum set:
  - Hosted: error rate + latency by region + streaming health
  - Dedicated: autoscaling churn indicators + queue depth + latency
  - Private: installer upgrade error rate (if applicable) + deployment health basics
  - Audit logs: pipeline lag metric + backlog
  - Routing: fallback activation signal

D) Align SLA update cadence language with incident command reality
- Recommendation:
  - For P0/P1 enterprise tickets that are not incidents, set expectation that updates are time-boxed but can be “no change” updates (e.g., hourly) while investigation continues.
  - For incidents, updates follow incident comms policy and status page alignment.
- Explicitly separate:
  - “Support update cadence” (communication) vs
  - “Paging threshold” (technical signal)
- Capture in Confluence mapping doc; include examples to reduce debates mid-escalation.

E) Make Jira linking non-optional for bridges
- Action: INT-333 (Jira required fields + SLA timers + auto-link prompts).
- Add automation:
  - If a Slack bridge channel is created, bot posts the template and prompts for SUP ticket link.
  - If priority is P0/P1 and no request IDs after X minutes, bot reminds.

F) Incident bot improvements for discoverability
- Action: ENG-4830 (incident bot prompts for and pins related SUP tickets and customer impact summary).
- Expected outcome: post-incident, Support can find the full thread and decisions without spelunking Slack.

G) Known-issues sprint integration (reduce repeat escalations)
- For top issues (streaming timeouts, 429/header mismatch, dedicated autoscaling oscillation, private upgrade stuck, audit log lag):
  - Ensure each runbook has:
    - “Customer-safe workaround” paragraph that matches KB article
    - “When to escalate / page SRE” section with thresholds
  - Ensure each KB article links back to:
    - what signals Support can check
    - what artifacts to collect if escalation is needed

Runbook delta requests (specific edits we should make)
1) Streaming disconnects runbook
- Add:
  - Checklist: request IDs, client timeout, proxy/CDN, HTTP version, retry behavior
  - Approved mitigations: retry/backoff guidance; client keepalive settings; when to suggest disabling HTTP/2 (if applicable)
  - Escalation trigger: error rate threshold or multi-customer + same region

2) 429 / quota runbook
- Add:
  - “How to interpret rate-limit headers” section for Support
  - Note on known inconsistency edge case + link to fix work (ENG-4825) and interim troubleshooting

3) Audit log lag runbook
- Add:
  - Explicit expected delivery SLO language (internal) and how to message “degraded but working”
  - Page threshold tied to customer tier and lag duration
  - Link to dashboard/alert work (ENG-4824)

4) Dedicated autoscaling oscillation runbook
- Add:
  - Simplified decision tree for Support/CS
  - Safe knobs: min replicas, scale-down cooldown (guardrails; coordinate with SRE)
  - When to page SRE vs when to route to capacity planning

5) Private upgrade failures runbook
- Add:
  - Artifact bundle checklist (installer logs, cluster events, version info)
  - Safe rollback steps and explicit “stop points” (when not to proceed)
  - Link to diagnostics bundle work (ENG-4828)

Open questions (needs resolution before playbook is finalized)
- What are the final numeric thresholds for paging on:
  - streaming disconnect rate
  - audit log lag
  - embeddings timeout rate
  - fallback activation (is it ever page-worthy by itself?)
- Which dashboards can be made Support-safe without exposing sensitive multi-tenant internals?
- What is the expected after-hours policy for non-incident P1s (enterprise)?
  - Proposal: no guaranteed synchronous troubleshooting after-hours unless incident/SLO-impacting, but we still send acknowledgement + next update time.

Action items
- SRE (Sean/Rafael)
  - Draft “minimum viable context” template and get Support sign-off (Dev/Owen). Target: 2026-02-07.
  - Propose threshold wording that maps to severity matrix (coordinate with Support SLA vs SLO mapping). Target: 2026-02-09.
- Support Ops (Dev)

- Tooling (Dylan)

- Observability (Allison)

Notes to self (SRE)
- Keep the bar high for paging (protect on-call), but make comms predictable (protect customer trust).

text:
Purpose
- Capture current state of SRE/Eng runbooks that Support touches during escalations.
- Identify gaps that cause slow triage / noisy paging.
- Propose concrete changes to paging + bridge practices that align with the refreshed Support playbook (P0–P4) and Engineering incident severity (Sev0–Sev3).

Scope / constraints
- This doc is internal working notes for SRE + Support Ops.
- Do not include customer-sensitive details (names, contract terms, specific IPs). Use ticket links if needed.
- Proposed processes should not bypass #incidents / formal incident command when the impact crosses thresholds.

Attendees / sources
- Inputs from:
  - Support Ops: Dev Patel (playbook refresh), Owen Phillips (escalation SME)
  - SRE: Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes
  - Platform: Ethan Park, Mina Haddad
  - Observability: Allison Grant
  - Tooling/Jira: Dylan Brooks
- Referenced discussions: eng-oncall + incidents channel conventions; multiple Support threads about required paging context.

Current runbook inventory (what exists today)
Note: “exists” does not mean Support can find it quickly or has access to the right dashboards.

1) Hosted API / Gateway / Runtime
- Streaming disconnects / truncated streams
  - Runbook exists (SRE + Runtime oncall). Focused on gateway timeouts, upstream resets, and regional edge issues.
  - Missing: customer-facing workaround snippet + explicit “what to ask for” checklist.
- Latency regression triage
  - Runbook exists. Uses golden dashboards + release diff checks.
  - Missing: Support-safe “quick view” dashboards and a minimal triage path when dashboards are restricted.
- 5xx spikes / partial region degradation
  - Runbook exists with incident thresholds, but does not map cleanly to Support P0/P1 definitions.

2) Routing / Fallback behavior
- Fallback routing activation & pinned model behavior
  - Partial docs exist across routing design notes and SRE incident notes.
  - Missing: single authoritative runbook section that explains:
    - What signals confirm fallback is active
    - What policies can override pins (and which cannot)
    - What Support should say to customers (and what not to claim)

3) Quotas / Rate limiting
- 429 investigation
  - Runbook exists in Platform/Control Plane notes.
  - Missing: clear explanation of header semantics and how they can differ by endpoint during edge cases (should be fixed, but Support needs short-term guidance).

4) Dedicated
- Autoscaling instability / oscillation
  - Runbook exists (SRE + Dedicated capacity). Good internal detail.
  - Missing: “customer-actionable knobs” section (what Support/CS can safely suggest without SRE) and a simple decision tree.

5) Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Installer upgrade failures / stuck migrations
  - Runbook exists but requires tribal knowledge and references internal cluster tooling.
  - Missing: Support-ready artifact list + a safe rollback checklist that’s consistent across versions.

6) Audit logs pipeline
- Audit log delivery latency
  - Basic runbook exists (check backlog, pipeline health).
  - Missing: defined customer expectation language + threshold that triggers paging vs async investigation.

7) Console / Observability UX
- Missing region breakdowns / telemetry gaps
  - Scattered notes exist.
  - Missing: “known telemetry delay vs incident” guidance and what evidence Support should collect.

Where Support gets stuck (recurring gaps observed in escalations)
1) Missing “minimum viable context” for pages
- On-call often receives: “customer is down” without region/model/request IDs.
- Result: back-and-forth in Slack, delayed mitigation, and sometimes duplicate investigation.

2) Dashboards are not discoverable (or not Support-safe)
- Support does not have a canonical list of dashboards for:
  - streaming health
  - region error rate
  - audit log lag
  - routing/fallback signals
- Result: Support escalates earlier than needed or cannot validate mitigation.

3) Threshold mismatch: Support SLA language vs SRE paging thresholds
- Support wants strict update cadence; SRE wants pages when SLO-impacting thresholds are crossed.
- Confusion when the ticket is “high importance” but not “incident-worthy.”

4) Bridge/channel confusion
- When to keep in #support bridge vs when to move to #incidents is inconsistent.
- Some escalations spin up ad-hoc channels without incident bot conventions; artifacts get lost.

5) “Waiting on Customer” status breaks SLA clarity
- Support pauses while awaiting logs, but customer perceives silence.
- Internally, pages are sometimes triggered just because the conversation is stalled.

6) No standard place to capture mitigation decisions
- Workarounds and “safe retries/timeouts” guidance live in Slack threads and don’t make it into KB/runbooks.
- Support repeats the same instructions across tickets.

7) Ownership boundaries not obvious at 2am
- Platform vs SRE vs Runtime ownership is clear to leads but not to the person triaging.
- Result: wrong paging target, then a handoff.

Proposed changes (process + tooling)
A) Standardize the “page template” (Support -> On-call)
- Required fields (must be in first message or page is re-requested):
  - Customer tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) + severity (Support P0–P4)
  - Region(s)
  - Model + version (or “latest”)
  - Endpoint (chat/generate/embeddings/rerank) + streaming yes/no
  - Request IDs (>= 3) + approximate timestamps (UTC)
  - Error sample (sanitized) + HTTP status
  - Recent customer changes (SDK version, timeout settings, proxy/CDN, retry behavior)
  - Business impact summary (approx % traffic, number of affected users, hard deadline)
- Non-required but useful:
  - Links: SUP ticket, Slack bridge, any dashboards available to Support

B) Clarify “when to move to #incidents”
- Proposed rule of thumb (to align with incident practices):
  - Move to #incidents when:
    - Multiple customers impacted OR
    - Regional error/latency crosses known SLO-impacting threshold OR
    - There is credible risk of broad impact (e.g., auth outage, routing misconfig, widespread 5xx)
  - Keep in #support bridge when:
    - Single-customer configuration/edge issue AND
    - No SLO/region signal AND
    - Mitigation likely customer-side or account-level
- Add explicit examples to the playbook (streaming disconnects: depends on scope; audit log lag: incident only when lag > enterprise threshold + multiple tenants affected).

C) Create Support-safe “Quick View” dashboards + links from runbooks
- Action: ENG-4823 (add canonical dashboard links to runbooks + expose a Support-safe quick view set).
- Minimum set:
  - Hosted: error rate + latency by region + streaming health
  - Dedicated: autoscaling churn indicators + queue depth + latency
  - Private: installer upgrade error rate (if applicable) + deployment health basics
  - Audit logs: pipeline lag metric + backlog
  - Routing: fallback activation signal

D) Align SLA update cadence language with incident command reality
- Recommendation:
  - For P0/P1 enterprise tickets that are not incidents, set expectation that updates are time-boxed but can be “no change” updates (e.g., hourly) while investigation continues.
  - For incidents, updates follow incident comms policy and status page alignment.
- Explicitly separate:
  - “Support update cadence” (communication) vs
  - “Paging threshold” (technical signal)
- Capture in Confluence mapping doc; include examples to reduce debates mid-escalation.

E) Make Jira linking non-optional for bridges
- Action: INT-333 (Jira required fields + SLA timers + auto-link prompts).
- Add automation:
  - If a Slack bridge channel is created, bot posts the template and prompts for SUP ticket link.
  - If priority is P0/P1 and no request IDs after X minutes, bot reminds.

F) Incident bot improvements for discoverability
- Action: ENG-4830 (incident bot prompts for and pins related SUP tickets and customer impact summary).
- Expected outcome: post-incident, Support can find the full thread and decisions without spelunking Slack.

G) Known-issues sprint integration (reduce repeat escalations)
- For top issues (streaming timeouts, 429/header mismatch, dedicated autoscaling oscillation, private upgrade stuck, audit log lag):
  - Ensure each runbook has:
    - “Customer-safe workaround” paragraph that matches KB article
    - “When to escalate / page SRE” section with thresholds
  - Ensure each KB article links back to:
    - what signals Support can check
    - what artifacts to collect if escalation is needed

Runbook delta requests (specific edits we should make)
1) Streaming disconnects runbook
- Add:
  - Checklist: request IDs, client timeout, proxy/CDN, HTTP version, retry behavior
  - Approved mitigations: retry/backoff guidance; client keepalive settings; when to suggest disabling HTTP/2 (if applicable)
  - Escalation trigger: error rate threshold or multi-customer + same region

2) 429 / quota runbook
- Add:
  - “How to interpret rate-limit headers” section for Support
  - Note on known inconsistency edge case + link to fix work (ENG-4825) and interim troubleshooting

3) Audit log lag runbook
- Add:
  - Explicit expected delivery SLO language (internal) and how to message “degraded but working”
  - Page threshold tied to customer tier and lag duration
  - Link to dashboard/alert work (ENG-4824)

4) Dedicated autoscaling oscillation runbook
- Add:
  - Simplified decision tree for Support/CS
  - Safe knobs: min replicas, scale-down cooldown (guardrails; coordinate with SRE)
  - When to page SRE vs when to route to capacity planning

5) Private upgrade failures runbook
- Add:
  - Artifact bundle checklist (installer logs, cluster events, version info)
  - Safe rollback steps and explicit “stop points” (when not to proceed)
  - Link to diagnostics bundle work (ENG-4828)

Open questions (needs resolution before playbook is finalized)
- What are the final numeric thresholds for paging on:
  - streaming disconnect rate
  - audit log lag
  - embeddings timeout rate
  - fallback activation (is it ever page-worthy by itself?)
- Which dashboards can be made Support-safe without exposing sensitive multi-tenant internals?
- What is the expected after-hours policy for non-incident P1s (enterprise)?
  - Proposal: no guaranteed synchronous troubleshooting after-hours unless incident/SLO-impacting, but we still send acknowledgement + next update time.

Action items
- SRE (Sean/Rafael)
  - Draft “minimum viable context” template and get Support sign-off (Dev/Owen). Target: 2026-02-07.
  - Propose threshold wording that maps to severity matrix (coordinate with Support SLA vs SLO mapping). Target: 2026-02-09.
- Support Ops (Dev)
  - Ensure template lands in Confluence playbook + Slack workflow. Target: 2026-02-10.
- Tooling (Dylan)
  - Implement Jira required fields + reminders (INT-333). Target: week of 2026-02-10.
- Incident bot / Platform
  - Land SUP-ticket pinning improvements (ENG-4830). Target: end of sprint.
- Observability (Allison)
  - Define “Quick View” dashboards list; validate access model. Target: 2026-02-12.

Notes to self (SRE)
- Keep the bar high for paging (protect on-call), but make comms predictable (protect customer trust).
- If we make “Quick View” dashboards real, Support can often self-triage and page with better precision.
- Every page should feel like a handoff, not a request for someone else to start from scratch.

title: KV compaction canary — alert tuning notes (SRE working doc)
preview:
Context
- Canary: runtime-1.22 KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction behind feature flag.
- Goal of this doc: record what was noisy during canary, what we changed, and why (so we don’t re-learn this next rollout).
- Owners: SRE (Sean/Allison/Rafael) w/ runtime input (Omar/Samir).

Quick glossary (as used in alerts/dashboards)
- frag_ratio: fragmentation ratio in KV arena (0–1). Higher = more fragmented.
- compaction_bytes_moved: bytes moved due to compaction (rate + total).
- compaction_step_ms: time spent in compaction “step” (budgeted).
- eviction_events: sequences/prefixes evicted (tier-tagged).
- decode_p99_ms: p99 decode latency (route/model/tier).

What we learned (high-level)
1) The first wave of alerting tied compaction activity too directly to paging/latency spikes.
   - Compaction activity ≠ bad. Some compaction is expected and healthy, especially immediately after enabling.
2) p99-only alerting without gating on traffic + cohort size was noisy.
   - Small canary cohorts have spiky p99; need min request volume / sustained windows.
3) Fragmentation ratio is a leading indicator but also has benign oscillation.
   - We should alert on “high frag_ratio sustained + rising + no effective compaction” rather than a single threshold.

Timeline of tuning (rough)
- 2026-01-30: Canary cohort A (hosted, 2% traffic, 2 models). First set of alerts enabled.
- 2026-02-01: Noted noisy paging for decode_p99_ms and compaction_bytes_moved correlation (false positives).
- 2026-02-03: Incident thread (short-lived tail spike) prompted rollback criteria review (see slack://incidents/1767735109).
- 2026-02-06: Updated alert definitions + added gating (min volume + longer windows) and separated “compaction activity” from “latency regression”.
- 2026-02-10: Re-enabled automated rollback for cohort B after tuning.

Noisy alerts (and why they were noisy)
A) ALERT: “Compaction bytes moved high”
- Original trigger: compaction_bytes_moved_rate > 3 GiB/min for 5m (any tier)
- What happened:
  - Fired immediately after enabling compaction on a node that had accumulated fragmentation while flag was off.
  - Also fired during normal recovery after scale-up events (new GPU pool member starts “cold”).
- Diagnosis:
  - This is closer to a *state transition* signal than an “incident” signal.
  - It’s only bad if it coincides with decode stalls or violates compaction budget.
- Change:
  - Downgraded to INFO page only (no pager) + added annotation in dashboard.
  - New: compaction_bytes_moved_rate is tracked for capacity planning / cost of compaction, not paging.

B) ALERT: “Decode p99 regression (hosted)”
- Original trigger: decode_p99_ms increases > 25% vs 24h baseline for 10m (per model)
- What happened:
  - Too sensitive in small cohorts; baseline variance large.
  - Triggered on known periodic bursts + noisy baselines (baseline includes different traffic mix).
- Change:
  - Add gating:
    - Only evaluate if requests_per_min >= 300 for the scoped model+route.
    - Require 20m sustained regression (not 10m).
  - Use dual condition:
    - (p99 absolute > target_ms) AND (p99 relative regression > X%)
  - Rationale:
    - We care about SLO impact (absolute) and regressions (relative), but either alone pages too much.

C) ALERT: “Fragmentation ratio high”
- Original trigger: frag_ratio > 0.35 for 15m
- What happened:
  - Fired in scenarios where compaction is working but frag_ratio is still above threshold while it converges.
  - Also fired after workload mix shifts (more long prompts) where frag_ratio naturally moves.
- Change:
  - New trigger:
    - frag_ratio > 0.40 for 30m AND compaction_step_ms_budget_saturation > 0.8 for 30m
    - AND decode_p99_ms not improving (flat or worse over same window)
  - If frag_ratio > 0.55 for 15m (hard ceiling) => page (but still gated by min traffic).
- Rationale:
  - High fragmentation is only actionable if (a) compaction is constrained (budget saturated) or ineffective and (b) user-facing impact is present.
  - Add “hard ceiling” for safety (prevents creeping toward OOM scenarios).

D) ALERT: “Eviction events high / eviction rate spike”
- Original trigger: eviction_events_rate > 50/min for 10m
- What happened:
  - Fired during legitimate tier-aware eviction when burst traffic arrives and low-priority tiers are preempted.
  - This is expected behavior per design; not always customer-visible.
- Change:
  - Split into two alerts:
    1) Non-paging: eviction_events_rate spike (any tier) -> ticket-level, dashboard annotation.
    2) Paging: high-priority eviction detected (tier=enterprise/dedicated or pinned sessions) OR “evicted_active_decode_sequences > 0”
       - Trigger: >= 1 for 5m (this should be extremely rare).
- Rationale:
  - Page only on correctness / policy violations: evicting sessions we promised not to evict (pinned/high-tier).
  - Otherwise treat as capacity pressure signal for SRE/runtime tuning.

Threshold changes (explicit before/after summary)
1) decode_p99 regression
- Before: +25% vs baseline for 10m
- After: (p99_abs > SLO_target_ms) AND (p99_rel > +30%) for 20m, gated by rpm >= 300

2) frag_ratio high
- Before: frag_ratio > 0.35 for 15m
- After: frag_ratio > 0.40 for 30m + budget saturation > 0.8 + no p99 improvement
- Hard ceiling: frag_ratio > 0.55 for 15m (pager; gated)

3) compaction activity
- Before: bytes moved rate paging
- After: bytes moved rate is INFO-only; pager is tied to compaction_step_ms overruns (see below)

4) compaction budget overrun (new)
- New alert:
  - compaction_step_ms > 8ms p95 for 15m (per node) OR compaction_step_ms > 15ms p95 for 5m
  - Why:
    - This catches scheduler integration regressions (compaction leaking into decode time).
  - Actionability:
    - If triggered alongside p99 decode regression -> candidate rollback / disable compaction.

5) OOM / allocator health (existing but adjusted routing)
- We already had GPU OOM + “KV alloc failures” alerts.
- Change: during canary we route canary-cohort OOM alerts directly to runtime oncall secondary (Rafael + Omar), not just infra oncall.

Rollout automation (rollback) criteria — revised
- Old rollback: any p99 regression alert triggers rollback.
- New rollback policy (for canary cohorts): rollback only if BOTH conditions hold:
  1) decode_p99_ms breach: p99_abs > target_ms for 20m (gated traffic)
  2) compaction-related signal present:
     - compaction_step_ms overrun OR frag_ratio hard ceiling OR eviction of high-priority/pinned sessions OR KV alloc failures
- Notes:
  - This prevented “rollback thrash” during normal traffic bursts.
  - Still retains safety for correctness/allocator failure.

Operational notes / runbook deltas
- When pages fire during canary, first question is cohorting:
  - Is this only canary nodes? Compare control vs canary side-by-side.
- If compaction_step_ms overrun is true:
  - Consider flipping kill-switch (feature flag) on cohort only, not global.
  - Then capture:
    - exemplar traces around compaction spans (PR-19405)
    - allocator snapshot if available
- If frag_ratio hard ceiling is true without p99 impact:
  - Still investigate. This can be “quietly approaching OOM” territory.

Open questions / TODO
- Need better “min traffic” gating per dedicated pool; rpm thresholds differ a lot.
- Add explicit alert on “compaction disabled while frag_ratio high” (config drift / kill-switch stuck).
- Confirm naming consistency with metrics standard doc (Allison to reconcile with perf standards; link TBD).

Appendix: specific changes we pushed (notes)
- Dashboard panels updated to show:
  - decode_p50/p95/p99 + shaded canary window
  - frag_ratio + compaction_bytes_moved_rate + budget saturation overlay
  - eviction_events broken out by tier (hosted-free/hosted-pro/dedicated)
- Alert routing changes:
  - INFO-only alerts now go to #eng-sre (no pager)
  - paging alerts remain in pager rotation but with runtime-oncall as mandatory responder during canary windows

Last updated
- 2026-02-12 (after cohort B re-enable + 48h observation).

- Alert routing changes:
  - INFO-only alerts now go to #eng-sre (no pager)
  - paging alerts remain in pager rotation but with runtime-oncall as mandatory responder during canary windows

Last updated
- 2026-02-12 (after cohort B re-enable + 48h observation).

title: Audit Log Pipeline SLO Proposal (Latency + Drop Rate) — v0.4
preview:
Owner: Sean Gallagher (SRE)
Last reviewed with: Aisha Bello (dashboards/alerts), Kevin Osei (exporter), Logan Wright (coverage telemetry)
Status: draft (targeting “SLO v1” in same release window as schema v1 rollout)

---

1) Purpose
We are standardizing audit log event schema + expanding coverage for sensitive actions across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. This doc proposes:
- SLIs/SLOs for the audit log pipeline (delivery latency + drop rate)
- alert thresholds (paging vs ticket) and burn-rate policies
- an error budget policy appropriate for enterprise security expectations

Scope in this doc: the platform’s ability to accept, durably ingest, and make audit events available for downstream export/consumption.
Out of scope: correctness of event contents/taxonomy (handled by schema + coverage workstreams), and customer SIEM availability/ingestion beyond our exporter boundary.

---

2) Definitions (to reduce ambiguity)
Audit event: a structured record emitted for a sensitive action (RBAC, key lifecycle, routing/policy changes, retention/residency changes, Private install/upgrade actions, etc.).

Pipeline stages (conceptual):
A. Producer emits event (service, console backend, gateway, private installer)
B. Ingestion endpoint accepts event (returns “ack”)
C. Event is durably stored in Redwood audit log store ("durable store")
D. Optional: Exporter delivers to customer-configured destination (Splunk HEC / Sentinel / etc.)

This proposal sets primary SLOs on B→C (platform durability and timeliness). Export is tracked with a separate “export availability/latency” SLI (important, but currently too destination-dependent to be our primary SLO).

Delivery semantics assumption: at-least-once within the platform boundary is acceptable; duplicates may occur (customer de-dupe guided via event_id). Dropped events are not acceptable beyond small error budget.

---

3) Reliability goals and customer expectations
Enterprise/security expectations (from sales/security review calls + support tickets):
- Investigations require reasonably fresh audit events (minutes, not hours)
- Missing events are worse than duplicates
- During partial outages, we should degrade gracefully: sensitive admin actions should not be blocked solely due to audit sink issues, BUT we must provide clear operator signals and recover/backfill where feasible.

We also need to align with internal SOC2/ISO evidence expectations: measurable controls, ongoing monitoring, and documented incident response.

---

4) Proposed SLIs
4.1 Primary SLI: Durable delivery latency (B→C)
Definition: time from ingestion ack timestamp to durable store commit timestamp.
- Measure as distribution; use p50/p95/p99.
- Compute on successfully delivered events only.
- Use ingestion timestamp from the ingestion service (server-side), not producer client time.

Metric sketch (names TBD by observability standards):
- audit_ingest_ack_total{region, env, deployment_mode}
- audit_durable_commit_total{region, env, deployment_mode}
- audit_delivery_latency_seconds_bucket{region, env, deployment_mode}

4.2 Primary SLI: Drop rate (B→C)
Definition: percentage of ingested events that fail to reach durable store within a bounded window.
Practical implementation:
- For each event_id, mark durable commit.
- In a rolling window (e.g., 1h), compute missing commits after a grace period (e.g., 15m) to avoid counting normal backlog.
- Drop = ingested - committed after grace.

Notes:
- Some “missing” may be delayed rather than dropped; we should report both “late” and “dropped” for incident triage.

4.3 Secondary SLI: Ingestion availability (producer→B)
Definition: % of requests to ingestion endpoint that receive ack (2xx) within 1s.
Reason: catches hard outages where producers can’t even send events.

4.4 Secondary SLI: Exporter health (C→D)
Definition: exporter success rate and end-to-end export latency to destination.
- Report separately per export target type (splunk_hec, sentinel, datadog, etc.)
- Not used for “platform audit log SLO v1” paging until we finish destination-specific runbooks and customer configuration validation.

---

5) Proposed SLOs (v1)
SLOs are per region and per environment tier (prod only for commitments). We can also segment by deployment_mode:
- hosted (Redwood-managed)
- dedicated (Redwood-managed)
- private (customer environment; our control varies)

5.1 Platform Durable Delivery Latency SLO (B→C)
Target: 99.9% of audit events durably committed within 5 minutes.
Measurement window: 30 days.
Applies: prod hosted + prod dedicated.

Rationale:
- 5 minutes supports “near real-time” investigations without over-optimizing for tail cases.
- 99.9% aligns with high-reliability expectations without forcing invasive coupling to admin workflows.

Stretch target (not a commitment yet): 99.9% within 2 minutes once buffering/retry improvements and capacity tuning are complete.

5.2 Platform Drop Rate SLO (B→C)
Target: <= 0.01% dropped (i.e., >= 99.99% delivered) over 30 days.
Applies: prod hosted + prod dedicated.

Rationale:
- Dropped audit events are materially worse than latency.
- 0.01% still allows operational reality (rare bugs, catastrophic corruption) but sets a strong bar.

5.3 Ingestion Availability SLO (producer→B)
Target: 99.95% ingestion acks within 1s over 30 days.
Applies: prod hosted + prod dedicated.

Notes:
- This is supportive; the durable delivery SLO is the “real” promise.

5.4 Private deployment (initial posture)
Private has variable infra; we should publish “targets” rather than commitments initially.
Targets:
- Durable delivery latency: 99.5% within 10 minutes (30d) for “recommended sizing” reference deployment.
- Drop rate: <= 0.05% (30d) for “recommended sizing”.

We should be explicit: for Private, Redwood provides tooling/monitoring and best-effort guidance; meeting targets depends on customer-provisioned resources and correct configuration.

---

6) Alerting policy (paging vs ticket)
Goals:
- Page only on conditions likely to burn error budget quickly or indicate active loss of audit events
- Avoid noisy paging from transient backlog that self-heals
- Make alerts actionable and linked to runbook sections

6.1 Paging alerts (prod hosted/dedicated)
A) Drop rate burn alert (high severity)
Condition:
- “Committed-after-grace” rate falls below 99.9% in 5m AND below 99.99% in 30m (multiwindow)
OR
- sustained non-zero “drop_confirmed_total” for >10m with upward slope.
Action: page SRE on-call + notify #eng-security.

B) Backlog/latency burn alert (high severity)
Condition:
- p99 delivery latency > 5 minutes for 15 minutes (and ingest rate steady)
OR
- backlog depth exceeds threshold that predicts SLO miss (queue time estimate > 5m).
Action: page SRE on-call.

C) Ingestion endpoint availability (high severity)
Condition:
- ack success rate < 99% for 5 minutes OR error rate > 1% with 5xx spike.
Action: page SRE on-call.

6.2 Ticket alerts (non-page)
- p95 delivery latency > 2 minutes for 1 hour (performance regression)
- exporter failure rate > 1% for 1 hour per target type (investigate + improve docs/config validation)
- increasing duplicate rate (signal of retry loops) > threshold (needs tuning; see customer ticket about duplicates)

6.3 Suggested burn-rate alert thresholds (initial)
We’ll implement in SLO toolkit as standard multiwindow burn alerts.
For each SLO, define:
- Fast burn: 2% budget consumed in 1 hour (page)
- Slow burn: 5% budget consumed in 6 hours (page)
- Very slow burn: 10% budget consumed in 3 days (ticket)

Tuning notes:
- The above assumes the drop SLO is stringent; we may need to adjust fast/slow windows to avoid paging on tiny absolute counts during low traffic.

---

7) Error budget policy
7.1 Error budget calculation
For each SLO, maintain a rolling 30-day budget.
- Latency SLO budget counts events exceeding 5 minutes as “bad”
- Drop SLO budget counts “drop_confirmed” as “bad”

7.2 Enforcement / release guardrails
If either SLO is in violation (budget exhausted):
- Freeze non-essential changes to audit pipeline components (ingestion, buffering, storage, exporter) until budget is back in compliance OR mitigations are complete.
- Security/compliance work that reduces risk of missing events can proceed with explicit sign-off from SRE + Security Eng (Marcus Lin / delegate).

7.3 Exception process
If we are knowingly operating below SLO for a defined period (e.g., migration, region capacity constraints):
- File an exception (time-bounded) with mitigation plan and customer comms plan where applicable.
- Track in Linear and link from incident record.

---

8) Dashboards and reporting
Minimum dashboards (to be linked from on-call home and runbooks):
- Audit Pipeline Overview (per region): ingest rate, durable commit rate, drop_confirmed, p95/p99 latency, backlog depth
- SLO compliance view: 30d rolling, burn rate, error budget remaining (latency + drops)
- Producer breakdown: top producers by volume and by error/drop correlation (helps identify a single bad producer)
- Exporter view: per target type success rate, retry rate, queue depth, last success timestamp

Reporting cadence:
- Weekly review in #eng-sre (10 min): any slow-burn issues, capacity forecasts, noisy alerts
- Monthly review with Security (Marcus/Kevin): SLO compliance summary + top incidents + action items

---

9) Operational notes / known failure modes
- Backpressure: if ingestion depends on downstream durability synchronously, we risk impacting admin workflows; prefer buffered ingest with durable local queue + async commit where possible.
- Duplicates: at-least-once + retries can create duplicates; ensure event_id is stable and documented for customer de-dupe.
- Regional isolation: failures should not cascade; pipeline should degrade per-region.
- Cardinality: ensure labels/dimensions for metrics do not explode (avoid per-tenant high cardinality in Prometheus labels; use logs/traces for tenant-level drilldown).

---

10) Rollout plan (proposed)
Phase 1 (now):
- Implement SLO definitions in slo-toolkit and initial dashboards in observability-pack.
- Add basic burn alerts for latency + drop.

Phase 2:
- Tune thresholds after 1–2 weeks of production data (address alert noise).
- Validate grace-period logic for drop vs late delivery.

Phase 3:
- Publish customer-facing statement for Hosted/Dedicated (what we measure, what we don’t, dedupe guidance).
- For Private, publish “targets + sizing guidance” and ensure installer enables recommended monitoring.

---

11) Open questions / TODOs
- [TODO] Agree on “durable store” boundary (is it Kafka commit, object store write, or queryable audit store). SLO boundary must match what customers can export/query.
- [TODO] Confirm grace period for drop calculation (15m vs 30m) based on worst-case backlog during region maintenance.
- [TODO] Decide whether to set a single SLO across hosted+dedi or split tiers (enterprise tier tighter).
- [TODO] Finalize metric names and labels with Observability Standards (Allison) to avoid cardinality issues.
- [TODO] Document customer guidance: duplicates and idempotency (link to support FAQ once written).

Appendix A: Quick proposed SLO summary (hosted/dedicated)
- Durable delivery latency: 99.9% <= 5 minutes (30d)
- Drop rate: <= 0.01% dropped (30d)
- Ingestion availability: 99.95% <= 1s ack (30d)

Appendix B: Related incidents/tickets
- Incident thread: brief ingestion backlog (see Slack incidents/1737422288)
- Customer duplicates concern: SUP-18510 (SIEM export retries)

(End)
