title: Shadow Traffic: Failure Mode Review (SRE) — scoring/telemetry/shadow-target degradation
preview:
Context
- Shadow traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow targets” (candidate model/version/quant profile/prompt) while returning the control response to the user.
- Core reliability promise: shadowing MUST NOT impact the user path (latency, availability, correctness of control response). Shadow path is fail-open by design.
- This review focuses on: (1) scoring pipeline down / lagging, (2) telemetry ingestion lag/drops, (3) shadow target errors/overload/timeouts, plus cross-cutting risks (cardinality, quotas, backpressure).

Definitions (for shared language)
- Control path: the request that serves the user response.
- Shadow mirror: async/in-parallel invocation to shadow target(s) with strict timeout budget and isolation.
- Shadow artifact: request/response pair metadata emitted to telemetry, plus optional redacted prompt/response payload refs.
- Scoring: async evaluation of shadow output (rubrics/heuristics/model-graded), producing quality metrics.

Assumptions / non-negotiables
1) User-path SLOs are never subordinated to shadow-path success.
2) Mirror calls must be bounded (timeouts, concurrency caps) and never block returning control response.
3) Telemetry/scoring are “best effort” but must be observable; silent failure is unacceptable beyond a short window.
4) Defaults must be safe for Dedicated and Hosted; Private may disable/redirect components but must preserve fail-open.

System sketch (mental model)
A) Data plane/router
- Determines whether to mirror based on policy sampling + deterministic bucketing.
- Sends control request to control target (existing behavior).
- Sends shadow request(s) in parallel with:
  - Strict per-shadow timeout
  - Resource caps
  - Circuit-breaker if shadow subsystem unhealthy
- Emits events (shadow_attempt, shadow_result, control_result correlation fields).

B) Telemetry ingestion
- Receives shadow pair events.
- Enforces cardinality limits and drops/aggregates if needed.
- Writes to short-retention storage for join between control+shadow, and downstream scoring queue pointer.

C) Scoring subsystem
- Reads queued artifacts, runs rubric set, writes scored metrics.
- Produces aggregates (deltas, CI bands) for Console; triggers regression alerts.

Primary failure domains
1) Shadow target(s) degraded (errors, timeouts, rate limiting, cost spikes)
2) Telemetry ingestion degraded (lag/backlog/drops; storage pressure)
3) Scoring pipeline degraded (queue backlog; worker failures; upstream model-graded evaluator outage)
4) Observability degraded (dashboards/alerts not firing; gaps appear as “looks good”)
5) Policy/config issues (misconfiguration increasing overhead, cardinality, or turning on shadowing too aggressively)


Failure mode catalog

FM-01: Shadow target returns errors (5xx/429) or timeouts
What happens
- Control path returns normally.
- Mirror attempt fails; we record shadow_outcome=error/timeout and do NOT retry inline.
- If errors sustained, circuit breaker opens to reduce/stop mirror attempts for that target.

User impact
- None directly (by design).
- Indirect risks: increased CPU/IO in router due to failing mirrors, potential tail latency creep if not isolated correctly.

Primary risks
- Router thread pool saturation if shadow attempts aren’t strictly bounded.
- Quota/rate-limit blowback if shadow uses shared quotas or shared downstream dependencies.
- “False” quality regressions if candidate is broken (this is still useful, but must be labeled “candidate unhealthy”).

Mitigations (required)
- Per-shadow timeout budget (hard cap). Target: <= 250ms or <= 25% of control timeout, whichever smaller (tunable per deployment).
- Async fire-and-forget semantics after control response is available; do not wait on shadow completion.
- Dedicated concurrency pool for shadow calls (separate from control execution resources).
- Circuit breaker conditions (proposed):
  - Open if shadow_target_error_rate (5xx + timeouts) > 20% for 3 consecutive 1m windows AND >= N attempts (N=200) within that window.
  - Half-open after 5m cooldown; allow 1% probe traffic.
- Quota isolation: shadow requests must be charged against a distinct “shadow budget” with hard caps to avoid starving control.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Dashboard: shadow_target_error_rate by (route_id, shadow_target_id) with cardinality-safe bucketing.
- Page: shadow_target_error_rate > 30% for 10m AND shadow_attempts_per_min > 500 (signals real usage).
- Ticket: shadow_target_error_rate > 10% for 30m (for less urgent degradation).

Owner(s)
- Routing: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
- Guardrails/quotas: Cole Summers


FM-02: Shadow mirroring increases control path p99 latency (regression)
What happens
- Even if mirror calls are async, additional work (fingerprinting, event emission, buffering) can push router CPU/memory and increase p99.

User impact
- Direct user-facing latency regression; unacceptable.

Mitigations (required)
- Fingerprinting must be O(1) / bounded (no heavy serialization). Cache fingerprints where safe.
- Telemetry emission must be non-blocking with bounded buffering; drop on pressure.
- Router must enforce a “shadow overhead budget” (SRE requirement):
  - p99 control latency delta attributable to shadowing <= +1% (Hosted/Dedicated) in steady state.
  - CPU overhead in router <= +5% at same QPS baseline.
- Kill switch at runtime:
  - Global: disable all shadowing for tenant/region
  - Per-route: disable shadow policy
  - Per-target: disable specific shadow target

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: control_path_p99_latency increase > 5% vs 1h baseline AND shadow_policy_enabled=true (guarded to avoid noisy global events).
- Dashboard: control p50/p99 vs shadowing on/off (feature flag or canary group).

Owner(s)
- SRE: Sean Gallagher
- Routing/data plane: Vikram Singh


FM-03: Telemetry ingestion lag (pipeline backlog)
What happens
- Router emits shadow events, but ingestion falls behind.
- Score pipeline sees fewer artifacts; Console shows partial or stale results.

User impact
- No user-response impact.
- Product impact: decision-making impaired; risk of shipping regressions because “no data” can look like “no regression” if UI/alerts aren’t explicit.

Mitigations
- Emit “ingestion_lag_seconds” metric from ingestion service (or derived from event timestamps).
- Backpressure + drop strategy:
  - If ingestion_lag > threshold, start sampling down shadow events further or drop optional payload refs.
  - Prefer to keep minimal counters (attempt/success/error) over full artifact payloads.
- Console must display “data freshness” and “coverage” (percentage of expected scored artifacts).

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: ingestion_lag_seconds p95 > 120s for 10m.
- Ticket: ingestion_lag_seconds p95 > 30s for 30m.
- Page: shadow_events_drop_rate > 5% for 10m (if drop is unexpected / not configured).

Owner(s)
- Telemetry: Nadia Rahman / Caleb Johnson


FM-04: Telemetry drops due to cardinality explosion
What happens
- Unbounded dimensions (prompt_id, user_id, raw route names, arbitrary tags) cause metrics backend strain.
- Ingestion may throttle, drop, or degrade other telemetry.

User impact
- Potential broader observability degradation (worst case), plus missing shadow metrics.

Mitigations (required)
- Hard allowlist of dimensions; reject policy configs that request unsafe labels.
- Enforce hashing/bucketing for identifiers:
  - route_id numeric internal
  - shadow_target_id limited set
  - customer_id optionally bucketed (or omitted in multi-tenant views)
- Rate limit example-level retrieval; do not index on high-cardinality keys.
- “Cardinality guardrail” alarms owned by SRE/Obs.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: metrics_backend_cardinality_alert OR ingestion_rejects_due_to_cardinality > 0 sustained 5m.
- Ticket: topN dimension keys approaching thresholds.

Owner(s)
- Observability: Allison Grant


FM-05: Scoring queue backlog (workers can’t keep up)
What happens
- Artifacts arrive faster than scoring workers process.
- Score lag grows; Console deltas become stale.

User impact
- No direct user impact.
- Product impact: delayed regression detection; “auto rollback” (if enabled later) could be unsafe due to stale data.

Mitigations
- Backpressure knobs:
  - Adaptive sampling: if queue depth > X, reduce sampling % (shadow mirror stays same, but scoring input sampled) OR pause scoring of low-priority routes.
  - Priority queues: canary/rollout-attached experiments > ad-hoc experiments.
- Autoscaling with max spend cap.
- Deduping: if repeated requests identical fingerprint within short window, score once and count occurrences (careful: only for heuristic metrics; model-graded may still need sampling).

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: scoring_queue_age_seconds p95 > 15m for 15m.
- Ticket: scoring_queue_age_seconds p95 > 5m for 30m.
- Page: scoring_worker_error_rate > 5% for 10m.

Owner(s)
- Applied ML / scoring infra: Jonas Weber / Karthik Iyer


FM-06: Scoring subsystem down (complete outage)
What happens
- No new quality metrics computed.
- Telemetry may still collect artifacts; backlog increases.

User impact
- No direct user impact.
- Operational risk: teams might assume “no regression” if UI defaults are unclear.

Mitigations
- UI explicitly shows “scoring unavailable” and disables regression conclusions.
- Automatically pause “quality regression alerts” based on stale data, and instead alert on “scoring down.”
- Optional: if scoring down > 24h, auto-expire / pause experiments to avoid unlimited artifact retention or compute surge on recovery.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: scoring_success_rate < 1% for 10m AND artifacts_enqueued > 1000 in same window.
- Page: no_scored_points_emitted for 15m while shadowing_enabled=true (deadman switch).

Owner(s)
- Applied ML / scoring infra: Jonas Weber
- SRE: Sean Gallagher


FM-07: “Shadowing succeeded” but join between control and shadow is missing
What happens
- Control_result and shadow_result cannot be correlated due to missing fingerprint/correlation IDs or partial event loss.
- Metrics become biased (e.g., only successful joins).

User impact
- No user impact.
- Quality signal integrity issue.

Mitigations
- Correlation fields must be generated in router and included in all events; event schema versioned.
- In ingestion, enforce joinable invariants and emit join_failure metrics.
- Show “join coverage %” in Console.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Ticket: join_coverage < 95% for 1h.
- Page: join_coverage < 80% for 15m on any route with > 1k attempts.

Owner(s)
- Telemetry + routing: Nadia Rahman / Ravi Menon


FM-08: Circuit breaker misfires (too aggressive) causing silent disabling of shadowing
What happens
- Shadowing stops due to CB opening on transient errors.
- Teams lose coverage without realizing.

User impact
- No direct user impact.

Mitigations
- CB open/close events must be logged and surfaced:
  - Audit-ish operational log (not customer audit) + dashboard panel.
  - Console banner: “Shadowing paused due to target health.”
- CB should consider minimum request volume before opening.
- Half-open probes must be small and bounded.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Ticket: cb_open_events > 0 for any route/target AND persists > 30m.
- Page: cb_open_events > 0 AND shadow_coverage drops > 50% for 10m during an active rollout.

Owner(s)
- Routing: Mina Haddad
- SRE: Sean Gallagher


FM-09: Shadow traffic inflates customer bill / surprises customers
What happens
- Shadow requests may consume GPU/compute; if billed, customer surprise; if not billed, Redwood cost blowout.

User impact
- Customer trust issue; escalations.

Mitigations
- Explicit “shadow budget” and sampling guardrails in policy validation.
- Console cost estimate prior to enabling: “at current QPS, 5% sampling ~ X shadow RPM, ~Y tokens/min.”
- Default off for new tenants; beta gating.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Ticket: shadow_compute_cost > budget by > 20% for 24h.
- Page: sudden jump > 2x shadow_cost_minute_over_minute for 15m (potential runaway config).

Owner(s)
- Product/FinOps: (loop in Marissa Cole + platform finance contact)
- SRE: Sean Gallagher


FM-10: Data handling regression (PII leakage into telemetry/scoring)
What happens
- Prompts/contexts may include sensitive data.
- If redaction fails or retention too long, compliance risk.

User impact
- High severity security/compliance exposure.

Mitigations (required)
- Default data minimization: store hashes/derived metrics; only store content for short retention and only if explicitly enabled.
- Redaction pipeline must run before persistence; drop fields on failure (fail-closed for payload storage; fail-open for shadowing itself).
- Access control for example drilldowns (RBAC + audit logs).

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: redaction_failure_rate > 0.5% for 10m (payload write path).
- Page: any policy enabling raw payload retention without required approval flag (config validation).

Owner(s)
- Security: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei
- Telemetry: Nadia Rahman


Operational guardrails (recommended defaults for beta)
- Sampling default: 1% (beta), max 5% without SRE approval.
- Max shadow targets per route: 2 for beta.
- Timeout per shadow attempt: 250ms default, hard max 500ms.
- Shadow concurrency pool: capped per instance; hard ceiling to prevent CPU starvation.
- Storage retention: short by default (e.g., 72h artifacts, 14d aggregates), subject to security policy.


Alert routing proposal (page vs ticket)
P0 Page (immediate)
- Any evidence shadowing impacts control path latency/availability (FM-02).
- Telemetry/cardinality issues that threaten core metrics stack health (FM-04).
- Data handling/redaction failures above threshold (FM-10).

P1 Page (fast response)
- Scoring deadman switch when shadowing enabled (FM-06).
- Ingestion lag beyond 2m sustained (FM-03) during active rollout.

Ticket / async
- Scoring backlog moderate (FM-05) not impacting rollouts.
- Join coverage degradation (FM-07) unless severe.
- Circuit breaker open events (FM-08) unless it blocks a rollout decision.


Notes from dogfood incident (reference: Slack:incidents/1768180020)
- Observed: scoring queue age hit ~45m during peak; workers throttled due to downstream evaluator rate limits.
- Fixes already in flight:
  - Backpressure tuning + evaluator-client retry jitter (ENG-5415)
  - Priority queue for rollout-attached experiments (add subtask under ENG-5405)
- SRE ask: add deadman and “data freshness” banner before expanding beta.


Open questions
1) Should router still emit minimal counters when telemetry ingestion is degraded, or drop everything? (SRE preference: keep minimal counters; drop payload refs first.)
2) How do we represent “candidate unhealthy” vs “candidate lower quality” in Console? (Need clear labeling to avoid misinterpretation.)
3) For Private deployments with no external scoring, what’s the minimum supported “quality signal” story? (At least: shadow attempt/success/error + optional export.)
4) Do we allow “auto rollback on quality regression” at launch? (SRE preference: advisory-only until scoring freshness + coverage + false-positive rate are characterized.)


Action items / follow-ups
- [Routing] Confirm shadow call isolation: dedicated pool + strict timeout + CB instrumentation (PR-24833/PR-24895).
- [Obs] Implement ingestion lag + join coverage metrics, plus dashboards and alert rules (tie to PR-24872 / observability-pack PR-391).
- [Scoring] Add deadman metric and queue-age alerts; implement priority queue (ENG-5405).
- [Console] Add explicit banners: scoring unavailable, stale data, coverage % (coordinate with Dylan Brooks).
- [Security] Validate redaction failure modes and ensure payload storage fails closed; add alert on redaction failures (ENG-5411).

SRE sign-off criteria for limited beta (Dedicated)
- Demonstrated no control-path SLO regression in perf canary / load test.
- Kill switches tested (global + per-route + per-target) and documented.
- Alerting in place: control-latency regression, ingestion lag, scoring deadman, redaction failures, cardinality guardrails.
- Console communicates freshness/coverage clearly; no “green” state when data missing.

Appendix: quick “failure mode to metric” map
- Shadow target unhealthy → shadow_target_error_rate, cb_open_events
- Telemetry lag → ingestion_lag_seconds, shadow_events_drop_rate
- Scoring lag/outage → scoring_queue_age_seconds, scored_points_emitted (deadman)
- Data integrity → join_coverage
- User impact risk → control_latency_delta_when_shadow_enabled
text:
Context
- Shadow traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow targets” (candidate model/version/quant profile/prompt) while returning the control response to the user.
- Core reliability promise: shadowing MUST NOT impact the user path (latency, availability, correctness of control response). Shadow path is fail-open by design.
- This review focuses on: (1) scoring pipeline down / lagging, (2) telemetry ingestion lag/drops, (3) shadow target errors/overload/timeouts, plus cross-cutting risks (cardinality, quotas, backpressure).

Definitions (for shared language)
- Control path: the request that serves the user response.
- Shadow mirror: async/in-parallel invocation to shadow target(s) with strict timeout budget and isolation.
- Shadow artifact: request/response pair metadata emitted to telemetry, plus optional redacted prompt/response payload refs.
- Scoring: async evaluation of shadow output (rubrics/heuristics/model-graded), producing quality metrics.

Assumptions / non-negotiables
1) User-path SLOs are never subordinated to shadow-path success.
2) Mirror calls must be bounded (timeouts, concurrency caps) and never block returning control response.
3) Telemetry/scoring are “best effort” but must be observable; silent failure is unacceptable beyond a short window.
4) Defaults must be safe for Dedicated and Hosted; Private may disable/redirect components but must preserve fail-open.

System sketch (mental model)
A) Data plane/router
- Determines whether to mirror based on policy sampling + deterministic bucketing.
- Sends control request to control target (existing behavior).
- Sends shadow request(s) in parallel with:
  - Strict per-shadow timeout
  - Resource caps
  - Circuit-breaker if shadow subsystem unhealthy
- Emits events (shadow_attempt, shadow_result, control_result correlation fields).

B) Telemetry ingestion
- Receives shadow pair events.
- Enforces cardinality limits and drops/aggregates if needed.
- Writes to short-retention storage for join between control+shadow, and downstream scoring queue pointer.

C) Scoring subsystem
- Reads queued artifacts, runs rubric set, writes scored metrics.
- Produces aggregates (deltas, CI bands) for Console; triggers regression alerts.

Primary failure domains
1) Shadow target(s) degraded (errors, timeouts, rate limiting, cost spikes)
2) Telemetry ingestion degraded (lag/backlog/drops; storage pressure)
3) Scoring pipeline degraded (queue backlog; worker failures; upstream model-graded evaluator outage)
4) Observability degraded (dashboards/alerts not firing; gaps appear as “looks good”)
5) Policy/config issues (misconfiguration increasing overhead, cardinality, or turning on shadowing too aggressively)


Failure mode catalog

FM-01: Shadow target returns errors (5xx/429) or timeouts
What happens
- Control path returns normally.
- Mirror attempt fails; we record shadow_outcome=error/timeout and do NOT retry inline.
- If errors sustained, circuit breaker opens to reduce/stop mirror attempts for that target.

User impact
- None directly (by design).
- Indirect risks: increased CPU/IO in router due to failing mirrors, potential tail latency creep if not isolated correctly.

Primary risks
- Router thread pool saturation if shadow attempts aren’t strictly bounded.
- Quota/rate-limit blowback if shadow uses shared quotas or shared downstream dependencies.
- “False” quality regressions if candidate is broken (this is still useful, but must be labeled “candidate unhealthy”).

Mitigations (required)
- Per-shadow timeout budget (hard cap). Target: <= 250ms or <= 25% of control timeout, whichever smaller (tunable per deployment).
- Async fire-and-forget semantics after control response is available; do not wait on shadow completion.
- Dedicated concurrency pool for shadow calls (separate from control execution resources).
- Circuit breaker conditions (proposed):
  - Open if shadow_target_error_rate (5xx + timeouts) > 20% for 3 consecutive 1m windows AND >= N attempts (N=200) within that window.
  - Half-open after 5m cooldown; allow 1% probe traffic.
- Quota isolation: shadow requests must be charged against a distinct “shadow budget” with hard caps to avoid starving control.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Dashboard: shadow_target_error_rate by (route_id, shadow_target_id) with cardinality-safe bucketing.
- Page: shadow_target_error_rate > 30% for 10m AND shadow_attempts_per_min > 500 (signals real usage).
- Ticket: shadow_target_error_rate > 10% for 30m (for less urgent degradation).

Owner(s)
- Routing: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
- Guardrails/quotas: Cole Summers


FM-02: Shadow mirroring increases control path p99 latency (regression)
What happens
- Even if mirror calls are async, additional work (fingerprinting, event emission, buffering) can push router CPU/memory and increase p99.

User impact
- Direct user-facing latency regression; unacceptable.

Mitigations (required)
- Fingerprinting must be O(1) / bounded (no heavy serialization). Cache fingerprints where safe.
- Telemetry emission must be non-blocking with bounded buffering; drop on pressure.
- Router must enforce a “shadow overhead budget” (SRE requirement):
  - p99 control latency delta attributable to shadowing <= +1% (Hosted/Dedicated) in steady state.
  - CPU overhead in router <= +5% at same QPS baseline.
- Kill switch at runtime:
  - Global: disable all shadowing for tenant/region
  - Per-route: disable shadow policy
  - Per-target: disable specific shadow target

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: control_path_p99_latency increase > 5% vs 1h baseline AND shadow_policy_enabled=true (guarded to avoid noisy global events).
- Dashboard: control p50/p99 vs shadowing on/off (feature flag or canary group).

Owner(s)
- SRE: Sean Gallagher
- Routing/data plane: Vikram Singh


FM-03: Telemetry ingestion lag (pipeline backlog)
What happens
- Router emits shadow events, but ingestion falls behind.
- Score pipeline sees fewer artifacts; Console shows partial or stale results.

User impact
- No user-response impact.
- Product impact: decision-making impaired; risk of shipping regressions because “no data” can look like “no regression” if UI/alerts aren’t explicit.

Mitigations
- Emit “ingestion_lag_seconds” metric from ingestion service (or derived from event timestamps).
- Backpressure + drop strategy:
  - If ingestion_lag > threshold, start sampling down shadow events further or drop optional payload refs.
  - Prefer to keep minimal counters (attempt/success/error) over full artifact payloads.
- Console must display “data freshness” and “coverage” (percentage of expected scored artifacts).

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: ingestion_lag_seconds p95 > 120s for 10m.
- Ticket: ingestion_lag_seconds p95 > 30s for 30m.
- Page: shadow_events_drop_rate > 5% for 10m (if drop is unexpected / not configured).

Owner(s)
- Telemetry: Nadia Rahman / Caleb Johnson

What happens
- Unbounded dimensions (prompt_id, user_id, raw route names, arbitrary tags) cause metrics backend strain.

- Rate limit example-level retrieval; do not index on high-cardinality keys.
- “Cardinality guardrail” alarms owned by SRE/Obs.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: metrics_backend_cardinality_alert OR ingestion_rejects_due_to_cardinality > 0 sustained 5m.
- Ticket: topN dimension keys approaching thresholds.

Owner(s)
- Observability: Allison Grant

- Score lag grows; Console deltas become stale.

- Autoscaling with max spend cap.

Detection/alerting proposals

- Ticket: scoring_queue_age_seconds p95 > 5m for 30m.
- Page: scoring_worker_error_rate > 5% for 10m.

Owner(s)
- Applied ML / scoring infra: Jonas Weber / Karthik Iyer


FM-06: Scoring subsystem down (complete outage)
What happens
- No new quality metrics computed.
- Telemetry may still collect artifacts; backlog increases.

User impact
- No direct user impact.
- Operational risk: teams might assume “no regression” if UI defaults are unclear.

Mitigations
- UI explicitly shows “scoring unavailable” and disables regression conclusions.
- Automatically pause “quality regression alerts” based on stale data, and instead alert on “scoring down.”
- Optional: if scoring down > 24h, auto-expire / pause experiments to avoid unlimited artifact retention or compute surge on recovery.

Detection/alerting proposals
- Page: scoring_success_rate < 1% for 10m AND artifacts_enqueued > 1000 in same window.
- Page: no_scored_points_emitted for 15m while shadowing_enabled=true (deadman switch).

Owner(s)
- Applied ML / scoring infra: Jonas Weber
- SRE: Sean Gallagher


FM-07: “Shadowing succeeded” but join between control and shadow is missing
What happens
- Control_result and shadow_result cannot be correlated due to missing fingerprint/correlation IDs or partial event loss.
- Metrics become biased (e.g., only successful joins).

- In ingestion, enforce joinable invariants and emit join_failure metrics.

title: Rollback Gameday Plan — Optimization Config Safe Apply + One-Click Rollback
preview:
Doc intent
- This is the run-of-show for a 90-minute gameday drill validating: detection, pause, rollback, and verification for a bad Optimization Config rollout (batching/caching/quant profiles).
- This doc is written for SRE/on-call + platform/runtime participants. If something is unclear, update this doc during/after.

Why we’re doing this
- We’ve had real incidents/regressions from optimization changes (mis-scoped applies, latency spikes, partial rollout states). Gameday should prove we can:
  - Detect regressions quickly via the Rollout Health dashboard + alerts
  - Use the Safe Apply gates appropriately (pause/resume/abort)
  - Execute one-click rollback (Console + API) and return to baseline safely
  - Confirm audit events + tags are emitted (tenant/model/region/config_version)
  - Measure time-to-mitigate (TTM) and find friction points

Success criteria (what “good” looks like)
- Detection:
  - Regression is detectable within 5 minutes via dashboard/alerts (p95 latency, 5xx, saturation).
- Control:
  - Rollout can be paused in < 2 minutes once detected.
  - Rollback can be initiated in < 3 minutes after decision.
- Recovery:
  - p95 latency returns to within +5% of baseline within 10 minutes of rollback initiation.
  - 5xx rate returns to baseline within 5 minutes.
- Correctness:
  - Active Optimization Config version shows as previous known-good across the scoped target (no “mixed worker” state beyond allowed propagation window).
  - Audit log events present for: APPLY_STARTED, APPLY_PAUSED (if used), ROLLBACK_REQUESTED, ROLLBACK_COMPLETED.
  - Runtime/worker metrics tagged with config_version before and after.
- Process:
  - Comms and roles were clear (IC, operator, scribe).
  - A short retrospective + action items created same day.

Non-goals / safety
- Do NOT run this in true production for external customers.
- Drill should happen in staging or internal beta environment with synthetic load.
- No deliberate GPU exhaustion beyond agreed safe limits.
- We will not attempt “auto-rollback without confirmation” in this gameday.

Scope of drill (what we are testing)
- Hosted (internal beta environment) + Dedicated-like staging pool
- One model family in one region (keep blast radius contained)
- One Optimization Config version change applied via canary controller
- Rollback via Console button (primary) and API endpoint (backup)

Pre-req checklist (48h before)
1) Environment
- Staging env w/ a representative serving cluster and synthetic load generator
- Confirm which tenant + model + region scope we’re using:
  - Tenant: sre-gameday (internal)
  - Model: rw-llama-70b-instruct (example)
  - Region: us-west-2 (example)
- Confirm we can generate stable baseline traffic for 15+ minutes.

2) Feature flags + permissions
- Ensure safe apply / rollback flags enabled for the env.
- Ensure the “optimization change operator” RBAC role assigned to the operator(s).
- Confirm at least one participant has read access to audit logs and dashboard edit rights (for annotations if needed).

3) Dashboards/alerts ready
- Rollout Health dashboard must have panels (minimum):
  - Request rate (RPS), token throughput
  - p50/p95/p99 latency (overall + per-route if available)
  - 5xx rate + error budget burn proxy
  - GPU utilization + queue time / saturation proxy
  - Canary controller state (progress % / phase) and gate evaluation results
  - Annotations for config apply/promote/rollback events
- Alerts should be configured (paging disabled for gameday; use a test channel) on:
  - p95 latency > baseline * 1.20 for 5 min
  - 5xx rate > baseline + 0.5% for 2 min

4) Known-good config version
- Identify the baseline version “V_good” for the scope and record it here:
  - V_good: ocfg_2026-01-22_18-03Z_v17
- Confirm the baseline is active + stable for at least 15 minutes before drill.

5) “Bad” config version prepared (do not activate yet)
- Prepare “V_bad” version that is semantically valid (passes schema) but causes performance regression.
- Examples of “bad” changes (pick one):
  A) Batching: increase max batch tokens too aggressively (induces queueing, p95/p99 spikes)
  B) Caching: disable prefix/KV caching for long prompts (cost + latency increase)
  C) Mis-scope: apply to region includes unintended shard (ONLY if gating + rollback verified safe)
- Record chosen bad change and expected signal:
  - V_bad: ocfg_2026-01-27_20-15Z_v18
  - Chosen fault: Batching max_queue_delay_ms increased to 75ms + max_batch_tokens 2x (expect p95 up, queue time up)

6) Observability tags validation
- Confirm metrics emitted with: tenant, model, region, config_version
- Confirm the response header (if enabled) surfaces config version for sampled requests.

Roles (assign at start of gameday)
- Incident Commander (IC): runs the clock, calls decisions (pause/rollback), ensures safety
- Operator: executes apply/pause/rollback actions in Console/API; narrates actions
- Scribe: timestamps events + copies links to dashboard screenshots/graphs
- Runtime/Platform observers: watch for worker state propagation issues and log anomalies
- Applied ML observer (optional): if drill includes quantization gating; otherwise FYI only

Channels + comms
- Primary: #incidents (test thread) or #eng-sre (as decided)
- Secondary: Zoom huddle
- Use a single thread for timestamps and links. Scribe posts time markers.

Run-of-show (90 min)

T-15 min: Setup & baseline
- Confirm baseline traffic running
- Record baseline metrics:
  - p95 latency: ____ ms
  - p99 latency: ____ ms
  - 5xx: ____ %
  - GPU util: ____ %
- Confirm active config version = V_good in Console and/or API.

T0: Start drill
- IC: “Start gameday. This is a drill.”
- Scribe: Create timeline with T0.

Phase 1 — Introduce regression via safe apply canary (10–15 min)
1) Operator: initiate safe apply for V_bad with canary
- Scope: tenant=sre-gameday, model=rw-llama-70b-instruct, region=us-west-2
- Canary: 10% for 5 minutes (or 1 shard), then eligible to ramp to 50% if gates pass
- Ensure “gate on latency + 5xx” enabled.

2) Observers: watch canary state
- Confirm canary controller shows phase=CANARY, % traffic = 10
- Confirm gate evaluation is running and referencing the correct metrics tags.

Expected outcome
- Regression shows up quickly in canary slice: p95 up, queue/saturation up.
- Gate should fail OR at least raise warnings depending on threshold configuration.

Phase 2 — Detection + decision (5–10 min)
3) Detection
- Scribe timestamps when first clear signal is visible.
- Call out which signal triggered:
  - p95 latency (canary slice) breach
  - 5xx increase
  - saturation/queue time

4) Decision point: pause vs immediate rollback
- If canary is still at 10% and gates already halted progression: pause rollout (if not already) + proceed to rollback drill anyway.
- If canary progressed further than expected: call immediate rollback; do not attempt further ramp.

Phase 3 — Execute rollback (10–15 min)
5) Operator: execute rollback in Console (primary path)
- Navigate: Optimize → Optimization Configs → History → Select V_good → “Rollback to this version”
- Confirm the modal includes:
  - blast radius (tenant/model/region)
  - current version and target rollback version
  - acknowledgement/type-to-confirm behavior (if enabled)
- Submit rollback

6) Operator: capture rollback request ID + any audit/log correlation IDs
- Post in thread: rollback_request_id, config_version target, and timestamps

7) If Console fails: execute rollback via API (backup)
- Use internal CLI/curl (as per runbook) with same scope
- Confirm idempotency: repeated call should be safe and return “already rolling back / already at target”

Expected outcome
- Canary controller transitions to ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS
- Runtime workers converge back to V_good without mixed-state lasting beyond propagation window

Phase 4 — Verification (15–20 min)
8) Verify system recovery
- Confirm p95/p99 back to baseline range
- Confirm 5xx back to baseline
- Confirm throughput stabilizes

9) Verify config version convergence
- Confirm active version in Console shows V_good
- Confirm metrics tags show config_version=V_good
- If response headers are enabled: sample requests show V_good

10) Verify audit events
- Confirm events exist (names may differ by implementation; record actual):
  - OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_APPLY_STARTED (V_bad)
  - OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_GATE_FAILED and/or APPLY_PAUSED
  - OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_ROLLBACK_REQUESTED (to V_good)
  - OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_ROLLBACK_COMPLETED
- Verify fields include: actor, tenant/model/region scope, from_version, to_version, request_id, timestamp

Phase 5 — Measure & debrief (10–15 min)
11) Capture metrics for the drill
- Time to detect (TTD): T_detect - T0
- Time to decision (TTDec): T_decision - T_detect
- Time to initiate rollback (TTIR): T_rollback_start - T_decision
- Time to mitigate (TTM): T_recovered - T_rollback_start
- Gate behavior: Did it stop progression as expected? Any false positives/negatives?

12) Quick debrief prompts (5 min)
- What was confusing? (UI labels, scoping, confirmation copy)
- Any missing dashboard panels or tags?
- Any unexpected runtime behavior (cache invalidation spike, partial apply weirdness)?
- Did RBAC/audit meet expectations?

Fault injection details (choose ONE)
Option A: Batching regression (recommended)
- Change: increase queue delay / batch size limits
- Signal: canary p95/p99 increases; GPU util may increase; queue time spikes
- Notes: easiest to see and usually safe in staging.

Option B: Caching disabled
- Change: disable prefix/KV caching for the route
- Signal: latency + cost per token; possible throughput drop
- Notes: may require longer prompts to show clearly.

Option C: Quantization profile regression (only if Applied ML available)
- Change: apply a quant profile with known quality or perf regression
- Gates: eval harness quality signals + latency gates
- Signal: quality gate fails (preferred) and rollout pauses before ramp.

Data collection (what scribe should paste into timeline)
- Links:
  - Rollout Health dashboard (with time range pinned)
  - Canary controller view (status page)
  - Console history view showing V_bad apply + rollback to V_good
  - Audit log query results (screenshots/text)
- Numbers:
  - baseline p95/p99, peak p95/p99, post-rollback p95/p99
  - baseline 5xx, peak 5xx, post-rollback 5xx
  - times: T0, T_detect, T_decision, T_rollback_start, T_recovered

Common failure modes to watch for (and what to do)
- Mixed worker states persist > propagation window
  - Action: runtime observer checks apply pipeline logs; operator re-issues rollback (idempotent) and confirm controller state.
- Gate doesn’t trigger despite clear regression
  - Action: pause rollout immediately; file bug against gate thresholds/tags; capture the metric query used by gate.
- Rollback button missing / permission denied
  - Action: validate RBAC role assignment; fall back to API; record exact error for follow-up (ties to RBAC policy bundle).
- Rollback returns success but metrics do not revert
  - Action: verify scope; check for mis-scoped rollback; validate that traffic is actually going through targeted deployment.

Post-gameday follow-ups (owner + due date)
- SRE (Sean): write 1-page gameday summary + metrics (TTD/TTM) — due next business day
- Observability (Allison): confirm dashboard panels/tags; add missing annotations — due 1 week
- Platform (Ethan/Benji): triage any controller/apply/rollback issues found — due 1 week
- Console (Chloe/Jada): address UX friction (confirmation copy, blast radius visibility) — due 2 weeks
- Security (Kevin/Marcus): confirm audit payload completeness + retention expectations — due 2 weeks

Appendix: quick commands / shortcuts (fill in per environment)
- Dashboard: (link)
- Console: (link)
- API endpoint / CLI: see runbook (Confluence: rollback-optimization-config)
- Test traffic generator: (link/command)

Notes to self / open questions (keep short)
- Do we want the gate to auto-pause on first breach, or require manual pause? Current expectation: gate pauses progression; humans decide rollback.
- Confirm cooldown rules: are we blocking re-apply for X minutes after rollback? If yes, capture behavior.
- Confirm whether rollback also restores per-route overrides (if any) or only the selected config scope.
text:
Doc intent
- This is the run-of-show for a 90-minute gameday drill validating: detection, pause, rollback, and verification for a bad Optimization Config rollout (batching/caching/quant profiles).
- This doc is written for SRE/on-call + platform/runtime participants. If something is unclear, update this doc during/after.

Why we’re doing this
- We’ve had real incidents/regressions from optimization changes (mis-scoped applies, latency spikes, partial rollout states). Gameday should prove we can:
  - Detect regressions quickly via the Rollout Health dashboard + alerts
  - Use the Safe Apply gates appropriately (pause/resume/abort)
  - Execute one-click rollback (Console + API) and return to baseline safely
  - Confirm audit events + tags are emitted (tenant/model/region/config_version)
  - Measure time-to-mitigate (TTM) and find friction points

Success criteria (what “good” looks like)
- Detection:
  - Regression is detectable within 5 minutes via dashboard/alerts (p95 latency, 5xx, saturation).
- Control:
  - Rollout can be paused in < 2 minutes once detected.
  - Rollback can be initiated in < 3 minutes after decision.
- Recovery:
  - p95 latency returns to within +5% of baseline within 10 minutes of rollback initiation.
  - 5xx rate returns to baseline within 5 minutes.
- Correctness:
  - Active Optimization Config version shows as previous known-good across the scoped target (no “mixed worker” state beyond allowed propagation window).
  - Audit log events present for: APPLY_STARTED, APPLY_PAUSED (if used), ROLLBACK_REQUESTED, ROLLBACK_COMPLETED.
  - Runtime/worker metrics tagged with config_version before and after.
- Process:
  - Comms and roles were clear (IC, operator, scribe).
  - A short retrospective + action items created same day.

Non-goals / safety
- Do NOT run this in true production for external customers.
- Drill should happen in staging or internal beta environment with synthetic load.
- No deliberate GPU exhaustion beyond agreed safe limits.
- We will not attempt “auto-rollback without confirmation” in this gameday.

Scope of drill (what we are testing)
- Hosted (internal beta environment) + Dedicated-like staging pool
- One model family in one region (keep blast radius contained)
- One Optimization Config version change applied via canary controller
- Rollback via Console button (primary) and API endpoint (backup)

- Confirm we can generate stable baseline traffic for 15+ minutes.

3) Dashboards/alerts ready

  - Request rate (RPS), token throughput
  - p50/p95/p99 latency (overall + per-route if available)
  - 5xx rate + error budget burn proxy
  - GPU utilization + queue time / saturation proxy
  - Canary controller state (progress % / phase) and gate evaluation results
  - Annotations for config apply/promote/rollback events
- Alerts should be configured (paging disabled for gameday; use a test channel) on:
  - p95 latency > baseline * 1.20 for 5 min
  - 5xx rate > baseline + 0.5% for 2 min

4) Known-good config version
- Identify the baseline version “V_good” for the scope and record it here:
  - V_good: ocfg_2026-01-22_18-03Z_v17
- Confirm the baseline is active + stable for at least 15 minutes before drill.

5) “Bad” config version prepared (do not activate yet)

  A) Batching: increase max batch tokens too aggressively (induces queueing, p95/p99 spikes)
  B) Caching: disable prefix/KV caching for long prompts (cost + latency increase)
  C) Mis-scope: apply to region includes unintended shard (ONLY if gating + rollback verified safe)
- Record chosen bad change and expected signal:
  - V_bad: ocfg_2026-01-27_20-15Z_v18
  - Chosen fault: Batching max_queue_delay_ms increased to 75ms + max_batch_tokens 2x (expect p95 up, queue time up)

6) Observability tags validation

- Confirm the response header (if enabled) surfaces config version for sampled requests.

Roles (assign at start of gameday)

- Use a single thread for timestamps and links. Scribe posts time markers.

Run-of-show (90 min)

  - p95 latency: ____ ms
  - p99 latency: ____ ms
  - 5xx: ____ %
  - GPU util: ____ %

T0: Start drill

Expected outcome
- Canary controller transitions to ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS
- Runtime workers converge back to V_good without mixed-state lasting beyond propagation window

Phase 4 — Verification (15–20 min)
8) Verify system recovery
- Confirm p95/p99 back to baseline range
- Confirm 5xx back to baseline
- Confirm throughput stabilizes

9) Verify config version convergence
- Confirm active version in Console shows V_good
- Confirm metrics tags show config_version=V_good

10) Verify audit events

  - OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_APPLY_STARTED (V_bad)

Phase 5 — Measure & debrief (10–15 min)

title: Benchmark Cluster Capacity Plan (Q1 2026) — Perf Baseline & Representative Workload Suite
preview:
Doc purpose
- Ensure we have enough benchmark fleet capacity (GPU-hours + scheduling windows + isolation) to produce the Q1 2026 “golden baseline” and stand up ongoing perf-canary/gating.
- This is an internal planning sheet; numbers are directional and should be revalidated after the first full sweep (week of Jan 13).

Scope covered by this plan
- Regions: us-east (primary), eu-west (secondary)
- SKUs: H100 80GB, A100 80GB, L40S 48GB (where applicable)
- Workloads (representative suite v1):
  1) Chat streaming (short prompts, variable decode, streaming)
  2) Long-context summarization / prefill stress (large prefill, modest decode)
  3) Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn; modeled as bursty chat with forced pauses)
  4) Embeddings high-QPS (small payload, throughput + tail latency)
- Models: “top deployed” set per baseline plan (Llama-family, Qwen-family, Mistral-family variants). Exact list maintained in the run tracker; planning assumes 8 primary model configs.

Key planning assumptions (please challenge)
1) Repro isolation is a requirement: baseline runs should execute on dedicated nodes (no noisy neighbors) with fixed driver/CUDA stack per run manifest.
2) Sample size: per {model, workload, sku, region, scenario} we want >= 30 iterations of a fixed-seed run OR >= 60 min steady-state window (whichever longer), plus warmup.
3) Two scenarios must be reported for at least chat + long-prefill: “cold” and “warm” (cache aware), per Slack decision (include both so we can attribute changes).
4) Warmup overhead: add +15% GPU-time to any run that requires warmup/clock stabilization.
5) Re-run factor: add +20% GPU-time for expected reruns (variance, failed jobs, triage instrumentation).
6) Fleet availability: we can reliably reserve 6–10 GPUs overnight in us-east, 2–4 in eu-west, assuming we pre-book.

Definitions
- GPU-hour: 1 GPU allocated for 1 hour (exclusive).
- “Run” (planning unit): one harness invocation producing TTFT/TPOT/throughput + artifacts (manifest, logs, optional traces) for a single {model, workload, scenario, sku, region}.
- Scenario:
  - Cold: no prefix/KV cache primed; start from clean runtime state per harness.
  - Warm: cache primed per workload-specific warmup (documented in harness PR-21471).

Baseline matrix (planning)
We will not run an exhaustive full cartesian product across everything on day 1; we’ll stage:
- Stage A (baseline freeze candidate): us-east only; H100 + A100; all 4 workloads; 8 models; cold+warm where applicable.
- Stage B (region comparability): eu-west; H100 only; subset workloads for sanity (chat streaming + long-prefill + embeddings), subset models (top 4).
- Stage C (SKU comparability): L40S; subset models (top 3) + workloads (chat + embeddings) to calibrate expected deltas.

GPU-hour estimate summary (Q1 2026)
(All numbers include warmup (+15%) and rerun buffer (+20%).)

Table 1 — Planned GPU-hours for baseline sweeps (one-time)
SKU    Region   Stage   Models x Workloads x Scenarios (effective)   Base GPU-hrs   With buffers   Notes
H100   us-east  A       8 x 4 x ~1.5                              430            594            Long-prefill runs are longer; ~1.5 scenario avg assumes cold+warm for 2 workloads only
A100   us-east  A       8 x 4 x ~1.5                              520            718            A100 slower -> higher run duration for same statistical confidence
H100   eu-west  B       4 x 3 x ~1.3                              120            166            Mostly comparability checks; fewer reruns expected if stable
L40S   us-east  C       3 x 2 x ~1.2                              90             125            Primarily embeddings + chat; long-prefill not prioritized on L40S

Subtotal one-time baseline sweep GPU-hrs (buffers included): 1,603 GPU-hours

Table 2 — Ongoing capacity for perf-canary + release gating
Cadence   Suite slice                              SKU(s)          Region(s)          GPU-hrs/week   Notes
Nightly   Representative suite (reduced)           H100            us-east            80            10 jobs x ~8 GPU-hrs each; parallelizable; keeps trend line
Nightly   Embeddings high-QPS (throughput focus)   H100 + L40S     us-east            28            Useful for regression detection in batching/caching paths
Weekly    Full representative suite (expanded)     H100 + A100     us-east            120           Used for release signoff + confidence refresh
Weekly    Region comparability spot-check          H100            eu-west            20            Keeps an eye on region-specific fleet/kernel diffs

Ongoing total (steady state): ~248 GPU-hrs/week
Q1 duration planning (10 weeks): 2,480 GPU-hours

Grand total GPU-hours planning for Q1 2026 (baseline + steady-state): 4,083 GPU-hours

Concurrency and scheduling windows
- We should bias heavy/long runs to off-peak to reduce contention and variance (also reduces chance of preemption / noisy neighbor issues).
- Target windows (local time):
  - us-east: 8pm–8am ET (primary), 12pm–4pm ET (secondary, if we can isolate)
  - eu-west: 7pm–7am CET (primary)

Table 3 — Reservation plan (minimum viable)
Week (Q1)          us-east H100 reserved   us-east A100 reserved   us-east L40S reserved   eu-west H100 reserved   Comments
Week of Jan 05     6 GPUs nightly          4 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly           Bring-up + variance calibration
Week of Jan 12     8 GPUs nightly          6 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          3 GPUs nightly           First full sweep attempt
Week of Jan 19     10 GPUs nightly         6 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          3 GPUs nightly           Reruns + triage window
Week of Jan 26     6 GPUs nightly          4 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly           Transition to canary + gating
Feb–Mar steady     6 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs weekly blocks    2 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly           Nightly canary + weekly expanded

Expected contention (what will fight us for GPUs)
- Runtime team ad-hoc profiling runs during regressions (often daytime, often needs H100).
- SRE incident response exercises (if production adjacent issues pop up from perf signatures).
- Other orgs using the same shared GPU pool (internal training/evals spikes, unrelated launch tests).

Mitigation/operational agreements
- Pre-book windows at least 72 hours ahead for Stage A sweeps.
- A “bench fleet” queue with strict isolation labels (no co-scheduling) for baseline-tagged jobs.
- Throttle ad-hoc runs into a separate queue unless tagged as “baseline blocker” by Mateo/Noah/Sean.
- For variance investigations, prefer 1–2 dedicated “debug nodes” rather than disrupting the full sweep.

Cost model (estimates)
Assumed internal chargeback / blended rate (directional; see Neha’s approval thread for finance-confirmed numbers):
- H100: $4.25 / GPU-hour
- A100: $2.40 / GPU-hour
- L40S: $1.20 / GPU-hour
- Add 8% overhead for storage/egress/observability (artifacts, traces)

Table 4 — Q1 2026 cost estimate
Category                         GPU-hrs   Mix assumptions                                 Raw cost    +8% overhead   Notes
One-time baseline sweep           1,603    ~760 H100, ~718 A100, ~125 L40S                 $6,783      $7,326         Stage A/B/C combined
Ongoing canary + gating (10 wks)  2,480    ~1,900 H100, ~400 A100, ~180 L40S               $9,467      $10,224        Nightly+weekly steady-state

Total Q1 2026 estimate: ~$17.6k (including overhead)

Notes on why costs look “low” relative to intuition
- This is incremental compute cost for the benchmark fleet only (not including engineering labor).
- These rates are internal/blended chargeback, not public on-demand cloud list prices.
- If we end up needing more day-time guaranteed capacity or pinned dedicated hosts, the effective rate may increase.

Operational requirements / checklist
- Access / IAM:
  - Ensure benchmark service account can (a) schedule on reserved nodes, (b) write artifacts to bucket, (c) write run metadata to results store.
  - See Slack thread on permissions; track via INT-8821.
- Fleet hygiene:
  - Stable driver/CUDA images per baseline tag; do not auto-upgrade on baseline nodes during sweep weeks.
  - Clock stability: lock GPU clocks where permitted; otherwise standardize persistence mode + warmup.
- Artifact retention:
  - Keep raw artifacts for 30 days; keep summary + manifest indefinitely.
  - Traces: sample 5–10% of runs, with opt-in for regression triage.

Risk register
ID   Risk                                         Likelihood  Impact   Leading indicator                         Mitigation / owner
R1   GPU availability lower than reserved (burst)  Med         High     Jobs queued > 2 hrs during reserved       Pre-book + dedicated queue; escalate to Neha for capacity; owner: Sean
R2   Variance too high -> rerun explosion          Med         High     p95/p99 bands > regression threshold      Standard warmup, isolation, clock locking; tune harness; owner: Sana + Rina
R3   Region drift (eu-west differs materially)     Low/Med     Med      eu-west deltas unexplained               Keep weekly spot-check; ensure images match; owner: Allison
R4   Driver/CUDA mismatch across nodes             Med         High     Manifest shows mixed versions             Freeze images; validate at job start; block run if mismatch; owner: Sahana
R5   Storage/metrics pipeline lag                  Low         Med      Dashboards missing runs > 2 hrs           Backfill job + alerting; owner: Sergio + Logan
R6   Competing “urgent” profiling disrupts plan    Med         Med      Reservation calendar churn                Separate debug nodes; enforce tagging policy; owner: Mateo + Sean
R7   Budget pressure / scope creep                 Low/Med     Med      Extra SKUs/regions requested mid-sweep    Stage gates; require sponsor sign-off for expansions; owner: Talia + Neha

Open questions
1) Do we need a small “always-on” eu-west reservation for reproducibility (vs best-effort weekly blocks)?
2) Should we include a limited on-prem/Private-like environment in Q1, or explicitly defer to Q2 (out of scope currently)?
3) Confirm final internal chargeback rates for Feb–Mar (finance sometimes adjusts blended rates quarterly).
4) Confirm whether L40S coverage is required for baseline “golden” status or only as a trend line.

Action items (next 2 weeks)
- [Sean] Finalize reservation requests for Jan 12–Jan 23 sweep weeks; include contingency night blocks.
- [Sahana] Implement “image/version gate” at job start (fail fast if driver/CUDA mismatch).
- [Rina] Update run tracker with actual observed run durations from first sweep; revise GPU-hour model.
- [Talia] Align freeze date and publish schedule to stakeholders (runtime + SRE + Applied ML).
- [Allison/Sergio] Ensure dashboards link run → manifest → artifacts reliably before baseline freeze.

Changelog
- 2026-01-27: Updated ongoing canary estimate to include embeddings nightly slice; added R4 driver/CUDA mismatch risk.
- 2026-01-15: Added eu-west weekly spot-check block and clarified warm/cold scenario expectations.
- 2025-12-18: Initial draft based on baseline plan + expected model/workload matrix.
text:
Doc purpose
- Ensure we have enough benchmark fleet capacity (GPU-hours + scheduling windows + isolation) to produce the Q1 2026 “golden baseline” and stand up ongoing perf-canary/gating.
- This is an internal planning sheet; numbers are directional and should be revalidated after the first full sweep (week of Jan 13).

Scope covered by this plan
- Regions: us-east (primary), eu-west (secondary)
- SKUs: H100 80GB, A100 80GB, L40S 48GB (where applicable)
- Workloads (representative suite v1):
  1) Chat streaming (short prompts, variable decode, streaming)
  2) Long-context summarization / prefill stress (large prefill, modest decode)
  3) Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn; modeled as bursty chat with forced pauses)
  4) Embeddings high-QPS (small payload, throughput + tail latency)
- Models: “top deployed” set per baseline plan (Llama-family, Qwen-family, Mistral-family variants). Exact list maintained in the run tracker; planning assumes 8 primary model configs.

Key planning assumptions (please challenge)
1) Repro isolation is a requirement: baseline runs should execute on dedicated nodes (no noisy neighbors) with fixed driver/CUDA stack per run manifest.
2) Sample size: per {model, workload, sku, region, scenario} we want >= 30 iterations of a fixed-seed run OR >= 60 min steady-state window (whichever longer), plus warmup.
3) Two scenarios must be reported for at least chat + long-prefill: “cold” and “warm” (cache aware), per Slack decision (include both so we can attribute changes).
4) Warmup overhead: add +15% GPU-time to any run that requires warmup/clock stabilization.
5) Re-run factor: add +20% GPU-time for expected reruns (variance, failed jobs, triage instrumentation).
6) Fleet availability: we can reliably reserve 6–10 GPUs overnight in us-east, 2–4 in eu-west, assuming we pre-book.

Definitions
- GPU-hour: 1 GPU allocated for 1 hour (exclusive).
- “Run” (planning unit): one harness invocation producing TTFT/TPOT/throughput + artifacts (manifest, logs, optional traces) for a single {model, workload, scenario, sku, region}.
- Scenario:
  - Cold: no prefix/KV cache primed; start from clean runtime state per harness.
  - Warm: cache primed per workload-specific warmup (documented in harness PR-21471).

Baseline matrix (planning)
We will not run an exhaustive full cartesian product across everything on day 1; we’ll stage:
- Stage A (baseline freeze candidate): us-east only; H100 + A100; all 4 workloads; 8 models; cold+warm where applicable.
- Stage B (region comparability): eu-west; H100 only; subset workloads for sanity (chat streaming + long-prefill + embeddings), subset models (top 4).
- Stage C (SKU comparability): L40S; subset models (top 3) + workloads (chat + embeddings) to calibrate expected deltas.

GPU-hour estimate summary (Q1 2026)
(All numbers include warmup (+15%) and rerun buffer (+20%).)

Table 1 — Planned GPU-hours for baseline sweeps (one-time)
SKU    Region   Stage   Models x Workloads x Scenarios (effective)   Base GPU-hrs   With buffers   Notes
H100   us-east  A       8 x 4 x ~1.5                              430            594            Long-prefill runs are longer; ~1.5 scenario avg assumes cold+warm for 2 workloads only
A100   us-east  A       8 x 4 x ~1.5                              520            718            A100 slower -> higher run duration for same statistical confidence
H100   eu-west  B       4 x 3 x ~1.3                              120            166            Mostly comparability checks; fewer reruns expected if stable
L40S   us-east  C       3 x 2 x ~1.2                              90             125            Primarily embeddings + chat; long-prefill not prioritized on L40S

Subtotal one-time baseline sweep GPU-hrs (buffers included): 1,603 GPU-hours

Table 2 — Ongoing capacity for perf-canary + release gating
Cadence   Suite slice                              SKU(s)          Region(s)          GPU-hrs/week   Notes
Nightly   Representative suite (reduced)           H100            us-east            80            10 jobs x ~8 GPU-hrs each; parallelizable; keeps trend line
Nightly   Embeddings high-QPS (throughput focus)   H100 + L40S     us-east            28            Useful for regression detection in batching/caching paths
Weekly    Full representative suite (expanded)     H100 + A100     us-east            120           Used for release signoff + confidence refresh
Weekly    Region comparability spot-check          H100            eu-west            20            Keeps an eye on region-specific fleet/kernel diffs

Ongoing total (steady state): ~248 GPU-hrs/week
Q1 duration planning (10 weeks): 2,480 GPU-hours

Grand total GPU-hours planning for Q1 2026 (baseline + steady-state): 4,083 GPU-hours

Concurrency and scheduling windows
- We should bias heavy/long runs to off-peak to reduce contention and variance (also reduces chance of preemption / noisy neighbor issues).
- Target windows (local time):
  - us-east: 8pm–8am ET (primary), 12pm–4pm ET (secondary, if we can isolate)
  - eu-west: 7pm–7am CET (primary)

Table 3 — Reservation plan (minimum viable)
Week (Q1)          us-east H100 reserved   us-east A100 reserved   us-east L40S reserved   eu-west H100 reserved   Comments
Week of Jan 05     6 GPUs nightly          4 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly           Bring-up + variance calibration
Week of Jan 12     8 GPUs nightly          6 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          3 GPUs nightly           First full sweep attempt
Week of Jan 19     10 GPUs nightly         6 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          3 GPUs nightly           Reruns + triage window
Week of Jan 26     6 GPUs nightly          4 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly           Transition to canary + gating
Feb–Mar steady     6 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs weekly blocks    2 GPUs nightly          2 GPUs nightly           Nightly canary + weekly expanded

Expected contention (what will fight us for GPUs)
- Runtime team ad-hoc profiling runs during regressions (often daytime, often needs H100).
- SRE incident response exercises (if production adjacent issues pop up from perf signatures).
- Other orgs using the same shared GPU pool (internal training/evals spikes, unrelated launch tests).

Mitigation/operational agreements
- Pre-book windows at least 72 hours ahead for Stage A sweeps.
- A “bench fleet” queue with strict isolation labels (no co-scheduling) for baseline-tagged jobs.
- Throttle ad-hoc runs into a separate queue unless tagged as “baseline blocker” by Mateo/Noah/Sean.
- For variance investigations, prefer 1–2 dedicated “debug nodes” rather than disrupting the full sweep.

Cost model (estimates)
Assumed internal chargeback / blended rate (directional; see Neha’s approval thread for finance-confirmed numbers):
- H100: $4.25 / GPU-hour
- A100: $2.40 / GPU-hour
- L40S: $1.20 / GPU-hour
- Add 8% overhead for storage/egress/observability (artifacts, traces)

Table 4 — Q1 2026 cost estimate
Category                         GPU-hrs   Mix assumptions                                 Raw cost    +8% overhead   Notes
One-time baseline sweep           1,603    ~760 H100, ~718 A100, ~125 L40S                 $6,783      $7,326         Stage A/B/C combined
Ongoing canary + gating (10 wks)  2,480    ~1,900 H100, ~400 A100, ~180 L40S               $9,467      $10,224        Nightly+weekly steady-state

Total Q1 2026 estimate: ~$17.6k (including overhead)

Notes on why costs look “low” relative to intuition
- This is incremental compute cost for the benchmark fleet only (not including engineering labor).
- These rates are internal/blended chargeback, not public on-demand cloud list prices.
- If we end up needing more day-time guaranteed capacity or pinned dedicated hosts, the effective rate may increase.

Operational requirements / checklist
- Access / IAM:
  - Ensure benchmark service account can (a) schedule on reserved nodes, (b) write artifacts to bucket, (c) write run metadata to results store.
  - See Slack thread on permissions; track via INT-8821.
- Fleet hygiene:
  - Stable driver/CUDA images per baseline tag; do not auto-upgrade on baseline nodes during sweep weeks.
  - Clock stability: lock GPU clocks where permitted; otherwise standardize persistence mode + warmup.
- Artifact retention:
  - Keep raw artifacts for 30 days; keep summary + manifest indefinitely.
  - Traces: sample 5–10% of runs, with opt-in for regression triage.

Risk register
ID   Risk                                         Likelihood  Impact   Leading indicator                         Mitigation / owner
R1   GPU availability lower than reserved (burst)  Med         High     Jobs queued > 2 hrs during reserved       Pre-book + dedicated queue; escalate to Neha for capacity; owner: Sean
R2   Variance too high -> rerun explosion          Med         High     p95/p99 bands > regression threshold      Standard warmup, isolation, clock locking; tune harness; owner: Sana + Rina
R3   Region drift (eu-west differs materially)     Low/Med     Med      eu-west deltas unexplained               Keep weekly spot-check; ensure images match; owner: Allison
R4   Driver/CUDA mismatch across nodes             Med         High     Manifest shows mixed versions             Freeze images; validate at job start; block run if mismatch; owner: Sahana
R5   Storage/metrics pipeline lag                  Low         Med      Dashboards missing runs > 2 hrs           Backfill job + alerting; owner: Sergio + Logan
R6   Competing “urgent” profiling disrupts plan    Med         Med      Reservation calendar churn                Separate debug nodes; enforce tagging policy; owner: Mateo + Sean
R7   Budget pressure / scope creep                 Low/Med     Med      Extra SKUs/regions requested mid-sweep    Stage gates; require sponsor sign-off for expansions; owner: Talia + Neha

Open questions
1) Do we need a small “always-on” eu-west reservation for reproducibility (vs best-effort weekly blocks)?
2) Should we include a limited on-prem/Private-like environment in Q1, or explicitly defer to Q2 (out of scope currently)?
3) Confirm final internal chargeback rates for Feb–Mar (finance sometimes adjusts blended rates quarterly).
4) Confirm whether L40S coverage is required for baseline “golden” status or only as a trend line.

Action items (next 2 weeks)
- [Sean] Finalize reservation requests for Jan 12–Jan 23 sweep weeks; include contingency night blocks.
- [Sahana] Implement “image/version gate” at job start (fail fast if driver/CUDA mismatch).
- [Rina] Update run tracker with actual observed run durations from first sweep; revise GPU-hour model.
- [Talia] Align freeze date and publish schedule to stakeholders (runtime + SRE + Applied ML).
- [Allison/Sergio] Ensure dashboards link run → manifest → artifacts reliably before baseline freeze.

- 2026-01-27: Updated ongoing canary estimate to include embeddings nightly slice; added R4 driver/CUDA mismatch risk.

title: Private observability minimum pack — sizing guidance (Prometheus/Grafana/log collector) — DRAFT
preview:
Audience: customer platform teams + Redwood Support/SRE validating Redwood Private installs (VPC + on-prem).

Status: DRAFT. Numbers below are intentionally conservative with headroom; we should validate against smoke-test harness + 2 reference clusters (VPC + on-prem-ish) before copying into installer defaults.

Scope of this doc
- What we size: Prometheus (TSDB), Alertmanager, Grafana, and the default log collector/forwarder shipped with the Private observability minimum pack.
- What we do NOT size here: the customer’s SIEM/log backend (Splunk/ELK/etc), long-term metrics storage, multi-cluster federation, or “premium” observability.

Assumptions (v1 minimum pack)
- Kubernetes cluster running Redwood Private control plane + data plane (serving runtime pods).
- Prometheus-based metrics collection (Prometheus Operator / kube-prometheus-stack style deployment).
- Grafana dashboards installed via config (dashboards-as-code); Grafana persistence optional.
- Logs collected as structured JSON and forwarded out of cluster (no default durable log store assumed in minimum pack).
- Cardinality is bounded per Private standards (e.g., avoid unbounded model/route/tenant labels; use hashed/limited labels and recording rules).

Quick defaults (recommended starting point)
These are the “safe” defaults we should target in the Helm values + installer for typical Private installs.

DEFAULTS (typical prod-small)
- Prometheus: 2 vCPU, 8–12Gi memory, 100Gi PVC, 7 days retention, 15–30s scrape
- Grafana: 250m–500m CPU, 512Mi–1Gi memory, 1 replica (2 if customer requires HA), persistence optional (5–10Gi if enabled)
- Alertmanager: 100m–200m CPU, 256–512Mi memory, 1–3 replicas depending on HA needs
- Log collector (DaemonSet): 100m CPU / 256Mi memory per node; optional on-disk buffer 5–20Gi per node for disconnected/on-prem environments

When to override defaults
- If Prometheus memory > ~70% steadily OR frequent OOM kills: increase memory first (head series lives in RAM).
- If Prometheus PVC usage growth suggests < 20% free disk at peak: increase retention PVC or reduce retention.
- If customer enables high-frequency scraping (<=15s) + larger GPU fleets: scale Prometheus up (more head series + WAL churn).
- If air-gapped/on-prem with intermittent SIEM connectivity: enable/size log on-disk buffers.

Sizing model (Prometheus)
Prometheus capacity is primarily driven by active series + scrape interval, secondarily by query load (Grafana users, alert rules).

Rule-of-thumb formula (for rough PVC sizing)
- samples/day ≈ active_series * (86400 / scrape_interval_seconds)
- raw_data/day ≈ samples/day * bytes_per_sample
- effective_storage/day ≈ raw_data/day * overhead_factor

Recommended constants (empirical-ish)
- bytes_per_sample: 1.5 bytes (compressed samples only; DOES NOT include index/WAL)
- overhead_factor: 2.0 (index + chunks + WAL + compaction headroom)
- combined: ~3.0 bytes/sample as a planning number

So: storage/day (bytes) ≈ active_series * (86400 / scrape_interval) * 3.0
Convert to GiB: divide by 1024^3

Examples at 15s scrape (5760 samples/series/day)
- storage/day ≈ active_series * 5760 * 3 bytes
  - 100k series => ~1.6 GiB/day (plan 2–3 GiB/day with safety margin)
  - 250k series => ~4.0 GiB/day (plan 6–8 GiB/day)
  - 500k series => ~8.0 GiB/day (plan 12–16 GiB/day)

Important: if label cardinality drifts (e.g., model=unbounded, route=unbounded), active series can grow by 5–20x and these estimates become invalid.

Footprints (pick one) and recommended sizes
We’ve found it’s easier for customers to choose a footprint based on cluster scale / throughput, then tune based on observed series + disk growth.

FOOTPRINT A — Dev / POC
Typical: <= 2 runtime pods, <= 1–2 GPU nodes, low QPS, few dashboards users.
- Expected active series: 30k–80k
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 500m–1 vCPU / 2–4Gi memory
  - PVC: 50Gi
  - Retention: 7d (or 3d if very small disks)
  - Scrape: 30s default (15s only if needed for latency SLO burn alerts)
- Grafana
  - 200m CPU / 512Mi memory
  - PVC: optional; 5Gi if enabled
- Alertmanager
  - 100m CPU / 256Mi memory
- Log collector
  - 50–100m CPU / 128–256Mi memory per node
  - Buffer: memory-only is fine; 5Gi disk buffer if on-prem + unreliable export

FOOTPRINT B — Prod Small (recommended default)
Typical: 4–16 runtime pods, <= 8–16 GPUs total, normal dashboard usage, standard alerting.
- Expected active series: 80k–200k
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 2 vCPU / 8–12Gi memory
  - PVC: 100Gi
  - Retention: 7d
  - Scrape: 15s for runtime + 30s for infra (if split scrape configs are used)
- Grafana
  - 250m–500m CPU / 512Mi–1Gi memory
  - PVC: optional; 5–10Gi if enabled
- Alertmanager
  - 200m CPU / 512Mi memory
- Log collector
  - 100m CPU / 256Mi memory per node
  - Optional on-disk buffer: 10Gi per node (on-prem strongly recommended)

FOOTPRINT C — Prod Medium
Typical: 16–64 runtime pods, 32–64 GPUs, multiple teams using dashboards, heavier alerting/query load.
- Expected active series: 200k–500k
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 4 vCPU / 16–24Gi memory
  - PVC: 300Gi
  - Retention: 14d
  - Scrape: 15s (consider 30s for non-SLO-critical targets)
  - Consider: shard Prometheus or add remote_write if customer wants >14d
- Grafana
  - 500m–1 vCPU / 1–2Gi memory
  - Consider 2 replicas + shared datasource config; persistence optional
- Alertmanager
  - 3 replicas if customer requires HA paging; 200m CPU / 512Mi–1Gi memory
- Log collector
  - 100–200m CPU / 256–512Mi memory per node
  - On-disk buffer: 10–20Gi per node if exports can back up

FOOTPRINT D — Prod Large
Typical: 64+ runtime pods, 64–256 GPUs, high alert/query volume, more complex networking policies.
- Expected active series: 500k–1.2M (ONLY if cardinality controls are followed; otherwise risk is much higher)
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 8 vCPU / 32–48Gi memory
  - PVC: 500Gi–1Ti
  - Retention: 14–30d (30d only with careful series control and disk class)
  - Strongly consider: sharding Prometheus by target group or remote_write to customer’s long-term store
- Grafana
  - 1–2 vCPU / 2–4Gi memory, 2 replicas
- Alertmanager
  - 3 replicas; integrate with customer paging policy
- Log collector
  - 200m CPU / 512Mi memory per node (varies by log volume)
  - On-disk buffers recommended; verify node disk capacity

Retention knobs and how they impact disk
Storage scales roughly linearly with retention.
- 7d => baseline (recommended)
- 14d => ~2x disk
- 30d => ~4–5x disk (needs more careful TSDB management and compaction headroom)

PVC headroom recommendations
- Target <= 70% utilization at steady-state (gives room for WAL/compactions and incident spikes).
- If using local-path or slower network storage, compaction stalls can increase disk transiently.

Disk class recommendations (Prometheus)
- Prefer SSD-backed persistent volumes (gp3/pd-ssd/managed-prem SSD equivalents).
- Avoid very slow HDD volumes for anything beyond Dev/POC.
- Ensure IOPS/throughput is sufficient; symptoms of underpowered disk: high WAL fsync latency, slow compactions, missed scrapes.

Prometheus memory guidance
Prometheus memory is driven by head series + label index + query workload.
- If you see frequent OOM: increase memory (first), then reduce cardinality / scrape frequency.
- Alert rules and recording rules also increase memory (more series in head).

Query load guidance
Grafana dashboards can cause expensive queries if panels are not using recording rules.
- Use recording rules for “golden signals” (RPS, error rate, p95/p99 latency, saturation) and build dashboards on top of those.
- Limit dashboard time ranges by default (e.g., 6h/24h) for large clusters.

Grafana sizing notes
Grafana is usually not the limiting component in Private; it becomes an issue if:
- Many concurrent users (>= 20–50)
- Expensive dashboards (wide time ranges, high-res queries, many panels)
Recommendations:
- Start: 250m CPU / 512Mi memory.
- If dashboards feel slow or Grafana restarts: bump to 1 vCPU / 2Gi memory.
- Persistence:
  - If dashboards are loaded via configmaps and auth is external, Grafana can be stateless.
  - If customers install plugins or want saved dashboards locally, enable a small PVC (5–10Gi).

Alertmanager sizing notes
Alertmanager is light. Size for HA rather than compute.
- 1 replica is fine for dev.
- 3 replicas recommended if customer expects paging continuity through node maintenance.

Log collector / forwarder sizing (Vector/Fluent Bit style)
We assume log export to customer backend is enabled (Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Loki, OTLP, etc.). The collector is usually a DaemonSet.

Baseline per-node requests/limits
- CPU: 100m (spikes if multiline parsing or heavy JSON transforms)
- Memory: 256Mi (spikes if backend is slow and buffers build)

Buffering
- For VPC installs with reliable connectivity to log backend: memory buffering is often sufficient.
- For on-prem / air-gapped-ish with intermittent connectivity:
  - Enable on-disk buffering (recommended)
  - Start with 10Gi per node for buffer
  - Increase if customer expects multi-hour outages of the log sink

Backpressure/dropping policy (needs to be explicit in customer docs)
- Default: do not block application pods. If collector can’t export, it should apply backpressure internally and eventually drop oldest buffered logs once limits reached.
- For regulated environments: customers may prefer “block or fail closed” semantics, but that has operational risk. Treat as an explicit opt-in.

How to validate sizing after install (what to look at)
Prometheus
- Check active series:
  - prometheus_tsdb_head_series
  - prometheus_tsdb_head_chunks
- Check ingestion and scrape health:
  - scrape_samples_post_metric_relabeling
  - up{job=...}
  - rate(scrape_duration_seconds_sum[5m]) / rate(scrape_duration_seconds_count[5m])
- Check TSDB/disk:
  - prometheus_tsdb_storage_blocks_bytes
  - prometheus_tsdb_wal_size_bytes
  - node_filesystem_avail_bytes on the Prometheus PVC mount (depending on exporter)
- Check performance red flags:
  - high rate(prometheus_engine_query_duration_seconds_bucket{le="..."})
  - high prometheus_tsdb_compactions_failed_total
  - frequent container OOMKilled events

Grafana
- Pod restarts / OOM
- Dashboard load times (subjective) + datasource query durations

Log collector
- Export error counts / retry backlog
- Buffer usage (memory + disk)
- Dropped log counters (if enabled)

Common “gotchas” that cause mis-sizing
1) Cardinality leaks
- Symptoms: active series grows linearly with traffic or with number of requests.
- Fix: remove unbounded labels; aggregate via recording rules; cap route/model dimension.

2) Too-aggressive scrape interval
- 5s scraping for everything is expensive. Default should be 15s for SLO-critical runtime, 30s for most infra.

3) NetworkPolicy changes break scrapes
- Looks like “Prometheus is empty” but is actually connectivity. Validate scrape targets and service monitors after any policy hardening.

4) Air-gapped + log export misconfigured
- Collector retries forever, builds buffers, eats memory/disk. Ensure sane retry limits + disk buffer size + clear drop policy.

Open questions / TODOs before GA
- Confirm expected active series ranges after metric label reductions (ref: PR-18503) for each footprint.
- Decide whether installer defaults should ship 7d retention universally or use disk-based heuristics.
- Define minimum node disk requirements when on-disk log buffering is enabled.
- Add “sizing calculator” snippet to docs: given active series + scrape interval + retention, compute recommended PVC.

Notes to self (internal)
- Once smoke tests are stable (PR-18544), add an automated check that warns if prometheus_tsdb_head_series exceeds the selected footprint envelope.
- If we keep retention at 7d by default, ensure dashboards use appropriate query ranges and recording rules so customers aren’t tempted to turn retention up just for convenience.
