Convert to GiB: divide by 1024^3

Examples at 15s scrape (5760 samples/series/day)
- storage/day ≈ active_series * 5760 * 3 bytes
  - 100k series => ~1.6 GiB/day (plan 2–3 GiB/day with safety margin)
  - 250k series => ~4.0 GiB/day (plan 6–8 GiB/day)
  - 500k series => ~8.0 GiB/day (plan 12–16 GiB/day)

Important: if label cardinality drifts (e.g., model=unbounded, route=unbounded), active series can grow by 5–20x and these estimates become invalid.

Footprints (pick one) and recommended sizes
We’ve found it’s easier for customers to choose a footprint based on cluster scale / throughput, then tune based on observed series + disk growth.

FOOTPRINT A — Dev / POC
Typical: <= 2 runtime pods, <= 1–2 GPU nodes, low QPS, few dashboards users.
- Expected active series: 30k–80k
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 500m–1 vCPU / 2–4Gi memory
  - PVC: 50Gi
  - Retention: 7d (or 3d if very small disks)
  - Scrape: 30s default (15s only if needed for latency SLO burn alerts)
- Grafana
  - 200m CPU / 512Mi memory
  - PVC: optional; 5Gi if enabled
- Alertmanager
  - 100m CPU / 256Mi memory
- Log collector
  - 50–100m CPU / 128–256Mi memory per node
  - Buffer: memory-only is fine; 5Gi disk buffer if on-prem + unreliable export

FOOTPRINT B — Prod Small (recommended default)
Typical: 4–16 runtime pods, <= 8–16 GPUs total, normal dashboard usage, standard alerting.
- Expected active series: 80k–200k
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 2 vCPU / 8–12Gi memory
  - PVC: 100Gi
  - Retention: 7d
  - Scrape: 15s for runtime + 30s for infra (if split scrape configs are used)
- Grafana
  - 250m–500m CPU / 512Mi–1Gi memory
  - PVC: optional; 5–10Gi if enabled
- Alertmanager
  - 200m CPU / 512Mi memory
- Log collector
  - 100m CPU / 256Mi memory per node
  - Optional on-disk buffer: 10Gi per node (on-prem strongly recommended)

FOOTPRINT C — Prod Medium
Typical: 16–64 runtime pods, 32–64 GPUs, multiple teams using dashboards, heavier alerting/query load.
- Expected active series: 200k–500k
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 4 vCPU / 16–24Gi memory
  - PVC: 300Gi
  - Retention: 14d
  - Scrape: 15s (consider 30s for non-SLO-critical targets)
  - Consider: shard Prometheus or add remote_write if customer wants >14d
- Grafana
  - 500m–1 vCPU / 1–2Gi memory
  - Consider 2 replicas + shared datasource config; persistence optional
- Alertmanager
  - 3 replicas if customer requires HA paging; 200m CPU / 512Mi–1Gi memory
- Log collector
  - 100–200m CPU / 256–512Mi memory per node
  - On-disk buffer: 10–20Gi per node if exports can back up

FOOTPRINT D — Prod Large
Typical: 64+ runtime pods, 64–256 GPUs, high alert/query volume, more complex networking policies.
- Expected active series: 500k–1.2M (ONLY if cardinality controls are followed; otherwise risk is much higher)
- Prometheus
  - Requests/limits: 8 vCPU / 32–48Gi memory
  - PVC: 500Gi–1Ti
  - Retention: 14–30d (30d only with careful series control and disk class)
  - Strongly consider: sharding Prometheus by target group or remote_write to customer’s long-term store
- Grafana
  - 1–2 vCPU / 2–4Gi memory, 2 replicas
- Alertmanager
  - 3 replicas; integrate with customer paging policy
- Log collector
  - 200m CPU / 512Mi memory per node (varies by log volume)
  - On-disk buffers recommended; verify node disk capacity

Retention knobs and how they impact disk
Storage scales roughly linearly with retention.
- 7d => baseline (recommended)
- 14d => ~2x disk
- 30d => ~4–5x disk (needs more careful TSDB management and compaction headroom)

PVC headroom recommendations
- Target <= 70% utilization at steady-state (gives room for WAL/compactions and incident spikes).
- If using local-path or slower network storage, compaction stalls can increase disk transiently.

Disk class recommendations (Prometheus)
- Prefer SSD-backed persistent volumes (gp3/pd-ssd/managed-prem SSD equivalents).
- Avoid very slow HDD volumes for anything beyond Dev/POC.
- Ensure IOPS/throughput is sufficient; symptoms of underpowered disk: high WAL fsync latency, slow compactions, missed scrapes.

Prometheus memory guidance
Prometheus memory is driven by head series + label index + query workload.
- If you see frequent OOM: increase memory (first), then reduce cardinality / scrape frequency.
- Alert rules and recording rules also increase memory (more series in head).

Query load guidance
Grafana dashboards can cause expensive queries if panels are not using recording rules.
- Use recording rules for “golden signals” (RPS, error rate, p95/p99 latency, saturation) and build dashboards on top of those.
- Limit dashboard time ranges by default (e.g., 6h/24h) for large clusters.

Grafana sizing notes
Grafana is usually not the limiting component in Private; it becomes an issue if:
- Many concurrent users (>= 20–50)
- Expensive dashboards (wide time ranges, high-res queries, many panels)
Recommendations:
- Start: 250m CPU / 512Mi memory.
- If dashboards feel slow or Grafana restarts: bump to 1 vCPU / 2Gi memory.
- Persistence:
  - If dashboards are loaded via configmaps and auth is external, Grafana can be stateless.
  - If customers install plugins or want saved dashboards locally, enable a small PVC (5–10Gi).

Alertmanager sizing notes
Alertmanager is light. Size for HA rather than compute.
- 1 replica is fine for dev.
- 3 replicas recommended if customer expects paging continuity through node maintenance.

Log collector / forwarder sizing (Vector/Fluent Bit style)
We assume log export to customer backend is enabled (Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Loki, OTLP, etc.). The collector is usually a DaemonSet.

Baseline per-node requests/limits
- CPU: 100m (spikes if multiline parsing or heavy JSON transforms)
- Memory: 256Mi (spikes if backend is slow and buffers build)

Buffering
- For VPC installs with reliable connectivity to log backend: memory buffering is often sufficient.
- For on-prem / air-gapped-ish with intermittent connectivity:
  - Enable on-disk buffering (recommended)
  - Start with 10Gi per node for buffer
  - Increase if customer expects multi-hour outages of the log sink

Backpressure/dropping policy (needs to be explicit in customer docs)
- Default: do not block application pods. If collector can’t export, it should apply backpressure internally and eventually drop oldest buffered logs once limits reached.
- For regulated environments: customers may prefer “block or fail closed” semantics, but that has operational risk. Treat as an explicit opt-in.

How to validate sizing after install (what to look at)
Prometheus
- Check active series:
  - prometheus_tsdb_head_series
  - prometheus_tsdb_head_chunks
- Check ingestion and scrape health:
  - scrape_samples_post_metric_relabeling
  - up{job=...}
  - rate(scrape_duration_seconds_sum[5m]) / rate(scrape_duration_seconds_count[5m])
- Check TSDB/disk:
  - prometheus_tsdb_storage_blocks_bytes
  - prometheus_tsdb_wal_size_bytes
  - node_filesystem_avail_bytes on the Prometheus PVC mount (depending on exporter)
- Check performance red flags:
  - high rate(prometheus_engine_query_duration_seconds_bucket{le="..."})
  - high prometheus_tsdb_compactions_failed_total
  - frequent container OOMKilled events

Grafana
- Pod restarts / OOM
- Dashboard load times (subjective) + datasource query durations

Log collector
- Export error counts / retry backlog
- Buffer usage (memory + disk)
- Dropped log counters (if enabled)

Common “gotchas” that cause mis-sizing
1) Cardinality leaks
- Symptoms: active series grows linearly with traffic or with number of requests.
- Fix: remove unbounded labels; aggregate via recording rules; cap route/model dimension.

2) Too-aggressive scrape interval
- 5s scraping for everything is expensive. Default should be 15s for SLO-critical runtime, 30s for most infra.

3) NetworkPolicy changes break scrapes
- Looks like “Prometheus is empty” but is actually connectivity. Validate scrape targets and service monitors after any policy hardening.

4) Air-gapped + log export misconfigured
- Collector retries forever, builds buffers, eats memory/disk. Ensure sane retry limits + disk buffer size + clear drop policy.

Open questions / TODOs before GA
- Confirm expected active series ranges after metric label reductions (ref: PR-18503) for each footprint.
- Decide whether installer defaults should ship 7d retention universally or use disk-based heuristics.
- Define minimum node disk requirements when on-disk log buffering is enabled.
- Add “sizing calculator” snippet to docs: given active series + scrape interval + retention, compute recommended PVC.

Notes to self (internal)
- Once smoke tests are stable (PR-18544), add an automated check that warns if prometheus_tsdb_head_series exceeds the selected footprint envelope.
- If we keep retention at 7d by default, ensure dashboards use appropriate query ranges and recording rules so customers aren’t tempted to turn retention up just for convenience.

title: Burst neighbor impact analysis (tail latency + detection signals + circuit breaker thresholds) — v0.3
preview:
Context
- Dedicated “burst capacity” lets a tenant exceed their reserved baseline by borrowing GPUs from a shared, Redwood-managed burst pool (per region + hardware class).
- Primary SRE concern: bursting can create noisy-neighbor effects within a pool or upstream shared components (router, tokenization, kv-cache infra) that degrade tail latency (p95/p99) and/or error rates for non-burst tenants.
- This doc is analysis notes + initial thresholds. Expect revisions after load testing + beta telemetry.

Definitions (working)
- Baseline capacity: tenant’s reserved Dedicated allocation.
- Burst usage: traffic/compute above baseline admitted to the shared burst pool.
- Neighbor tenant: another Dedicated tenant in the same region/hardware class whose requests are NOT bursting (or bursting at a much lower level).
- Protected SLO (non-burst): We must not materially regress non-burst p95/p99 end-to-end latency and error rates while burst is enabled.

Hypothesis: how bursts cause tail latency regressions
We think there are 4 primary paths to “neighbor impact”:

1) GPU contention / scheduling
- Bursting increases queueing on burst GPUs and can spill into shared runtime scheduling decisions.
- If we co-locate multiple bursters in the same pool segment, “head-of-line” (HoL) blocking can increase per-request wait time variability.
- Tail amplification is expected when batching gets more aggressive under load and requests have varied sequence lengths.

2) Shared control-plane / routing and admission latency
- Admission controller call adds a hop; under overload it can time out, causing retries or slower failover.
- Telemetry lag can cause oscillation: we over-admit briefly, then slam denials; the sawtooth can produce tail jitter.

3) Hot shared dependencies
- Router/gateway CPU saturation (tokenization, request parsing, streaming fanout)
- KV-cache backing store pressure (if any shared services are on common infra)
- Metrics / logging pipeline backpressure causing request handler slowdowns (rare but seen in other launches)

4) “Fail-open” / fallback behavior
- If routing falls back to a different pool/region unexpectedly, we can get cross-zone latency and tail spikes.
- If customers retry aggressively on burst denials (429/overload), they can create feedback loops that amplify load.

What “good” looks like (guardrail goals)
- When bursts happen, burst tenants may see some variability (they opted into burst), but non-burst tenants should remain within normal tail bounds.
- Our safety mechanism should:
  - Detect neighbor impact quickly (sub-5 minutes for severe tail blowups).
  - Prefer reducing burst admission over degrading the entire pool.
  - Provide debuggable reason codes and clear dashboards.

Measurement approach
We need to isolate “burst-induced regression” from normal variance:

A) Compare non-burst tenants to their own baseline
- Use trailing 7d/28d per-tenant baselines (same time-of-day if possible) to compute delta.
- For each tenant T, compute:
  - p95_delta = (p95_now - p95_baseline) / p95_baseline
  - p99_delta = (p99_now - p99_baseline) / p99_baseline
  - error_delta = (5xx_rate_now - 5xx_rate_baseline)

B) Compare within the same pool segment
- Within {region, hw_class, pool_segment}, split traffic into:
  - burst traffic (admitted above baseline)
  - non-burst traffic
- Track p95/p99 separately; the neighbor signal is the non-burst tail.

C) Use “tail amplification ratio” as a canary
- tail_amp = p99 / p50 (or p95 / p50)
- If tail_amp increases, we’re likely seeing queueing + variability; it’s less sensitive to absolute lat shifts.

D) Correlate with burst saturation signals
- Denials, queue depth, GPU utilization, admission latency, telemetry lag.

NOTE: We should be careful about per-route heterogeneity (chat vs embeddings). Ideally compute per-route, then roll up using token-weighted averages.

Candidate detection metrics (proposed)
Metrics should be available at:
- pool level: {region, hw_class, pool_segment}
- tenant level: {tenant_id, region, hw_class}
- route/model level only if label cardinality is controlled (Allison/Logan to confirm).

1) Non-burst tail deltas
- dedicated_request_latency_ms_p95{traffic_class="non_burst", region, hw_class, pool_segment}
- dedicated_request_latency_ms_p99{traffic_class="non_burst", ...}
- Derived alert metric:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct = (p99_now / p99_baseline) - 1

2) Tail amplification
- dedicated_request_latency_ms_p50 / p95 / p99 (same label set)
- Derived:
  - nonburst_tail_amp = p99 / p50

3) Denial / throttling rates
- burst_admission_denied_total{reason_code, region, hw_class}
- burst_admission_admitted_total{region, hw_class}
- Derived:
  - denial_ratio = denied / (admitted + denied)

4) Saturation and queueing
- burst_pool_queue_depth{region, hw_class, pool_segment}
- burst_pool_queue_wait_ms_p95{...} (if available)
- gpu_sm_utilization{...} and gpu_mem_utilization{...}

5) Admission controller health
- burst_admission_rpc_latency_ms_p95
- burst_admission_rpc_error_rate
- burst_telemetry_lag_seconds_p95 (lag between fleet signals and admission decisions)

6) Neighbor impact “smoking gun” composite
Proposed composite score per pool segment (for dashboard; alerting may still use individual thresholds):
- neighbor_impact_score =
  w1 * clamp(nonburst_p99_delta_pct, 0, 2.0) +
  w2 * clamp(nonburst_tail_amp / tail_amp_baseline - 1, 0, 2.0) +
  w3 * clamp(nonburst_5xx_delta, 0, 0.05)
Initial weights: w1=1.0, w2=0.5, w3=2.0 (error rate is “more severe”).

Candidate circuit breaker model (what it does)
We likely need two layers:

Layer 1: Pool-local burst reduction (fast, targeted)
- Triggered by pool segment conditions.
- Actions:
  - reduce max_burst_ratio (e.g., from 2.0x to 1.2x)
  - increase cooldown window for burst re-entries
  - switch from “queue” to “deny immediately” for certain reason codes to prevent HoL and long waits
  - optionally disable burst for lowest priority tier first (if tiers exist)

Layer 2: Global fleet safety cap (last resort)
- Triggered by region/hw-wide saturation or broad non-burst regressions.
- Actions:
  - disable burst pool entirely for {region, hw_class} for a short TTL (e.g., 15–30 min) and page SRE/Infra.

Proposed thresholds (initial numbers; expect tuning)
We want thresholds that:
- don’t flap during normal variation
- catch meaningful regressions quickly
- are conservative early in beta

All thresholds below assume evaluation over rolling windows with minimum request volume.

A) Non-burst tail latency regression triggers (SLO gate)
Applies to: {region, hw_class, pool_segment} aggregated non-burst traffic.

SEVERE (auto-disable burst for segment; page)
- Condition A1:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > +0.35 (35%) for 5 minutes
  - AND nonburst_rps >= 20 rps (avoid tiny samples)
- OR Condition A2:
  - nonburst_p99 absolute > 2.0s for 5 minutes (guardrail cap for “something is very wrong”)

MODERATE (auto-reduce burst caps; no page, create incident in channel)
- Condition A3:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > +0.20 (20%) for 10 minutes
  - AND tail_amp increase > +0.25 (25%)

B) Tail amplification spike triggers (early warning)
- Condition B1:
  - nonburst_tail_amp (p99/p50) > 6.0 for 10 minutes
  - AND p50 stable (p50_delta_pct < +0.10)
Rationale: tail-only blowups often show up here before p99 delta breaches.

C) Error-rate based triggers
SEVERE
- Condition C1:
  - nonburst_5xx_rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes
  - OR gateway_429_rate spikes > 2% for 5 minutes (if 429 indicates overload/backpressure)

D) Saturation / queueing triggers (protect against imminent tail blowup)
- Condition D1:
  - gpu_sm_utilization > 92% AND gpu_mem_utilization > 90% for 10 minutes
  - AND burst_pool_queue_depth increasing monotonically (or queue_wait_p95 > 250ms)
Action: reduce burst ratio even if tail deltas not yet high.

E) Admission controller degradation triggers
- Condition E1:
  - burst_admission_rpc_error_rate > 1% for 5 minutes OR p95 > 150ms for 10 minutes
Action: fail-safe to deny burst (not fail-open), and alert platform.

F) Telemetry lag / oscillation triggers (beta lesson)
- Condition F1:
  - burst_telemetry_lag_seconds_p95 > 20s for 10 minutes
  - AND denial_ratio swings by > 30 percentage points within 10 minutes
Action: apply smoothing mode; clamp burst admits; consider temporarily switching to stricter token bucket refill.

Notes on threshold choices
- The +20% / +35% p99 delta numbers are intentionally conservative for early rollout. We can loosen later per region/hw once we have stable baselines.
- The absolute p99 cap (2.0s) is a sanity breaker. It may be too strict for a subset of routes (long-context chat). If so, we should apply it only to “short request classes” or to normalized latency per output token.

Open question: normalize latency by tokens?
- For chat/generation, absolute latency is strongly token-dependent.
- We should track:
  - e2e_latency_ms_per_output_token_p95/p99 (or ms/token)
  - time_to_first_token (TTFT) separately
- Neighbor impact might present as TTFT regression even if overall completion time mostly scales with output length.

Recommended alerting (practical)
- Page only on SEVERE triggers (A1/A2/C1) and global circuit breaker activation.
- Everything else routes to #eng-sre + creates a ticket with dashboard links.

Suggested dashboards (panels we need)
1) Pool overview (per region/hw)
- burst_admitted vs denied
- saturation (gpu util, queue depth)
- non-burst p95/p99 vs baseline band

2) Neighbor impact view (per pool segment)
- scatter plot: burst_admit_rate vs nonburst_p99_delta
- top N segments by neighbor_impact_score

3) Tenant drilldown (support-facing)
- for tenant X:
  - burst usage and cap
  - denial reason breakdown
  - their own p95/p99 and TTFT
  - “other tenants bursting” indicator at pool level (redacted; no tenant identity)

Mitigations we can apply (ordered)
- Reduce burst max ratio (global or per segment) and increase cooldown.
- Tighten fairness enforcement (token bucket refill rate; priority tiers). Ensure no starvation.
- Disable queueing mode; prefer immediate deny (avoid HoL / long waits).
- Rate-limit retries at gateway; recommend client backoff in docs.
- If the pool is under-provisioned: increase pool size or split segments (blast-radius reduction).

Notes from early testing / anecdotes (not rigorous)
- In synthetic tests, the worst tail spikes happen when:
  - one burster sends long-context requests while others are short
  - batching becomes “too efficient” and increases latency variance
  - telemetry lag causes brief over-admit then harsh denial (sawtooth)
- Action item: ensure we can identify “sequence length mix skew” as a label or at least as a derived panel from histograms.

Edge cases / gotchas
- Low-volume tenants: p99 is noisy; should gate alerts on min requests.
- Partial brownouts: if admission controller is slow but not failing, the extra hop can hurt tail by itself.
- Cross-pool shared infra: if router is shared across multiple pools, pool-local thresholds might miss “global neighbor impact.”

Action items / TODO
- [Allison/Logan] Confirm metric label sets + cardinality constraints (especially tenant_id).
- [Peter] Confirm whether 429 is the correct overload signal for burst denials vs explicit denial codes.
- [Keiko] Provide expected “safe” GPU util ceilings per hw class (H100 vs L40S) and batching behavior.
- [Mina/Ravi] Validate how routing fallback interacts with non-burst traffic (ensure no surprise cross-zone).
- [Sean/Rafael] After ENG-4841 load tests, update thresholds based on observed deltas; decide on TTFT vs full latency for paging.

Appendix: strawman threshold table (copy/paste)
- Segment auto-disable burst:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > 35% for 5m AND nonburst_rps >= 20
  - OR nonburst_p99 > 2000ms for 5m
  - OR nonburst_5xx_rate > 0.5% for 5m
- Segment auto-reduce burst caps:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > 20% for 10m AND tail_amp increase > 25%
  - OR queue_wait_p95 > 250ms for 10m with high GPU util
- Admission controller fail-safe:
  - rpc_error_rate > 1% for 5m OR rpc_p95 > 150ms for 10m => deny burst, alert platform

Owner notes
- This doc is intentionally “SRE-biased”: we’d rather deny burst than degrade baseline Dedicated performance.
- We should align with Product on how to message expected behavior to burst customers (denials are normal under guardrails; they should design retry/backoff).

- catch meaningful regressions quickly
- are conservative early in beta

All thresholds below assume evaluation over rolling windows with minimum request volume.

A) Non-burst tail latency regression triggers (SLO gate)
Applies to: {region, hw_class, pool_segment} aggregated non-burst traffic.

SEVERE (auto-disable burst for segment; page)
- Condition A1:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > +0.35 (35%) for 5 minutes
  - AND nonburst_rps >= 20 rps (avoid tiny samples)
- OR Condition A2:
  - nonburst_p99 absolute > 2.0s for 5 minutes (guardrail cap for “something is very wrong”)

MODERATE (auto-reduce burst caps; no page, create incident in channel)
- Condition A3:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > +0.20 (20%) for 10 minutes
  - AND tail_amp increase > +0.25 (25%)

B) Tail amplification spike triggers (early warning)
- Condition B1:
  - nonburst_tail_amp (p99/p50) > 6.0 for 10 minutes
  - AND p50 stable (p50_delta_pct < +0.10)
Rationale: tail-only blowups often show up here before p99 delta breaches.

C) Error-rate based triggers
SEVERE
- Condition C1:
  - nonburst_5xx_rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes
  - OR gateway_429_rate spikes > 2% for 5 minutes (if 429 indicates overload/backpressure)

D) Saturation / queueing triggers (protect against imminent tail blowup)
- Condition D1:
  - gpu_sm_utilization > 92% AND gpu_mem_utilization > 90% for 10 minutes
  - AND burst_pool_queue_depth increasing monotonically (or queue_wait_p95 > 250ms)
Action: reduce burst ratio even if tail deltas not yet high.

E) Admission controller degradation triggers
- Condition E1:
  - burst_admission_rpc_error_rate > 1% for 5 minutes OR p95 > 150ms for 10 minutes
Action: fail-safe to deny burst (not fail-open), and alert platform.

F) Telemetry lag / oscillation triggers (beta lesson)
- Condition F1:
  - burst_telemetry_lag_seconds_p95 > 20s for 10 minutes
  - AND denial_ratio swings by > 30 percentage points within 10 minutes
Action: apply smoothing mode; clamp burst admits; consider temporarily switching to stricter token bucket refill.

Notes on threshold choices
- The +20% / +35% p99 delta numbers are intentionally conservative for early rollout. We can loosen later per region/hw once we have stable baselines.
- The absolute p99 cap (2.0s) is a sanity breaker. It may be too strict for a subset of routes (long-context chat). If so, we should apply it only to “short request classes” or to normalized latency per output token.

Open question: normalize latency by tokens?
- For chat/generation, absolute latency is strongly token-dependent.
- We should track:
  - e2e_latency_ms_per_output_token_p95/p99 (or ms/token)
  - time_to_first_token (TTFT) separately
- Neighbor impact might present as TTFT regression even if overall completion time mostly scales with output length.

Recommended alerting (practical)
- Page only on SEVERE triggers (A1/A2/C1) and global circuit breaker activation.
- Everything else routes to #eng-sre + creates a ticket with dashboard links.

Suggested dashboards (panels we need)
1) Pool overview (per region/hw)
- burst_admitted vs denied
- saturation (gpu util, queue depth)
- non-burst p95/p99 vs baseline band

2) Neighbor impact view (per pool segment)
- scatter plot: burst_admit_rate vs nonburst_p99_delta
- top N segments by neighbor_impact_score

3) Tenant drilldown (support-facing)
- for tenant X:
  - burst usage and cap
  - denial reason breakdown
  - their own p95/p99 and TTFT
  - “other tenants bursting” indicator at pool level (redacted; no tenant identity)

Mitigations we can apply (ordered)
- Reduce burst max ratio (global or per segment) and increase cooldown.
- Tighten fairness enforcement (token bucket refill rate; priority tiers). Ensure no starvation.
- Disable queueing mode; prefer immediate deny (avoid HoL / long waits).
- Rate-limit retries at gateway; recommend client backoff in docs.
- If the pool is under-provisioned: increase pool size or split segments (blast-radius reduction).

Notes from early testing / anecdotes (not rigorous)
- In synthetic tests, the worst tail spikes happen when:
  - one burster sends long-context requests while others are short
  - batching becomes “too efficient” and increases latency variance
  - telemetry lag causes brief over-admit then harsh denial (sawtooth)
- Action item: ensure we can identify “sequence length mix skew” as a label or at least as a derived panel from histograms.

Edge cases / gotchas
- Low-volume tenants: p99 is noisy; should gate alerts on min requests.
- Partial brownouts: if admission controller is slow but not failing, the extra hop can hurt tail by itself.
- Cross-pool shared infra: if router is shared across multiple pools, pool-local thresholds might miss “global neighbor impact.”

Action items / TODO
- [Allison/Logan] Confirm metric label sets + cardinality constraints (especially tenant_id).
- [Peter] Confirm whether 429 is the correct overload signal for burst denials vs explicit denial codes.
- [Keiko] Provide expected “safe” GPU util ceilings per hw class (H100 vs L40S) and batching behavior.
- [Mina/Ravi] Validate how routing fallback interacts with non-burst traffic (ensure no surprise cross-zone).
- [Sean/Rafael] After ENG-4841 load tests, update thresholds based on observed deltas; decide on TTFT vs full latency for paging.

Appendix: strawman threshold table (copy/paste)
- Segment auto-disable burst:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > 35% for 5m AND nonburst_rps >= 20
  - OR nonburst_p99 > 2000ms for 5m
  - OR nonburst_5xx_rate > 0.5% for 5m
- Segment auto-reduce burst caps:
  - nonburst_p99_delta_pct > 20% for 10m AND tail_amp increase > 25%
  - OR queue_wait_p95 > 250ms for 10m with high GPU util
- Admission controller fail-safe:
  - rpc_error_rate > 1% for 5m OR rpc_p95 > 150ms for 10m => deny burst, alert platform

Owner notes
- This doc is intentionally “SRE-biased”: we’d rather deny burst than degrade baseline Dedicated performance.
- We should align with Product on how to message expected behavior to burst customers (denials are normal under guardrails; they should design retry/backoff).

title: Route Experiments (A/B Testing) — SRE On-call Prep Notes
preview:
Purpose
These notes are for SRE on-call readiness for Route-level Experiments ("route experiments" / A/B testing at the request-routing layer) ahead of broader beta expansion and GA. The primary SRE goals are:
- Detect and contain customer-visible impact quickly (wrong routing, elevated error/latency, runaway cost).
- Ensure guardrails/auto-stop mechanisms are safe (avoid false positives causing churned traffic or incident loops).
- Provide a clear triage flow, escalation paths, and the minimum debugging evidence to collect.

Scope
- Control plane: experiment config API availability + config propagation (staleness) to routing layer
- Data plane: deterministic assignment + precedence with rollouts/fallback policies
- Telemetry: attribution integrity and metric cardinality safety
- Console: results freshness and query load (less critical than routing correctness, but can page if it threatens DB)

Non-goals
- Statistical significance correctness (out of scope for GA). On-call treats results as informational; guardrails are threshold-based.

Definitions (operational)
- Experiment assignment: deterministic bucketing based on a "stickiness key" (preferred: customer-provided stable identifier). When key missing/low-entropy, distribution can skew.
- Propagation lag / staleness: time between config update in control plane and config being used by routers/policy engine.
- Attribution drop rate: % requests where experiment_id/variant_id is expected but missing from tracing/metrics context.
- Soft-stop: mark degraded + notify + optionally auto-stop after human review.
- Hard-stop: immediately stop experiment (routing all traffic to control/default) based on severe guardrail breach; gated by kill switch.

Quick links (dashboards + first checks)
1) "Route Experiments — Guardrails" Grafana dashboard
- Panels: p50/p95/p99 latency by variant, error rate, throughput, cost/token (if available), guardrail status
2) "Route Experiments — Config Propagation" dashboard
- Panels: propagation_lag_seconds p50/p95/p99, staleness violations, config fetch errors, cache hit rate
3) "Route Experiments — Attribution Health" dashboard
- Panels: attribution_drop_rate, missing experiment_id rate, missing variant_id rate, missing route_id normalization rate
4) Internal debug endpoint (RBAC-protected): Assignment Explain
- Returns: selected experiment, variant, hash inputs, stickiness key source, config version, evaluation trace

Paging philosophy (what we actually page on)
We page on issues that imply customer traffic is being routed incorrectly, experiments are destabilizing production, or system health is at risk.
- P0: widespread incorrect routing / elevated errors / severe latency regressions / control plane outage preventing safe stop
- P1: propagation lag beyond SLO affecting safe operations, attribution collapse causing guardrails to be blind, auto-stop thrashing
- P2: Console results stale, high query load, localized skew without customer impact

SLOs / alert thresholds (draft, tuned during beta)
NOTE: thresholds below are meant to be pragmatic and to avoid noisy paging during early beta. Prefer ticket/notification for single-tenant or low-traffic scenarios.

A) Control plane API + config propagation
1) Experiment API availability (create/update/start/stop)
- Alert: 5xx rate > 1% for 5m (P1) OR > 5% for 2m (P0)
- Alert: p95 API latency > 2s for 10m (P2 -> ticket), > 5s for 5m (P1)
2) Propagation lag (config update -> routers using new version)
- SLO target: p95 propagation lag < 60s, p99 < 180s (beta target; revisit for GA)
- Page (P1): p95 > 180s for 10m OR p99 > 600s for 5m
- Page (P0): p99 > 1800s for 5m AND active experiments count > 10 (broad blast radius)
3) Config fetch failures on router/policy engine
- Page (P1): sustained fetch error rate > 0.5% for 10m
- Page (P0): fetch error rate > 2% for 5m or routers falling back to "no config" safe default at scale

B) Data plane routing correctness
1) Variant 0 traffic / stuck traffic detection
- Notify: any active experiment shows 0 traffic for a non-zero allocation variant for 10m (P2)
- Page (P1): > 3 active experiments with variant-0-traffic anomaly for 10m (likely systemic)
2) Split skew / determinism drift
- Notify: observed allocation deviates > 10 percentage points from configured split over 30m (requires enough traffic; otherwise suppress)
- Page (P1): deviation > 20 points over 30m for top-5 traffic routes OR determinism check failures (same stickiness key yields different variant) observed across restarts
3) Precedence conflicts (experiment vs canary rollout vs fallback)
- Page (P1): experiments not applied due to fallback overriding at scale (symptom: spike in "fallback_selected" with experiment active)
- Collect: route_id, rollout_id, fallback policy id, experiment_id, router build/version

C) Telemetry / attribution integrity
1) Attribution drop rate (experiment fields missing)
- SLO target: attribution_drop_rate < 0.5% overall (beta), < 0.2% GA goal
- Page (P1): > 2% for 10m across any region with active experiments
- Page (P0): > 5% for 5m across 2+ regions (guardrails blind + results wrong)
2) Streaming/retry path attribution
- Notify (P2): spike in missing attribution correlated with streaming=true
- Page (P1): missing attribution on streaming retries > 3% for 10m after a deployment (regression likely)

D) Guardrails / auto-stop stability
1) Auto-stop triggered events
- Notify: any hard-stop triggered (always) + create incident if customer-facing
- Page (P1): hard-stop triggers > 3 per hour or repeated stop/start thrash on same experiment_id (suggest false positives or unstable metrics)
2) Kill switch status
- Ensure: global kill switch for hard-stop is accessible to SRE (documented owner + procedure)
- Page (P0): inability to disable hard-stop during false-positive cascade

E) Console results + data freshness + query load
1) Results freshness
- Ticket: results freshness lag > 30m for 2h
- Page (P2/P1 depending on DB impact): results freshness lag > 2h for high-usage tenants OR repeated customer support escalations
2) Query cost / DB load from results endpoints
- Page (P1): results endpoint p95 latency > 3s and DB CPU > 80% for 10m (prevent broader Console outage)

Likely failure modes (what we expect to see)
1) Config propagation lag / stale configs
Symptoms
- Customers report "I changed split but traffic didn’t move" or "stop didn’t stop".
- Dashboards show rising propagation_lag_seconds p95/p99.
- Routers show cache not updating / high fetch error.
Common causes
- Config service deploy/regression (cache invalidation bug)
- Regional networking issues between routers and control plane
- Hot partitions / increased load due to list operations or audit exports
Immediate mitigation
- If stop is required: use emergency stop path (if separate), or temporarily disable experiments feature flag for affected tenant/region.
- If systemic: roll back last control plane deployment; reduce config refresh interval only if safe.
Notes
- During a lag, routers should use last-known-good config within staleness bounds; if staleness exceeded, they should fail safe (no experiment) rather than apply unknown config.

2) Variant skew due to missing/low-entropy stickiness key
Symptoms
- Split is configured 50/50 but observed 95/5 (often all to one variant).
- Skew correlates to traffic without provided sticky key (e.g., anonymous users) or defaulting to IP/user-agent.
Common causes
- Customer not passing a stable key; SDK integration incomplete
- Router default key picks a low-entropy value
- Misconfigured eligibility rules causing a subpopulation to dominate
Immediate mitigation
- Reduce experiment scope (eligibility) or set allocation to 0 for affected variant; add Console warning to require sticky key
- If customer impact: stop experiment and revert
Evidence to collect
- % traffic missing sticky key per route
- stickiness key source breakdown (header vs derived)
- assignment explain output for sample requests

3) Attribution drop (blind guardrails)
Symptoms
- Metrics show big gap between total request count and experiment-attributed request count
- Guardrails show "insufficient data" or results freeze
Common causes
- Missing context propagation in streaming/retry paths
- OTel span sampling misconfiguration dropping attributes
- Tag cardinality protection stripping attributes unexpectedly
Immediate mitigation
- If guardrails rely on attribution: disable hard-stop; run experiments in monitor-only
- Roll back telemetry change if recent
- Use debug endpoint + logs to confirm fields exist in request context even if missing in spans

4) Metrics cardinality blow-up
Symptoms
- Metrics backend cost spike; ingestion latency; dropped series
- On-call sees warnings about tag explosion (route_id normalization failures, too many unique model_id/route combinations)
Common causes
- route_id emitted as raw path instead of template
- too many dimensions enabled simultaneously (route_id + model_id + tenant_id + experiment_id + variant_id)
Immediate mitigation
- Enable "restricted tagging" mode (sampled expansions only)
- Roll back release enabling extra tags; prioritize keeping experiment_id + variant_id, drop route raw path first

5) Precedence bug: fallback policy overrides experiment
Symptoms
- Experiment configured but traffic shows mostly fallback-selected model
- Customers see unexpected model behavior; results show no difference
Common causes
- Routing order incorrect; experiment evaluator applied after fallback
Immediate mitigation
- Stop affected experiment to remove ambiguity
- Escalate to routing/policy team with reproduction + route config snapshot

6) Auto-stop false positives
Symptoms
- Experiment stops unexpectedly without real latency/error regression
- Multiple stop events around deployment, low traffic, or partial outages in telemetry
Common causes
- Guardrail window too small; low traffic + p99 unstable
- Attribution drop makes denominator wrong
- Regional partial outage triggers guardrail but not representative
Immediate mitigation
- Flip kill switch to disable hard-stop; switch to soft-stop only
- Increase minimum traffic thresholds; widen evaluation window
- Communicate to Support/CS: "hard-stop disabled temporarily"

Triage flow (step-by-step)
Step 0: Identify the impact domain
- Is this routing correctness (data plane), config availability (control plane), or observability/attribution (telemetry)?
- Check: any customer-facing error/latency alerts firing simultaneously?

Step 1: Confirm whether experiments are active + blast radius
- How many active experiments in affected region?
- Are multiple tenants/routes impacted?
- If single tenant only: treat as P2 unless customer is blocked or broad impact.

Step 2: Check propagation health
- propagation_lag_seconds p95/p99
- config fetch errors / cache hit rate
If propagation is bad: focus on control plane + config distribution first. Don’t chase skew/attribution until configs are stable.

Step 3: Check routing correctness indicators
- variant-0-traffic detection
- split skew on high-traffic routes
- determinism drift checks (if available)
Use assignment explain endpoint for a few known stickiness keys.

Step 4: Check attribution integrity
- attribution_drop_rate overall and by streaming=true
- compare router logs (context has experiment fields) vs span export (fields missing)
If attribution failing: disable hard-stop and treat guardrails as unreliable.

Step 5: Mitigate
- Stop experiment(s) if they appear to be causing errors/latency regressions or incorrect routing
- If stop doesn’t propagate: disable experiments feature flag for tenant/region (beta allowlist)
- Roll back last deployment if correlated and safe

Step 6: Communicate + document
- Create incident channel if P0/P1
- Note exact experiment_id(s), route_id(s), region(s), control plane version, router build hash, and timestamps
- Hand-off to owning team with minimal reproduction

Escalation paths (who to page / when)
P0 (customer traffic at risk, or inability to stop experiments)
- Page SRE on-call (primary) + SRE lead (Sean Gallagher)
- Page Platform Control Plane on-call (Ethan Park’s rotation) if propagation/config outage suspected
- Page Routing/Policy Engine on-call (Jordan Lee / Mina Haddad) if incorrect routing/precedence suspected
- Page Telemetry/Analytics on-call (Logan Wright / Caleb Johnson) if attribution collapse is widespread
- If audit/security implications (unexpected debug endpoint exposure, audit export gaps during incident): page Security Eng (Marcus Lin)

P1 (degraded but contained)
- Start incident channel if multi-tenant or multi-region; otherwise a focused thread + ticket
- Pull in owners based on symptom:
  - Console/backend issues: Chloe Martin / Dylan Brooks
  - Results freshness / aggregation: Mei Lin / Nadia Rahman
  - Guardrail tuning: Rafael Mendes (SRE) + Marissa Cole (Product) if customer messaging needed

P2 (informational)
- File ticket with logs/screenshots; link dashboards; propose threshold tweaks

What evidence to capture (copy/paste checklist)
- experiment_id, variant_id(s), route_id (normalized), region, tenant/account id
- experiment config version and last update timestamp
- propagation lag metrics (p95/p99) at incident time
- router build/version and last deploy time
- attribution_drop_rate panels (overall + streaming)
- assignment explain output for 2–3 sample stickiness keys (redact user identifiers)
- any guardrail evaluation events (soft-stop/hard-stop) + kill switch state

Known sharp edges / reminders
- Low traffic makes p99 guardrails noisy; avoid hard-stop on low-volume routes without minimum sample thresholds.
- Attribution health is a prerequisite for guardrail correctness. If attribution is failing, hard-stop must be disabled.
- Stop semantics may be “draining” (in-flight requests) depending on router. Don’t assume immediate; verify via traffic panels.
- Debug endpoint is internal; confirm RBAC before sharing outputs. Redact stickiness keys and customer identifiers.

Open questions / follow-ups (as of 2025-03-26)
- Final GA thresholds for propagation lag (beta targets above may be too strict/too loose depending on rollout).
- Minimum traffic thresholds for enabling hard-stop per experiment (need product + SRE alignment).
- Confirm ownership for results API and aggregation job paging: if DB health at risk, SRE pages; otherwise Telemetry team owns.
- Route_id normalization: ensure we never emit raw path in metrics tags (cardinality).

Changelog
- 2025-03-11 (Rafael): added attribution drop paging thresholds + streaming-specific checks
- 2025-03-18 (Allison): added cardinality blow-up mitigation + tagging restriction notes
- 2025-03-26 (Sean): clarified escalation paths and P0 criteria; added evidence checklist

Triage flow (step-by-step)
Step 0: Identify the impact domain
- Is this routing correctness (data plane), config availability (control plane), or observability/attribution (telemetry)?
- Check: any customer-facing error/latency alerts firing simultaneously?

Step 1: Confirm whether experiments are active + blast radius
- How many active experiments in affected region?
- Are multiple tenants/routes impacted?
- If single tenant only: treat as P2 unless customer is blocked or broad impact.

Step 2: Check propagation health
- propagation_lag_seconds p95/p99
- config fetch errors / cache hit rate
If propagation is bad: focus on control plane + config distribution first. Don’t chase skew/attribution until configs are stable.

Step 3: Check routing correctness indicators
- variant-0-traffic detection
- split skew on high-traffic routes
- determinism drift checks (if available)
Use assignment explain endpoint for a few known stickiness keys.

Step 4: Check attribution integrity
- attribution_drop_rate overall and by streaming=true
- compare router logs (context has experiment fields) vs span export (fields missing)
If attribution failing: disable hard-stop and treat guardrails as unreliable.

Step 5: Mitigate
- Stop experiment(s) if they appear to be causing errors/latency regressions or incorrect routing
- If stop doesn’t propagate: disable experiments feature flag for tenant/region (beta allowlist)
- Roll back last deployment if correlated and safe

Step 6: Communicate + document
- Create incident channel if P0/P1
- Note exact experiment_id(s), route_id(s), region(s), control plane version, router build hash, and timestamps
- Hand-off to owning team with minimal reproduction

Escalation paths (who to page / when)
P0 (customer traffic at risk, or inability to stop experiments)
- Page SRE on-call (primary) + SRE lead (Sean Gallagher)
- Page Platform Control Plane on-call (Ethan Park’s rotation) if propagation/config outage suspected
- Page Routing/Policy Engine on-call (Jordan Lee / Mina Haddad) if incorrect routing/precedence suspected
- Page Telemetry/Analytics on-call (Logan Wright / Caleb Johnson) if attribution collapse is widespread
- If audit/security implications (unexpected debug endpoint exposure, audit export gaps during incident): page Security Eng (Marcus Lin)

P1 (degraded but contained)
- Start incident channel if multi-tenant or multi-region; otherwise a focused thread + ticket
- Pull in owners based on symptom:
  - Console/backend issues: Chloe Martin / Dylan Brooks
  - Results freshness / aggregation: Mei Lin / Nadia Rahman
  - Guardrail tuning: Rafael Mendes (SRE) + Marissa Cole (Product) if customer messaging needed

P2 (informational)
- File ticket with logs/screenshots; link dashboards; propose threshold tweaks

What evidence to capture (copy/paste checklist)
- experiment_id, variant_id(s), route_id (normalized), region, tenant/account id
- experiment config version and last update timestamp
- propagation lag metrics (p95/p99) at incident time
- router build/version and last deploy time
- attribution_drop_rate panels (overall + streaming)
- assignment explain output for 2–3 sample stickiness keys (redact user identifiers)
- any guardrail evaluation events (soft-stop/hard-stop) + kill switch state

Known sharp edges / reminders
- Low traffic makes p99 guardrails noisy; avoid hard-stop on low-volume routes without minimum sample thresholds.
- Attribution health is a prerequisite for guardrail correctness. If attribution is failing, hard-stop must be disabled.
- Stop semantics may be “draining” (in-flight requests) depending on router. Don’t assume immediate; verify via traffic panels.
- Debug endpoint is internal; confirm RBAC before sharing outputs. Redact stickiness keys and customer identifiers.

Open questions / follow-ups (as of 2025-03-26)
- Final GA thresholds for propagation lag (beta targets above may be too strict/too loose depending on rollout).
- Minimum traffic thresholds for enabling hard-stop per experiment (need product + SRE alignment).
- Confirm ownership for results API and aggregation job paging: if DB health at risk, SRE pages; otherwise Telemetry team owns.
- Route_id normalization: ensure we never emit raw path in metrics tags (cardinality).

Changelog
- 2025-03-11 (Rafael): added attribution drop paging thresholds + streaming-specific checks
- 2025-03-18 (Allison): added cardinality blow-up mitigation + tagging restriction notes
- 2025-03-26 (Sean): clarified escalation paths and P0 criteria; added evidence checklist

title: Draft: Error budget policy for Dedicated throughput SLO (change gates + escalation thresholds)
preview:
Owner(s)
- Policy owner: Sean Gallagher (SLO semantics / error budget policy)
- Oncall owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE oncall policy + paging)
- Implementation: Aisha Bello (alerting + dashboards), Sergio Costa (dashboards)
- Release lead escalation: Connor O’Brien (change freeze enforcement)

Status
- Draft for internal preview. Target: align ahead of Dedicated tier internal enablement + limited GA.


1) Purpose
We are introducing a Dedicated throughput SLO with tiered commitments. This policy defines:
- how we interpret throughput error budget consumption
- when error budget status gates production changes (routing/runtime/infrastructure)
- when we escalate to an incident (and who gets paged)
- how we coordinate with Product/CS on customer comms when a Dedicated deployment is at risk

This is intended to prevent “death by a thousand cuts” where we keep shipping while the throughput floor is already degraded for one or more Dedicated customers.


2) Scope
In-scope
- Dedicated deployments only (hosted API is out-of-scope).
- All regions and all supported Dedicated shapes.
- Throughput SLO as defined in the canonical SLO doc + ADR (per-deployment attribution, canonical throughput metric).

Out-of-scope (handled by separate policies)
- Availability SLO / HTTP error rates (covered by standard API availability SLO policy)
- Provisioning lead-time and capacity acquisition timelines (fleet / capacity planning policy)
- Contractual remedies / credit policy (CS playbook + Finance/Legal terms)


3) Definitions (summary, not canonical)
Canonical signal (per ADR)
- Dedicated throughput is measured per deployment_id and model route using Redwood-defined token accounting.
- Streaming token accounting must be consistent with metering (see incident: double counting fix).

SLO objective (placeholder; pull final from SLO definition doc)
- 99.0% of 5-minute windows in a rolling 30-day window must meet or exceed the committed throughput floor for a given Dedicated deployment.
- A 5-minute window “meets” if observed throughput >= committed_floor AND the request mix qualifies (supported model/version + config).

Error budget
- Error budget is the allowed fraction of failing windows over the rolling 30-day window.
- For a 99.0% objective: 1.0% failing windows allowed.
  - 30 days * 24h * 60m = 43,200 minutes
  - 5-minute windows = 8,640 windows
  - 1.0% budget = 86 failing windows (≈ 430 minutes) per deployment per 30 days

Important: We track budgets at two layers
- Per-deployment budget: budget for each customer’s Dedicated deployment (primary operational indicator).
- Aggregate Dedicated budget: “fleet-wide” view (used for release gating, because a broad regression can hide in per-deployment noise).


4) What counts as “SLO burn” (budget consumption)
A window burns budget when:
- deployment has an active committed throughput floor (i.e., not in provisioning, not in “warmup/grace” period per contract), AND
- observed throughput < committed throughput floor, AND
- not excluded by the exclusion rules below.

Exclusions (policy intent)
We do not want to burn budget due to customer-induced throttling or non-supported configs.
Exclude windows where any of the following is true:
- Customer-side throttling or backpressure: customer sends below the committed concurrency/QPS required to reach the committed floor.
  - Note: we need a clear, auditable signal (admission logs / client QPS) before applying this exclusion.
- Unsupported model/version: deployment is pinned to a non-supported version for the shape/tier, or runs outside Redwood’s documented max context/batch recommendations.
- Maintenance events: explicitly declared, pre-announced maintenance with customer approval (Premium may have stricter requirements).
- Force majeure / upstream cloud event: only if declared as such and confirmed by incident commander + Infra lead.

Non-exclusions (we DO burn budget)
- Redwood regression in routing/admission control/runtime
- Capacity saturation in Redwood fleet (including mis-provisioning, headroom miscalc)
- Kernel/model perf regression introduced by Redwood changes
- Noisy neighbor within a Dedicated pool due to misconfigured isolation


5) Burn-rate alerting (and required responses)
We use multi-window burn-rate alerts to catch both fast and slow burns.

Alert levels (recommended; align with ENG-19288 / PR-19405 thresholds)
A) Page (customer-impact likely / fast burn)
- Trigger when burn rate >= 14x for 1h AND >= 6x for 6h (or equivalent multi-window pair).
- Required response: oncall SRE acknowledges within 10 minutes.
- Immediate actions:
  1) Validate metric integrity (streaming token accounting, double count, missing labels).
  2) Identify impacted deployments/tier/region/shape.
  3) Start incident if impact is sustained > 15 minutes or multiple Premium/Plus customers affected.

B) Ticket (slow burn / risk accumulation)
- Trigger when burn rate >= 2x for 6h AND >= 1x for 3d.
- Required response: triage within 1 business day.
- Create an owner + mitigation plan (capacity add, rollback, config change, perf investigation).

C) Budget low early warning (change gating input)
- Trigger when remaining budget for any Premium deployment < 70% (or predicted exhaustion within 7 days at current burn).
- Create “budget watch” entry in SRE weekly review and notify Product Dedicated (Lauren) + CS lead (Rachel) if customer risk is non-trivial.

Escalation routing
- Pages go to SRE oncall (Rafael rotation) + Observability oncall if metric integrity suspected.
- If root cause points to routing/admission: page Mina/Elena rotation (eng-platform routing).
- If root cause points to capacity/provisioning: page Irene/Vanessa rotation (infra/fleet).


6) Change management gates (tie budget health to release risk)
We gate changes based on error budget health across:
- aggregate Dedicated throughput budget
- any Premium deployment budget
- any ongoing “budget watch” flagged by oncall

Change categories
- Cat 0: documentation-only / no runtime impact
- Cat 1: low-risk config/UI (no routing, no admission, no runtime)
- Cat 2: moderate risk (dashboards, billing metering, non-critical control plane)
- Cat 3: high risk (routing policy changes, admission control changes, runtime/kernel updates, shape pool changes, autoscaling policy)
- Cat 4: emergency mitigation (rollback/hotfix/capacity add)

Gating states (apply to Cat 2–Cat 3 changes)

STATE GREEN (normal)
Criteria (any one of):
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining >= 80% (rolling 30d)
- No Premium deployment budget remaining < 70%
Actions:
- Normal change process (standard approvals)
- Canaries required for Cat 3 (tier-aware routing/admission/runtime) per release checklist

STATE YELLOW (heightened caution)
Criteria:
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining between 80% and 60% OR
- Any Premium deployment budget remaining between 70% and 50% OR
- Any active burn-rate ticket (slow burn) without mitigation in place
Actions:
- Cat 3 changes require explicit SRE sign-off (Sean or Rafael delegate) + release lead sign-off (Connor)
- Must have rollback plan + defined success metrics + 30–60 min canary window
- Freeze “optional” Cat 3 changes that touch throughput-critical components (routing/admission/runtime) unless business critical

STATE ORANGE (pre-freeze)
Criteria:
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining between 60% and 40% OR
- Any Premium deployment budget remaining between 50% and 30% OR
- Any open incident with unresolved throughput regression
Actions:
- Cat 3 changes are paused by default
- Exceptions require: SRE owner + Infra lead (if capacity-related) + Product Dedicated (Lauren) approval
- Only allowed: Cat 4 mitigation changes, plus narrowly scoped fixes that demonstrably improve throughput SLO
- Require post-change verification report (before/after throughput floor adherence)

STATE RED (change freeze)
Criteria:
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining < 40% OR
- Any Premium deployment budget remaining < 30% OR
- Two or more Dedicated customers in breach simultaneously for > 60 minutes (regardless of remaining budget)
Actions:
- Freeze all Cat 3 changes affecting Dedicated traffic paths (routing/admission/runtime/autoscaling/capacity scheduling)
- Allowed changes:
  - Cat 4 mitigation only (rollbacks, revert configs, capacity add, emergency routing override)
  - Pre-approved compliance/security hotfixes with “no perf impact” validation
- Incident commander required for any exception
- Notify: Product Dedicated + CS lead + Release lead

Notes / intent
- This gating is intentionally conservative for Premium because Premium customers will ask for “in-writing” guarantees and expect high operational rigor.
- We expect to tune thresholds after 4–6 weeks of real data (especially after the streaming accounting fix rollout).


7) Incident thresholds (when does a throughput issue become an incident?)
Not all SLO burns warrant a formal incident. We start an incident when the risk to customer commitments is real and time-sensitive.

Start an incident when ANY is true:
- Burn-rate page triggered AND sustained throughput below floor for > 15 minutes for any Premium deployment
- Any Plus/Premium deployment is below floor for > 30 minutes and mitigation is not obvious (e.g., capacity saturation)
- Multiple deployments across a region/shape show correlated degradation (likely fleet-wide regression)
- Metric integrity issue that invalidates SLO reporting for Dedicated (e.g., double counting, missing labels) and blocks our ability to assess commitments

Severity guidance (rough)
- Sev1: multiple Premium customers in breach OR single Premium customer with critical business impact + no workaround OR broad fleet regression
- Sev2: single Premium or multiple Plus customers impacted with mitigation path (rollback/capacity/reroute)
- Sev3: Standard-only impact, localized, mitigated quickly, minimal commitment risk

CS/Customer comms hooks
- CS is notified when a customer’s Dedicated deployment is projected to exhaust budget within 7 days OR when a breach lasts > 60 minutes.
- Use Dedicated escalation playbook for comms templates + credit hooks (do not invent credit language in incident channel).


8) Required artifacts / operational hygiene
Dashboards (must exist for policy enforcement)
- Per-deployment: committed floor vs observed throughput (5m), remaining error budget, burn-rate, saturation signals
- Aggregate: total Dedicated burn by region/shape/model route + change correlation

Runbooks
- “Throughput below commitment” triage runbook must include:
  - metric integrity checks (streaming accounting sanity)
  - routing/admission control checks (queue caps, shedding)
  - capacity checks (GPU saturation, headroom, noisy neighbor)
  - mitigation options by tier (reroute to warm pool, capacity add, rollback)

Post-incident
- For any Sev1/Sev2 Dedicated throughput incident: write a short postmortem with:
  - impacted deployments + time below floor
  - error budget burned
  - immediate mitigation + long-term fix
  - any changes to exclusions / metric integrity learnings


9) Exception process (overriding change gates)
Sometimes we need to ship while budgets are low (security, legal, or mitigating fixes).

To override YELLOW/ORANGE/RED gates:
- Provide a written justification (in release ticket) including:
  - why change is required now
  - why it will not worsen throughput (or why it improves it)
  - rollout and rollback plan
  - verification plan (what dashboard, which deployments)
- Required approvals by state:
  - Yellow: SRE owner + Release lead
  - Orange: SRE owner + Release lead + Product Dedicated
  - Red: Incident commander + SRE owner + Release lead (+ Infra lead if capacity involved)


10) Open questions (need alignment)
- Final tier-specific deltas: do we want stricter gating for Premium only, or for all Dedicated when aggregate budget is low?
- How do we operationalize the “customer throttling” exclusion without creating a loophole or excessive manual adjudication?
- What is the contract-aligned definition of “warmup/grace period” for a new Dedicated deployment (and how do we label it in metrics)?
- Do we want separate budgets for model routes within a deployment (multi-model Dedicated) vs single deployment budget?


Appendix A: Practical examples
Example 1: A slow regression after a routing change
- Aggregate budget drops from 90% to 70% over 10 days (no obvious paging events).
- Policy outcome: move to YELLOW; Cat 3 changes require explicit SRE + Release signoff; prioritize investigation; pause non-essential routing work.

Example 2: Fast burn in one Premium customer
- Premium deployment is below floor for 25 minutes; burn-rate page fires.
- Policy outcome: start incident (Sev2 minimum); mitigation-first (reroute/capacity add/rollback); notify CS; freeze Cat 3 changes if budget is now ORANGE/RED.

Example 3: Metric integrity bug (streaming token double count)
- Budget appears to burn quickly across multiple deployments; investigation shows accounting bug.
- Policy outcome: incident for metric integrity if it blocks SLO reporting; mitigation is to fix/reprocess; do not apply customer-facing breach claims until validated.


Notes to self (Sean) / next edits
- After metric definition is finalized in Confluence, replace placeholder SLO text here with the exact wording and objective.
- After PR-19405 rolls out, confirm the actual burn-rate pair thresholds match the policy and update if needed.
- Coordinate with Connor on how the change freeze is communicated (release calendar + “gates” checklist).

- Release lead escalation: Connor O’Brien (change freeze enforcement)

Alert levels (recommended; align with ENG-19288 / PR-19405 thresholds)
A) Page (customer-impact likely / fast burn)

  1) Validate metric integrity (streaming token accounting, double count, missing labels).
  2) Identify impacted deployments/tier/region/shape.
  3) Start incident if impact is sustained > 15 minutes or multiple Premium/Plus customers affected.

B) Ticket (slow burn / risk accumulation)
- Trigger when burn rate >= 2x for 6h AND >= 1x for 3d.
- Required response: triage within 1 business day.
- Create an owner + mitigation plan (capacity add, rollback, config change, perf investigation).

C) Budget low early warning (change gating input)
- Trigger when remaining budget for any Premium deployment < 70% (or predicted exhaustion within 7 days at current burn).
- Create “budget watch” entry in SRE weekly review and notify Product Dedicated (Lauren) + CS lead (Rachel) if customer risk is non-trivial.

Escalation routing
- Pages go to SRE oncall (Rafael rotation) + Observability oncall if metric integrity suspected.
- If root cause points to routing/admission: page Mina/Elena rotation (eng-platform routing).
- If root cause points to capacity/provisioning: page Irene/Vanessa rotation (infra/fleet).


6) Change management gates (tie budget health to release risk)
We gate changes based on error budget health across:
- aggregate Dedicated throughput budget
- any Premium deployment budget
- any ongoing “budget watch” flagged by oncall

Change categories
- Cat 0: documentation-only / no runtime impact
- Cat 1: low-risk config/UI (no routing, no admission, no runtime)
- Cat 2: moderate risk (dashboards, billing metering, non-critical control plane)
- Cat 3: high risk (routing policy changes, admission control changes, runtime/kernel updates, shape pool changes, autoscaling policy)
- Cat 4: emergency mitigation (rollback/hotfix/capacity add)

Gating states (apply to Cat 2–Cat 3 changes)

STATE GREEN (normal)
Criteria (any one of):
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining >= 80% (rolling 30d)
- No Premium deployment budget remaining < 70%
Actions:
- Normal change process (standard approvals)
- Canaries required for Cat 3 (tier-aware routing/admission/runtime) per release checklist

STATE YELLOW (heightened caution)
Criteria:
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining between 80% and 60% OR
- Any Premium deployment budget remaining between 70% and 50% OR
- Any active burn-rate ticket (slow burn) without mitigation in place
Actions:
- Cat 3 changes require explicit SRE sign-off (Sean or Rafael delegate) + release lead sign-off (Connor)
- Must have rollback plan + defined success metrics + 30–60 min canary window
- Freeze “optional” Cat 3 changes that touch throughput-critical components (routing/admission/runtime) unless business critical

STATE ORANGE (pre-freeze)
Criteria:
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining between 60% and 40% OR
- Any Premium deployment budget remaining between 50% and 30% OR
- Any open incident with unresolved throughput regression
Actions:
- Cat 3 changes are paused by default
- Exceptions require: SRE owner + Infra lead (if capacity-related) + Product Dedicated (Lauren) approval
- Only allowed: Cat 4 mitigation changes, plus narrowly scoped fixes that demonstrably improve throughput SLO

Criteria:
- Aggregate Dedicated budget remaining < 40% OR
- Any Premium deployment budget remaining < 30% OR
- Two or more Dedicated customers in breach simultaneously for > 60 minutes (regardless of remaining budget)
Actions:
- Freeze all Cat 3 changes affecting Dedicated traffic paths (routing/admission/runtime/autoscaling/capacity scheduling)
- Allowed changes:
  - Cat 4 mitigation only (rollbacks, revert configs, capacity add, emergency routing override)

- Incident commander required for any exception
- Notify: Product Dedicated + CS lead + Release lead

- This gating is intentionally conservative for Premium because Premium customers will ask for “in-writing” guarantees and expect high operational rigor.
- We expect to tune thresholds after 4–6 weeks of real data (especially after the streaming accounting fix rollout).


7) Incident thresholds (when does a throughput issue become an incident?)
Not all SLO burns warrant a formal incident. We start an incident when the risk to customer commitments is real and time-sensitive.

Start an incident when ANY is true:
- Burn-rate page triggered AND sustained throughput below floor for > 15 minutes for any Premium deployment
- Any Plus/Premium deployment is below floor for > 30 minutes and mitigation is not obvious (e.g., capacity saturation)
- Multiple deployments across a region/shape show correlated degradation (likely fleet-wide regression)
- Metric integrity issue that invalidates SLO reporting for Dedicated (e.g., double counting, missing labels) and blocks our ability to assess commitments

Severity guidance (rough)
- Sev1: multiple Premium customers in breach OR single Premium customer with critical business impact + no workaround OR broad fleet regression
- Sev2: single Premium or multiple Plus customers impacted with mitigation path (rollback/capacity/reroute)
- Sev3: Standard-only impact, localized, mitigated quickly, minimal commitment risk

- CS is notified when a customer’s Dedicated deployment is projected to exhaust budget within 7 days OR when a breach lasts > 60 minutes.
- Use Dedicated escalation playbook for comms templates + credit hooks (do not invent credit language in incident channel).


8) Required artifacts / operational hygiene
Dashboards (must exist for policy enforcement)

- Aggregate: total Dedicated burn by region/shape/model route + change correlation

Runbooks

Post-incident
- For any Sev1/Sev2 Dedicated throughput incident: write a short postmortem with:
  - impacted deployments + time below floor

9) Exception process (overriding change gates)
Sometimes we need to ship while budgets are low (security, legal, or mitigating fixes).

To override YELLOW/ORANGE/RED gates:
- Provide a written justification (in release ticket) including:
  - why change is required now
  - why it will not worsen throughput (or why it improves it)
  - rollout and rollback plan
  - verification plan (what dashboard, which deployments)
- Required approvals by state:
  - Yellow: SRE owner + Release lead
  - Orange: SRE owner + Release lead + Product Dedicated
  - Red: Incident commander + SRE owner + Release lead (+ Infra lead if capacity involved)


10) Open questions (need alignment)
- Final tier-specific deltas: do we want stricter gating for Premium only, or for all Dedicated when aggregate budget is low?
- How do we operationalize the “customer throttling” exclusion without creating a loophole or excessive manual adjudication?
- What is the contract-aligned definition of “warmup/grace period” for a new Dedicated deployment (and how do we label it in metrics)?
- Do we want separate budgets for model routes within a deployment (multi-model Dedicated) vs single deployment budget?


Appendix A: Practical examples
Example 1: A slow regression after a routing change
- Aggregate budget drops from 90% to 70% over 10 days (no obvious paging events).

title: Residency Enforcement — Region Mapping + Failover Groups (Hosted/Dedicated)
preview:
Owner: Infrastructure/SRE (Sean)
Last sanity check: 2025-03-01

Purpose
- Single place to map:
  (1) cloud regions (public IDs) -> Redwood internal region IDs
  (2) residency “zones” used in policy config + routing
  (3) default failover groups used by smart routing
  (4) which platform services are allowed per residency tier

Important notes (please read)
- Internal region IDs are stable identifiers used in routing logs, residency decision context, and policy objects.
- “Residency zone” is the customer-facing grouping used for allowlists (e.g., EU, US, APAC). For v1 we keep zones coarse, and the actual enforcement is at *region* granularity.
- Failover behavior for pinned traffic is constrained by ADR-021:
  - Cross-region fallback is disabled by default.
  - Fallback is only allowed to *other allowed regions* in the policy.
- Any service that handles “customer content” MUST be region-local for pinned traffic (see ADR-021). Global pipelines may only receive the allowed metadata fields.


SHEET 1 — Region Catalog (Hosted + Dedicated)
Columns:
- public_region_code: public cloud region
- cloud: provider
- metro: human label
- internal_region_id: Redwood region identifier
- residency_zone: policy grouping
- hosted_inference: whether Hosted API executes inference here
- dedicated_supported: whether Dedicated capacity can be provisioned here (as of last_modified)
- default_failover_group: used when customer has no pinning, or has a multi-region allowlist
- router_weight_profile: default traffic shaping profile (rough)
- notes

| public_region_code | cloud | metro | internal_region_id | residency_zone | hosted_inference | dedicated_supported | default_failover_group | router_weight_profile | notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| us-east-1 | aws | US East (N. Virginia) | rwd-us-1 | US | yes | yes | fg-us-a | default-us-primary | Primary control-plane adjacency; highest capacity. |
| us-west-2 | aws | US West (Oregon) | rwd-us-2 | US | yes | yes | fg-us-a | default-us-secondary | Used for west-coast latency + regional resiliency. |
| eu-central-1 | aws | EU Central (Frankfurt) | rwd-eu-1 | EU | yes | yes | fg-eu-a | default-eu-primary | Default EU primary. |
| eu-west-1 | aws | EU West (Dublin) | rwd-eu-2 | EU | yes | limited | fg-eu-a | default-eu-secondary | Dedicated is limited (quota-bound); Hosted OK. |
| ap-southeast-1 | aws | APAC SE (Singapore) | rwd-ap-1 | APAC | yes | limited | fg-apac-a | default-apac-primary | Primary APAC today; some model SKUs not available. |
| ap-northeast-1 | aws | APAC NE (Tokyo) | rwd-ap-2 | APAC | yes | limited | fg-apac-a | default-apac-secondary | Secondary APAC; higher cost. |

Legend / operational definitions
- hosted_inference=yes means: gateway + router + inference workers + content caches exist in-region.
- dedicated_supported=limited means: available for renewals/expansions only; coordinate with Capacity Planning.


SHEET 2 — Failover Groups (Smart Routing)
These are the *logical* groups that routing uses for non-pinned traffic and for pinned traffic when the allowlist includes multiple regions within the same zone.

Columns:
- failover_group_id: internal
- members_internal_region_id: ordered list (primary -> secondary)
- residency_zone
- cross_zone_allowed: should always be “no” for v1 defaults
- notes

| failover_group_id | members_internal_region_id | residency_zone | cross_zone_allowed | notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| fg-us-a | rwd-us-1, rwd-us-2 | US | no | Default US failover pair. |
| fg-eu-a | rwd-eu-1, rwd-eu-2 | EU | no | Default EU failover pair. |
| fg-apac-a | rwd-ap-1, rwd-ap-2 | APAC | no | Default APAC failover pair. |

Routing rules summary (how this is used)
- Non-pinned traffic:
  - Router can pick any region using global load/capacity policies and failover groups.
- Pinned traffic:
  - Router must pick a region that is explicitly allowed by the policy.
  - If primary is unhealthy/capacity-constrained, router may try secondary ONLY if secondary is also in allowed_regions.
  - Router must not “fail open” into another zone.


SHEET 3 — Residency Tiers (Policy) -> Service Permissions
This is a simplified matrix used by Infra/SRE + security reviews to confirm which services must be region-local vs which may be centralized.

Tier names here are operational shorthand (the control plane may encode this as policy fields like allowed_regions + primary_region).

Columns:
- service: platform subsystem
- handles_customer_content: per ADR-021 definition
- allowed_for_global (no pinning)
- allowed_for_pinned_single_region
- allowed_for_pinned_multi_region
- notes

| service | handles_customer_content | allowed_for_global (no pinning) | allowed_for_pinned_single_region | allowed_for_pinned_multi_region | notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| api-gateway (edge/regional) | yes | yes | yes (MUST be region-local) | yes (MUST be region-local) | Gateway must enforce + attach residency decision context. |
| smart-routing | yes | yes | yes (MUST route only within allowed region) | yes (MUST route only within allowed regions) | Cross-region fallback disabled unless within allowlist. |
| inference workers | yes | yes | yes (region-local) | yes (region-local, per selected region) | Includes streaming token emission path. |
| prefix/KV cache | yes | yes | yes (region-local) | yes (region-local per region) | No cross-region replication for v1. |
| prompt/semantic cache | yes | yes | yes (region-local) | yes (region-local) | Cache keys must not contain raw content. |
| request/response body logging | yes | sometimes | NO (must be disabled or region-local sink) | NO (must be disabled or region-local sink) | For pinned: metadata-only logs outside region. |
| tracing (spans) | sometimes | yes | yes (metadata-only cross-region) | yes (metadata-only cross-region) | No body capture; span attrs must follow allowlist. |
| metrics (counters/histos) | no (by design) | yes | yes | yes | Central aggregation OK if schemas prevent content. |
| billing/usage aggregation | no (by design) | yes | yes | yes | Token counts OK; no payload sampling for pinned. |
| audit logs (policy changes) | no | yes | yes | yes | Centralized OK; must never include content. |
| support tooling: request replay/debug capture | yes | yes | NO (unless strictly in-region + access-controlled) | NO (unless strictly in-region + access-controlled) | Treat replay queues as content. |


SHEET 4 — Region-local vs Global Endpoints (Implementation Reality Check)
This is a quick “where does the traffic go” cheat sheet for on-call / reviews.

Columns:
- subsystem
- region_local_component
- global_component
- pinned_traffic_behavior

| subsystem | region_local_component | global_component | pinned_traffic_behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Request handling | gateway+routing+workers in selected region | none | All customer content stays in selected/allowed region(s). |
| Telemetry | region log sinks (structured, metadata-only for pinned) | global telemetry aggregation (metadata-only) | Only allowed metadata can leave region (ADR-021 allowlist). |
| Config/control plane | none required for data plane | control plane APIs + DB (central) | Policy objects can be stored centrally (no content). |
| Artifact storage | regional buckets/volumes for any content captures | global buckets (metadata-only) | Content captures prohibited for pinned unless region-local + approved. |


SHEET 5 — Internal Identifiers / Naming Conventions
- internal_region_id format: rwd-{zone}-{n}
  - US: rwd-us-1 (us-east-1), rwd-us-2 (us-west-2)
  - EU: rwd-eu-1 (eu-central-1), rwd-eu-2 (eu-west-1)
  - APAC: rwd-ap-1 (ap-southeast-1), rwd-ap-2 (ap-northeast-1)
- failover_group_id format: fg-{zone}-{letter}


TODO / Open items (Infra)
- Confirm Dedicated support status for rwd-eu-2, rwd-ap-1, rwd-ap-2 for Q2 capacity plan (currently marked limited).
- Add rows for any new Hosted regions before GA announcements (do not let Docs publish region list before this sheet is updated).
- Add explicit mapping for internal “region shard” IDs used by metrics pipelines (needs Logan + Allison review) — keep metadata-only.

Change log
- 2025-02-03: Initial version created for residency enforcement rollout.
- 2025-02-19: Added service permission matrix + clarified pinned behavior.
- 2025-03-01: Marked Dedicated support as limited in EU West + APAC pending capacity review.
