title: Restore Validation Test Plan (Redwood Private Day-2 Backup/Restore)
preview:
Context / why this doc exists
- We need a repeatable, supportable way to prove that a given Redwood Private deployment can be restored from backup reliably.
- This is NOT just “restore completed successfully” — it’s deterministic state correctness + a set of signals SRE/Support can use to confidently sign off.
- This plan is used for:
  (1) engineering validation during development
  (2) periodic restore drills in staging
  (3) customer acceptance / Solutions-led validation during onboarding for enterprise Private accounts

Owner + reviewers
- Owner: Sean Gallagher (SRE lead, runbooks/on-call)
- Reviewers: Ethan Park (control plane), Hanae Suzuki (KMS/secrets), Allison Grant + Sergio Costa (observability), Fatima Noor (on-prem), Colin O’Donnell (VPC)

Goals
1) Define restore validation test cases with expected outcomes.
2) Enumerate failure injection ideas (what to break, why, and what we should observe).
3) Define minimum observability signals (metrics/logs/traces/events) required to make failures actionable.
4) Provide acceptance criteria for “restore readiness” per supported deployment archetype.

Non-goals / out of scope
- We do not validate customer app-level state (application DBs, user data) beyond Redwood control-plane and supporting platform requirements.
- We do not certify arbitrary K8s backup tools (Velero, etc.) as the primary restore mechanism; if used, they must not conflict with Redwood’s own snapshot/restore.
- We do not cover full etcd disaster recovery for the underlying Kubernetes cluster beyond sanity checks and “when to use etcd snapshot restore vs app-level restore” guidance.

Definitions
- Backup artifact: manifest + encrypted payload produced by installer tooling (see installer `backup create`).
- Restore workflow: installer `restore apply` (or Helm hook/job equivalent) to bootstrap services in correct order and apply state.
- Control plane state (high-level): configuration DB, tenant/routing/policy config, integration refs (KMS, object store), secrets references (NOT raw secret material unless explicitly configured; see ADR-027).
- Restore validation: post-restore checks that prove the platform is usable for intended purpose.

Test environments
We validate across 4 archetypes (minimum):
A) VPC single-region, single-cluster (baseline)
B) VPC single-region, multi-AZ (baseline + infra assumptions)
C) On-prem connected (restricted egress allowed, but not fully air-gapped)
D) On-prem air-gapped (no outbound; offline bundles only; manual artifact transfer)

Storage backends to cover (per archetype where relevant)
- S3 (+ KMS), with least-privilege IAM
- NFS (mounted to backup job + restore job)
- GCS (if/where supported; deprioritized unless customer requirement)

Pre-flight prerequisites / setup
- Known-good deployment with:
  - at least 2 tenants (to catch multi-tenant restore regressions)
  - at least 2 routing policies (e.g., primary + fallback)
  - at least 1 model version pin + 1 rollout config (canary/A-B) if supported in Private
  - audit log exporter enabled if customer uses it
- A “golden” prompt set for smoke tests (see Validation section) with deterministic prompts.
- Snapshot taken during a defined “quiesce window” (or defined consistency guarantees if no quiesce).
- Observability pack installed for test runs (dashboards + alerts). If not installed, minimum metrics/logs must still be emitted.

Acceptance criteria (high-level)
- RTO: restore + validation completes within the archetype target window (see RPO/RTO matrix doc; this plan only enforces relative constraints).
- RPO: restored state reflects a point-in-time consistent snapshot (no partial tenant state, no broken foreign key relationships, no missing routing policy objects).
- Determinism: restore of the same artifact into a clean cluster yields the same restored state (within expected non-deterministic fields like timestamps).
- Security: artifact encryption verified; failure modes do not leak plaintext secrets to logs.
- Observability: on failure, we can identify (a) which step failed, (b) why, (c) recommended operator action.

Test matrix (summary)
Each test case includes: setup, steps, expected result, and required signals.

| ID | Category | Archetype(s) | Storage | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RV-01 | Golden path restore to clean cluster | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-02 | Restore into existing cluster (brownfield) | A/B/C | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-03 | Artifact integrity / checksum mismatch | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-04 | Wrong KMS key / decrypt failure | A/B/C/D | S3 | P0 |
| RV-05 | KMS throttle/timeout during restore | A/B/C | S3 | P1 |
| RV-06 | Partial/corrupt payload (truncate) | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-07 | Missing CRDs / wrong install order | B/C/D | any | P0 |
| RV-08 | RBAC denied to read/write backup location | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-09 | Clock skew (cert validity + signed bundle) | C/D | NFS/local | P1 |
| RV-10 | Air-gapped offline import/export flow | D | local/NFS | P0 |
| RV-11 | Restore validation smoke tests (API) | A/B/C/D | n/a | P0 |
| RV-12 | Restore validation smoke tests (control-plane UI/status) | A/B/C | n/a | P1 |
| RV-13 | Audit log export continuity + retention config restored | A/B/C/D | any | P1 |
| RV-14 | Large state scaling (perf) | A/B | S3 | P2 |

Detailed test cases

RV-01: Golden path restore to clean cluster
- Purpose: baseline end-to-end success.
- Setup:
  - Take a backup from a stable deployment at T0.
  - Provision a clean target cluster (same version, same chart/installer version).
- Steps:
  1) `restore apply` with artifact URI/path
  2) Wait for restore workflow completion
  3) Run validation hook/tooling (post-restore checks)
- Expected:
  - Restore completes with exit code 0
  - Validation report shows PASS for all P0 checks
  - Tenants/routing policies/model pins match source snapshot
  - System is ready for traffic (control-plane APIs healthy)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_success{artifact_id,cluster_id}=1
  - Metric: restore_duration_seconds (p50/p95 tracked)
  - Log: structured step events (download, decrypt, unpack, db-restore, migrations, apply-config, post-checks)
  - Trace: parent span `restore.apply` with child spans per step
  - Event: `RestoreCompleted` with artifact_id, format_version, source_version

RV-02: Restore into existing cluster (brownfield)
- Purpose: support “restore to new namespace” or “replace state in place” patterns; catches resource collisions.
- Setup:
  - Existing cluster has Redwood Private installed (same version).
  - Optionally a second namespace with non-Redwood workloads to ensure we don’t overreach.
- Steps:
  1) Attempt restore with “in-place” mode (if supported) OR documented procedure
  2) Confirm pre-flight detection of conflicts
- Expected:
  - Either:
    - Safe in-place restore succeeds, OR
    - Restore halts with a clear, actionable error and no partial state applied
  - No orphaned Jobs/Pods left running with access to backup credentials
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_preflight_failed_total{reason="resource_conflict"}
  - Log: lists conflicting objects (namespaced) without dumping secret env

RV-03: Artifact integrity / checksum mismatch
- Purpose: ensure we don’t restore from tampered/corrupted artifacts.
- Failure injection:
  - Edit manifest checksum OR flip a few bytes in payload.
- Steps:
  1) Run restore
- Expected:
  - Restore fails before attempting to apply state
  - Clear error: “checksum mismatch” and recommended remediation (“re-copy artifact; verify offline transfer; re-run backup”)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="integrity_check_failed"}
  - Log: include artifact_id + expected/observed hash (hashes OK; no plaintext payload)

RV-04: Wrong KMS key / decrypt failure
- Purpose: validate envelope encryption behavior and error messaging.
- Failure injection:
  - Point restore to a KMS key that cannot decrypt (wrong key ID or policy denies decrypt).
- Expected:
  - Restore fails in decrypt step; no unpack attempted
  - Error explicitly indicates decrypt denied vs key not found vs bad ciphertext
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="decrypt_failed"}
  - Metric: kms_decrypt_error_total{code}
  - Log: sanitized KMS error code + request id; no key material

RV-05: KMS throttle/timeout during restore
- Purpose: ensure retry/backoff and proper operator guidance.
- Failure injection:
  - Apply KMS rate limit or simulate network delay.
- Expected:
  - Retries follow policy; if max retries exceeded, failure is explicit
  - If KMS unavailable, the runbook suggests when to retry vs escalate
- Required signals:
  - Metric: kms_decrypt_retry_total
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="kms_unavailable"}
  - Trace: child spans show time spent waiting/retrying

RV-06: Partial/corrupt payload (truncate)
- Purpose: catch cases where object storage returned partial data or NFS copy was incomplete.
- Failure injection:
  - Truncate the artifact file mid-stream.
- Expected:
  - Failure occurs during unpack/format validation
  - No partial DB restore committed (transactional where possible)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="payload_corrupt"}
  - Log: include step + actionable message (“re-transfer artifact; verify size matches manifest”)

RV-07: Missing CRDs / wrong install order
- Purpose: regression coverage for known on-prem issues (restore attempted before CRDs installed).
- Failure injection:
  - Remove a required CRD from target cluster OR install chart without CRDs.
- Expected:
  - Pre-flight detects missing CRDs and fails fast
  - Error includes exact CRD names and remediation (“apply CRDs from release bundle version X”)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_preflight_failed_total{reason="missing_crds"}
  - Log: missing CRD list + detected chart/installer version

RV-08: RBAC denied to read/write backup location
- Purpose: catch common misconfig: SA missing permissions, IAM policy too strict.
- Failure injection:
  - Remove get/list permissions for S3 prefix OR NFS mount read-only.
- Expected:
  - Failure in fetch step with precise permission error
  - Runbook hints: least-privilege policy snippet + where to apply
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="permission_denied"}
  - Log: include backend (s3/nfs), operation (GetObject/List), and principal/SA name

RV-09: Clock skew (cert validity + signed bundle)
- Purpose: air-gapped is often time-drifted; signing and TLS validation can break.
- Failure injection:
  - Shift node clock +/- 30 min (in lab)
- Expected:
  - If signature verification depends on time validity, error message should mention clock skew
  - TLS failures should be diagnosable (expired/not-yet-valid)
- Required signals:
  - Log: explicit detection when system time differs from NTP/expected (if available)
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="time_invalid"} (optional; good to have)

RV-10: Air-gapped offline import/export flow
- Purpose: prove restore works without outbound calls.
- Setup:
  - Use offline bundle containing:
    - installer binary
    - checksum manifest
    - signature file
    - docs/runbook snapshot
  - Backup artifact transferred via removable media / offline copy
- Steps:
  1) Verify bundle signature/checksums locally
  2) Import artifact to local path
  3) Run restore with “offline” mode (no remote fetch)
- Expected:
  - No outbound network attempted (or explicitly documented allowed endpoints = none)
  - Restore succeeds and validation passes
- Required signals:
  - Log: “offline mode enabled” banner
  - Metric: restore_mode{mode="offline"}=1 (or included as label)
  - Optional: network policy audit showing no egress from restore job

RV-11: Post-restore smoke tests (API)
- Purpose: verify platform is functionally usable (minimum viable checks).
- Steps (minimum):
  - Health endpoints:
    - control-plane API /healthz returns OK
    - any required internal services report ready
  - State correctness checks:
    - list tenants => expected count and IDs
    - fetch routing policies => expected names/IDs and fallback configuration
    - verify model catalog pins/versions (whatever is supported in Private control plane)
  - Auth check:
    - admin auth works; tenant-scoped auth works; no cross-tenant leakage
- Expected:
  - All checks pass and are captured as a machine-readable validation report artifact
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_validation_pass{check_id}=1, restore_validation_fail{check_id}=0
  - Artifact: `validation-report.json` stored alongside restore logs

RV-12: Post-restore smoke tests (console/status endpoints)
- Purpose: ensure operators can see restore readiness in UI/automation.
- Steps:
  - Query backup/restore status endpoint (if enabled)
  - Confirm last restore timestamp, artifact_id, and validation result are surfaced
- Expected:
  - UI/API shows accurate status; no stale “last_success” values
- Required signals:
  - Endpoint: GET /private/backup-status (or equivalent) returns last_success_ts, last_restore_ts, last_validation

RV-13: Audit log export continuity + retention config restored
- Purpose: regulated customers (MedData-type) care about evidence + continuity.
- Steps:
  - Restore, then confirm audit log exporter configuration present
  - Run exporter in “batch export” mode (if configured) to confirm it can read from restored config
- Expected:
  - Retention/export settings match snapshot
  - Export can run without permission errors
- Required signals:
  - Metric: audit_export_success_total
  - Log: exporter includes retention window + pagination stats (no PII in logs)

RV-14: Large state scaling (perf)
- Purpose: ensure restore doesn’t degrade catastrophically with size.
- Setup:
  - Seed config DB with ~N tenants and policies (synthetic) to represent large enterprise.
- Expected:
  - Restore completes within acceptable time and resource usage.
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_peak_memory_bytes, restore_cpu_seconds (if available via job metrics)
  - Metric: restore_duration_seconds

Failure injection ideas (additional)
- Storage backend specific
  - S3: inject 403 on ListObjects; inject slow GET responses; object versioning mismatch; wrong region endpoint.
  - NFS: simulate stale file handles; permission mismatch (root_squash); throughput drop.
- Kubernetes / platform
  - Kill restore Job pod mid-run; ensure idempotency / safe retry behavior.
  - Force node eviction; ensure partial state not left.
  - Change image pull policy / registry unavailable (air-gapped registry errors).
- Data
  - Restore from older artifact format version (compat check should block or migrate with clear output).
  - Introduce migration failure (simulate with incompatible DB schema) and confirm rollback guidance.

Observability requirements (minimum set)
Metrics (names are illustrative; final names should align to ENG-9826 / PR-24344 conventions)
- backup_success (already tracked elsewhere, but needed for readiness)
- restore_success (1/0)
- restore_failed_total{reason}
- restore_duration_seconds{phase} (or separate phase timers)
- restore_validation_pass{check_id}
- restore_validation_fail{check_id,error_class}
- kms_decrypt_error_total{code}
- kms_decrypt_retry_total
- restore_bytes_total (download/unpack sizes)

Logs
- Structured JSON logs with fields:
  - artifact_id, artifact_format_version
  - source_platform_version, target_platform_version
  - step_name, step_status, duration_ms
  - error_class, error_message (sanitized), remediation_hint
- Explicit redaction policy:
  - never log plaintext secrets, decrypted payload, tokens
  - avoid logging full config objects; log IDs/counts only

Traces
- Single top-level trace per restore with child spans per step:
  - fetch, decrypt, verify, unpack, db_restore, migrations, apply_config, post_checks

Artifacts
- validation report (machine-readable) stored next to restore logs
- optionally: “restore plan” file that lists intended steps and detected environment details (helps Support)

Automation + cadence
- Staging automated restore drill: target weekly (P1), with ability to ratchet to daily once stable.
- On-prem/air-gapped drill: monthly in lab (manual steps + offline media simulation).
- Report to:
  - Slack channel (eng-oncall / eng-sre) with summary: PASS/FAIL, duration, top reasons
  - Create/append a ticket automatically on failure (ENG-9827 scope)

Exit criteria for Phase 2 (what we need before calling this “ready”)
- RV-01, 03, 04, 06, 07, 08, 10, 11 pass in A + D archetypes.
- At least one failure injection test per category produces a clear error with remediation steps (no “unknown error”).
- Dashboard exists for:
  - last restore drill result
  - restore duration trend
  - top failure reasons
- On-call runbook updated with:
  - how to rerun restore drill
  - where to find validation report
  - paging policy / thresholds

Known gaps / open questions
- Do we require quiesce for backups to be “consistent” or do we document eventual consistency guarantees per datastore?
- For brownfield restores: do we officially support in-place restore, or only “restore into clean cluster”? (Current guidance leans clean cluster for DR.)
- Where do we persist validation reports in air-gapped environments (NFS path vs local disk) and how long?
- Final list of P0 validation checks (need sign-off from Ethan + Vivek): tenants, routing, model pins, auth, audit exporter config.

Notes from DR rehearsal (2025-02-14) to incorporate
- One failure mode observed: restore attempted before CRDs installed; resulted in confusing kubectl errors. This is why RV-07 is P0.
- Another: RBAC for backup CronJob was too permissive in one env and too restrictive in another; we should validate least-privilege but with explicit error messaging (RV-08).

Appendix: quick validation checklist (operator-friendly)
- [ ] Restore run completed; exit code 0
- [ ] validation-report.json exists and all P0 checks PASS
- [ ] tenants list matches expected IDs (sample 2 tenants)
- [ ] routing policies present + fallback enabled as expected
- [ ] auth works (admin + tenant scoped)
- [ ] backup/restore metrics visible in dashboard
- [ ] no pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff; no failed hooks left behind
text:
Context / why this doc exists
- We need a repeatable, supportable way to prove that a given Redwood Private deployment can be restored from backup reliably.
- This is NOT just “restore completed successfully” — it’s deterministic state correctness + a set of signals SRE/Support can use to confidently sign off.
- This plan is used for:
  (1) engineering validation during development
  (2) periodic restore drills in staging
  (3) customer acceptance / Solutions-led validation during onboarding for enterprise Private accounts

Owner + reviewers
- Owner: Sean Gallagher (SRE lead, runbooks/on-call)
- Reviewers: Ethan Park (control plane), Hanae Suzuki (KMS/secrets), Allison Grant + Sergio Costa (observability), Fatima Noor (on-prem), Colin O’Donnell (VPC)

Goals
1) Define restore validation test cases with expected outcomes.
2) Enumerate failure injection ideas (what to break, why, and what we should observe).
3) Define minimum observability signals (metrics/logs/traces/events) required to make failures actionable.
4) Provide acceptance criteria for “restore readiness” per supported deployment archetype.

Non-goals / out of scope
- We do not validate customer app-level state (application DBs, user data) beyond Redwood control-plane and supporting platform requirements.
- We do not certify arbitrary K8s backup tools (Velero, etc.) as the primary restore mechanism; if used, they must not conflict with Redwood’s own snapshot/restore.
- We do not cover full etcd disaster recovery for the underlying Kubernetes cluster beyond sanity checks and “when to use etcd snapshot restore vs app-level restore” guidance.

Definitions
- Backup artifact: manifest + encrypted payload produced by installer tooling (see installer `backup create`).
- Restore workflow: installer `restore apply` (or Helm hook/job equivalent) to bootstrap services in correct order and apply state.
- Control plane state (high-level): configuration DB, tenant/routing/policy config, integration refs (KMS, object store), secrets references (NOT raw secret material unless explicitly configured; see ADR-027).
- Restore validation: post-restore checks that prove the platform is usable for intended purpose.

Test environments
We validate across 4 archetypes (minimum):
A) VPC single-region, single-cluster (baseline)
B) VPC single-region, multi-AZ (baseline + infra assumptions)
C) On-prem connected (restricted egress allowed, but not fully air-gapped)
D) On-prem air-gapped (no outbound; offline bundles only; manual artifact transfer)

Storage backends to cover (per archetype where relevant)
- S3 (+ KMS), with least-privilege IAM
- NFS (mounted to backup job + restore job)
- GCS (if/where supported; deprioritized unless customer requirement)

Pre-flight prerequisites / setup
- Known-good deployment with:
  - at least 2 tenants (to catch multi-tenant restore regressions)
  - at least 2 routing policies (e.g., primary + fallback)
  - at least 1 model version pin + 1 rollout config (canary/A-B) if supported in Private
  - audit log exporter enabled if customer uses it
- A “golden” prompt set for smoke tests (see Validation section) with deterministic prompts.
- Snapshot taken during a defined “quiesce window” (or defined consistency guarantees if no quiesce).
- Observability pack installed for test runs (dashboards + alerts). If not installed, minimum metrics/logs must still be emitted.

Acceptance criteria (high-level)
- RTO: restore + validation completes within the archetype target window (see RPO/RTO matrix doc; this plan only enforces relative constraints).
- RPO: restored state reflects a point-in-time consistent snapshot (no partial tenant state, no broken foreign key relationships, no missing routing policy objects).
- Determinism: restore of the same artifact into a clean cluster yields the same restored state (within expected non-deterministic fields like timestamps).
- Security: artifact encryption verified; failure modes do not leak plaintext secrets to logs.
- Observability: on failure, we can identify (a) which step failed, (b) why, (c) recommended operator action.

Test matrix (summary)
Each test case includes: setup, steps, expected result, and required signals.

| ID | Category | Archetype(s) | Storage | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RV-01 | Golden path restore to clean cluster | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-02 | Restore into existing cluster (brownfield) | A/B/C | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-03 | Artifact integrity / checksum mismatch | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-04 | Wrong KMS key / decrypt failure | A/B/C/D | S3 | P0 |
| RV-05 | KMS throttle/timeout during restore | A/B/C | S3 | P1 |
| RV-06 | Partial/corrupt payload (truncate) | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-07 | Missing CRDs / wrong install order | B/C/D | any | P0 |
| RV-08 | RBAC denied to read/write backup location | A/B/C/D | S3/NFS | P0 |
| RV-09 | Clock skew (cert validity + signed bundle) | C/D | NFS/local | P1 |
| RV-10 | Air-gapped offline import/export flow | D | local/NFS | P0 |
| RV-11 | Restore validation smoke tests (API) | A/B/C/D | n/a | P0 |
| RV-12 | Restore validation smoke tests (control-plane UI/status) | A/B/C | n/a | P1 |
| RV-13 | Audit log export continuity + retention config restored | A/B/C/D | any | P1 |
| RV-14 | Large state scaling (perf) | A/B | S3 | P2 |

Detailed test cases

RV-01: Golden path restore to clean cluster
- Purpose: baseline end-to-end success.
- Setup:
  - Take a backup from a stable deployment at T0.
  - Provision a clean target cluster (same version, same chart/installer version).
- Steps:
  1) `restore apply` with artifact URI/path
  2) Wait for restore workflow completion
  3) Run validation hook/tooling (post-restore checks)
- Expected:
  - Restore completes with exit code 0
  - Validation report shows PASS for all P0 checks
  - Tenants/routing policies/model pins match source snapshot
  - System is ready for traffic (control-plane APIs healthy)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_success{artifact_id,cluster_id}=1
  - Metric: restore_duration_seconds (p50/p95 tracked)
  - Log: structured step events (download, decrypt, unpack, db-restore, migrations, apply-config, post-checks)
  - Trace: parent span `restore.apply` with child spans per step
  - Event: `RestoreCompleted` with artifact_id, format_version, source_version

RV-02: Restore into existing cluster (brownfield)
- Purpose: support “restore to new namespace” or “replace state in place” patterns; catches resource collisions.
- Setup:
  - Existing cluster has Redwood Private installed (same version).
  - Optionally a second namespace with non-Redwood workloads to ensure we don’t overreach.
- Steps:
  1) Attempt restore with “in-place” mode (if supported) OR documented procedure
  2) Confirm pre-flight detection of conflicts
- Expected:
  - Either:
    - Safe in-place restore succeeds, OR
    - Restore halts with a clear, actionable error and no partial state applied
  - No orphaned Jobs/Pods left running with access to backup credentials
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_preflight_failed_total{reason="resource_conflict"}
  - Log: lists conflicting objects (namespaced) without dumping secret env

RV-03: Artifact integrity / checksum mismatch
- Purpose: ensure we don’t restore from tampered/corrupted artifacts.
- Failure injection:
  - Edit manifest checksum OR flip a few bytes in payload.
- Steps:
  1) Run restore
- Expected:
  - Restore fails before attempting to apply state
  - Clear error: “checksum mismatch” and recommended remediation (“re-copy artifact; verify offline transfer; re-run backup”)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="integrity_check_failed"}
  - Log: include artifact_id + expected/observed hash (hashes OK; no plaintext payload)

RV-04: Wrong KMS key / decrypt failure
- Purpose: validate envelope encryption behavior and error messaging.
- Failure injection:
  - Point restore to a KMS key that cannot decrypt (wrong key ID or policy denies decrypt).
- Expected:
  - Restore fails in decrypt step; no unpack attempted
  - Error explicitly indicates decrypt denied vs key not found vs bad ciphertext
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="decrypt_failed"}
  - Metric: kms_decrypt_error_total{code}
  - Log: sanitized KMS error code + request id; no key material

RV-05: KMS throttle/timeout during restore
- Purpose: ensure retry/backoff and proper operator guidance.
- Failure injection:
  - Apply KMS rate limit or simulate network delay.
- Expected:
  - Retries follow policy; if max retries exceeded, failure is explicit
  - If KMS unavailable, the runbook suggests when to retry vs escalate
- Required signals:
  - Metric: kms_decrypt_retry_total
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="kms_unavailable"}
  - Trace: child spans show time spent waiting/retrying

RV-06: Partial/corrupt payload (truncate)
- Purpose: catch cases where object storage returned partial data or NFS copy was incomplete.
- Failure injection:
  - Truncate the artifact file mid-stream.
- Expected:
  - Failure occurs during unpack/format validation
  - No partial DB restore committed (transactional where possible)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="payload_corrupt"}
  - Log: include step + actionable message (“re-transfer artifact; verify size matches manifest”)

RV-07: Missing CRDs / wrong install order
- Purpose: regression coverage for known on-prem issues (restore attempted before CRDs installed).
- Failure injection:
  - Remove a required CRD from target cluster OR install chart without CRDs.
- Expected:
  - Pre-flight detects missing CRDs and fails fast
  - Error includes exact CRD names and remediation (“apply CRDs from release bundle version X”)
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_preflight_failed_total{reason="missing_crds"}
  - Log: missing CRD list + detected chart/installer version

RV-08: RBAC denied to read/write backup location
- Purpose: catch common misconfig: SA missing permissions, IAM policy too strict.
- Failure injection:
  - Remove get/list permissions for S3 prefix OR NFS mount read-only.
- Expected:
  - Failure in fetch step with precise permission error
  - Runbook hints: least-privilege policy snippet + where to apply
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="permission_denied"}
  - Log: include backend (s3/nfs), operation (GetObject/List), and principal/SA name

RV-09: Clock skew (cert validity + signed bundle)
- Purpose: air-gapped is often time-drifted; signing and TLS validation can break.
- Failure injection:
  - Shift node clock +/- 30 min (in lab)
- Expected:
  - If signature verification depends on time validity, error message should mention clock skew
  - TLS failures should be diagnosable (expired/not-yet-valid)
- Required signals:
  - Log: explicit detection when system time differs from NTP/expected (if available)
  - Metric: restore_failed_total{reason="time_invalid"} (optional; good to have)

RV-10: Air-gapped offline import/export flow
- Purpose: prove restore works without outbound calls.
- Setup:
  - Use offline bundle containing:
    - installer binary
    - checksum manifest
    - signature file
    - docs/runbook snapshot
  - Backup artifact transferred via removable media / offline copy
- Steps:
  1) Verify bundle signature/checksums locally
  2) Import artifact to local path
  3) Run restore with “offline” mode (no remote fetch)
- Expected:
  - No outbound network attempted (or explicitly documented allowed endpoints = none)
  - Restore succeeds and validation passes
- Required signals:
  - Log: “offline mode enabled” banner
  - Metric: restore_mode{mode="offline"}=1 (or included as label)
  - Optional: network policy audit showing no egress from restore job

RV-11: Post-restore smoke tests (API)
- Purpose: verify platform is functionally usable (minimum viable checks).
- Steps (minimum):
  - Health endpoints:
    - control-plane API /healthz returns OK
    - any required internal services report ready
  - State correctness checks:
    - list tenants => expected count and IDs
    - fetch routing policies => expected names/IDs and fallback configuration
    - verify model catalog pins/versions (whatever is supported in Private control plane)
  - Auth check:
    - admin auth works; tenant-scoped auth works; no cross-tenant leakage
- Expected:
  - All checks pass and are captured as a machine-readable validation report artifact
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_validation_pass{check_id}=1, restore_validation_fail{check_id}=0
  - Artifact: `validation-report.json` stored alongside restore logs

RV-12: Post-restore smoke tests (console/status endpoints)
- Purpose: ensure operators can see restore readiness in UI/automation.
- Steps:
  - Query backup/restore status endpoint (if enabled)
  - Confirm last restore timestamp, artifact_id, and validation result are surfaced
- Expected:
  - UI/API shows accurate status; no stale “last_success” values
- Required signals:
  - Endpoint: GET /private/backup-status (or equivalent) returns last_success_ts, last_restore_ts, last_validation

RV-13: Audit log export continuity + retention config restored
- Purpose: regulated customers (MedData-type) care about evidence + continuity.
- Steps:
  - Restore, then confirm audit log exporter configuration present
  - Run exporter in “batch export” mode (if configured) to confirm it can read from restored config
- Expected:
  - Retention/export settings match snapshot
  - Export can run without permission errors
- Required signals:
  - Metric: audit_export_success_total
  - Log: exporter includes retention window + pagination stats (no PII in logs)

RV-14: Large state scaling (perf)
- Purpose: ensure restore doesn’t degrade catastrophically with size.
- Setup:
  - Seed config DB with ~N tenants and policies (synthetic) to represent large enterprise.
- Expected:
  - Restore completes within acceptable time and resource usage.
- Required signals:
  - Metric: restore_peak_memory_bytes, restore_cpu_seconds (if available via job metrics)
  - Metric: restore_duration_seconds

Failure injection ideas (additional)
- Storage backend specific
  - S3: inject 403 on ListObjects; inject slow GET responses; object versioning mismatch; wrong region endpoint.
  - NFS: simulate stale file handles; permission mismatch (root_squash); throughput drop.
- Kubernetes / platform
  - Kill restore Job pod mid-run; ensure idempotency / safe retry behavior.
  - Force node eviction; ensure partial state not left.
  - Change image pull policy / registry unavailable (air-gapped registry errors).
- Data
  - Restore from older artifact format version (compat check should block or migrate with clear output).
  - Introduce migration failure (simulate with incompatible DB schema) and confirm rollback guidance.

Observability requirements (minimum set)
Metrics (names are illustrative; final names should align to ENG-9826 / PR-24344 conventions)
- backup_success (already tracked elsewhere, but needed for readiness)
- restore_success (1/0)
- restore_failed_total{reason}
- restore_duration_seconds{phase} (or separate phase timers)
- restore_validation_pass{check_id}
- restore_validation_fail{check_id,error_class}
- kms_decrypt_error_total{code}
- kms_decrypt_retry_total
- restore_bytes_total (download/unpack sizes)

Logs
- Structured JSON logs with fields:
  - artifact_id, artifact_format_version
  - source_platform_version, target_platform_version
  - step_name, step_status, duration_ms
  - error_class, error_message (sanitized), remediation_hint
- Explicit redaction policy:
  - never log plaintext secrets, decrypted payload, tokens
  - avoid logging full config objects; log IDs/counts only

Traces
- Single top-level trace per restore with child spans per step:
  - fetch, decrypt, verify, unpack, db_restore, migrations, apply_config, post_checks

Artifacts
- validation report (machine-readable) stored next to restore logs
- optionally: “restore plan” file that lists intended steps and detected environment details (helps Support)

Automation + cadence
- Staging automated restore drill: target weekly (P1), with ability to ratchet to daily once stable.
- On-prem/air-gapped drill: monthly in lab (manual steps + offline media simulation).
- Report to:
  - Slack channel (eng-oncall / eng-sre) with summary: PASS/FAIL, duration, top reasons
  - Create/append a ticket automatically on failure (ENG-9827 scope)

Exit criteria for Phase 2 (what we need before calling this “ready”)
- RV-01, 03, 04, 06, 07, 08, 10, 11 pass in A + D archetypes.
- At least one failure injection test per category produces a clear error with remediation steps (no “unknown error”).
- Dashboard exists for:
  - last restore drill result
  - restore duration trend
  - top failure reasons
- On-call runbook updated with:
  - how to rerun restore drill
  - where to find validation report
  - paging policy / thresholds

Known gaps / open questions
- Do we require quiesce for backups to be “consistent” or do we document eventual consistency guarantees per datastore?
- For brownfield restores: do we officially support in-place restore, or only “restore into clean cluster”? (Current guidance leans clean cluster for DR.)
- Where do we persist validation reports in air-gapped environments (NFS path vs local disk) and how long?
- Final list of P0 validation checks (need sign-off from Ethan + Vivek): tenants, routing, model pins, auth, audit exporter config.

Notes from DR rehearsal (2025-02-14) to incorporate
- One failure mode observed: restore attempted before CRDs installed; resulted in confusing kubectl errors. This is why RV-07 is P0.
- Another: RBAC for backup CronJob was too permissive in one env and too restrictive in another; we should validate least-privilege but with explicit error messaging (RV-08).

Appendix: quick validation checklist (operator-friendly)
- [ ] Restore run completed; exit code 0
- [ ] validation-report.json exists and all P0 checks PASS
- [ ] tenants list matches expected IDs (sample 2 tenants)
- [ ] routing policies present + fallback enabled as expected
- [ ] auth works (admin + tenant scoped)
- [ ] backup/restore metrics visible in dashboard
- [ ] no pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff; no failed hooks left behind

title: Dedicated Throughput SLO – metric exploration notes (SRE scratch)
preview:
Context
- Dedicated is moving toward formal throughput guarantees (tiers + shapes). We need a canonical metric that is: (a) hard to game, (b) stable across model/version + routing changes, (c) aligns with how customers experience “throughput”, and (d) can be enforced/alerted with reasonable operational burden.
- This doc captures SRE exploration (not final). Expect contradictions and open questions.

Goals for the metric
- Customer-relevant: reflects “how much work can I get done” on a Dedicated deployment.
- Comparable over time: changes in batching, caching, kernel selection, or model upgrades shouldn’t break continuity unless we explicitly change the commitment.
- Actionable: when we miss, we can quickly determine if it’s Redwood capacity, model regression, customer request pattern, or measurement artifact.
- Supports tier semantics: different floors, burst behavior, and possibly failover posture by tier.

Non-goals
- Perfectly model-agnostic measurement. Model/version affects tokens/sec. We may need to constrain what is covered under commitment (pinned model version, supported context lengths, etc.).
- Using customer app-level metrics. Commitment must be computed from Redwood-side telemetry.

Candidate metrics considered

Option A: Output tokens/sec (OTPS) per deployment
Definition (candidate):
- Numerator: sum of output tokens produced by the deployment during a window.
- Denominator: elapsed wall-clock time during that window.
- Reported as “output tokens per second” (OTPS).

Pros
- Maps to real GPU work + user-perceived speed (especially for streaming).
- Less sensitive to prompt length variability than total tokens/sec.
- Better for chat/generation where output is the dominant cost driver.

Cons / pitfalls
- Short completions can inflate perceived OTPS if we only count “generation active time” (needs careful denominator).
- If we count total wall-clock, idle time drags metric down and becomes gameable by customer traffic patterns (customer can stop sending traffic; do we then look “bad”?).
- If we count only time while requests are in-flight, it becomes gameable by micro-bursts and can hide tail latency/queueing issues.

Notes
- For Dedicated commitment, we likely want a “when under load” metric rather than “always-on regardless of utilization”. That suggests conditioning the measurement on a minimum concurrency / backlog signal.

Option B: Total tokens/sec (input + output) per deployment
Pros
- Better approximates GPU cost on some workloads where prompt ingestion dominates (long context).

Cons
- Easier to game: customer can send huge prompts and claim low throughput even if we are delivering reasonable output speed.
- Harder to align with what customers call throughput (“my app is slow”) vs “I am sending bigger prompts”.

Option C: Completed requests/sec (RPS) per deployment
Pros
- Easy to reason about for some customers.

Cons
- Highly dependent on request sizes. Not comparable across customers or even the same customer over time.
- Encourages undesirable behavior (optimize for tiny requests).

Option D: “Effective tokens/sec” normalized by request complexity
- E.g., weighted tokens/sec that penalizes long-context or includes a standardization.

Pros
- Tries to address comparability.

Cons
- Complexity and trust: hard to explain in contracts and hard to debug.

Current leaning (not final)
- Canonical throughput unit = output tokens/sec (OTPS), computed Redwood-side, attributed per deployment_id (Dedicated). This matches the direction in ADR-027 draft.

Attribution scope: per-deployment vs per-route
- Per-route can reflect mixed models/routes in the same deployment, but creates complicated contract language (“floor applies to each route?”).
- Per-deployment is simpler for the first version, but must handle multi-model deployments:
  - If a deployment serves multiple models, OTPS varies materially; a single floor can be unfair or impossible.
  - Proposal (draft): SLO commitment applies to deployments pinned to a specific model + version (or a Redwood-managed “compatibility set”). Multi-model deployments are either (a) excluded, (b) use per-route commitments, or (c) require Premium custom language.

Measurement conditioning: “under load” definition
Key question: When do we measure compliance?
We should avoid:
- Penalizing Redwood when customer sends little/no traffic.
- Allowing customers to send tiny trickle traffic and claim the floor is violated.
- Allowing Redwood to ignore prolonged slowdowns by claiming “not under load.”

Candidate “under load” gates (combine 1 + 1):
1) Concurrency gate:
- Measure only when there are >= N in-flight generation requests (or >= N active streams).
2) Backlog/queue gate:
- Measure only when queue depth > 0 OR admission control is non-empty OR scheduler reports “runnable tokens.”
3) Demand gate:
- Measure only when offered load (requested output tokens/sec) exceeds the committed floor by some margin.

Draft gate (for discussion)
- A minute is “eligible” if either:
  - avg_inflight_requests >= 1 AND p50_time_to_first_token (TTFT) is present (streaming active), OR
  - scheduler_queue_depth > 0 for >X% of the minute.

Concern
- If we use queue depth, batching can reduce queue depth even during saturation; also queue depth can be 0 while GPU is saturated if we admit tightly.

Denominator choice
We need consistency. Two common ways:
- Wall-clock OTPS: output_tokens / wall_time (e.g., 60s window). Simple but utilization-sensitive.
- Busy-time OTPS: output_tokens / model_busy_time. Less sensitive to utilization but can hide queueing problems.

Draft recommendation
- Use wall-clock OTPS but only on “eligible minutes” (under-load gated). This keeps it aligned with customer experience while avoiding idle penalties.

Streaming token accounting pitfalls
We hit multiple measurement traps in early prototypes.

Pitfall 1: Double counting streamed tokens
- If we emit token count on each partial chunk and also on final response completion, we can double count.
- Fix tracked in: GitHub redwood/pr-19422-fix-throughput-metric-double-counting-streaming.
- Takeaway: define a single canonical event:
  - Either “token_emitted” events only, aggregated per request_id, with idempotency keys; OR
  - “final_usage” at request end only, with accurate output token count.
- SRE preference: final_usage is simpler and less error-prone, but it delays visibility for long streams. Observability would like both, but SLO math should pick one.

Pitfall 2: TTFT and partial generation
- Some requests terminate early (client cancel, deadline, network drop). Should produced tokens count? Probably yes (we did work), but SLO should not be “free” for Redwood if we are slow and client times out.
- Proposed approach:
  - Count tokens actually emitted prior to termination.
  - But track cancellation_rate and client_deadline_rate as separate “exclusion candidate” signals.
  - Contractual exclusion should be narrow; we should not exclude all client cancels because customers may cancel due to Redwood slowness.

Batching impacts
Batching changes tokens/sec characteristics:
- Higher throughput at the cost of TTFT/latency.
- With continuous batching, the system can preserve high OTPS even when p95 latency is bad.

SLO implication
- If Dedicated guarantee is strictly throughput, we can meet OTPS while customers still complain about latency.
- Consider a paired SLO or at least a “guardrail”:
  - Throughput SLO is primary guarantee.
  - Latency (TTFT / per-token latency) tracked as secondary indicator; if degraded, oncall should still treat as incident even if OTPS is okay.
- For now, measure throughput alone but include “latency regression” triage step in runbooks.

Caching impacts (prefix/KV cache)
Question: If cache hits reduce GPU work, OTPS can spike and mask underlying capacity issues.
- From customer POV, cache hits are good. From “capacity guarantee” POV, cache can make a small deployment look like it meets a higher floor.

Potential approaches
1) Measure post-cache tokens only (status quo)
- Pros: reflects customer-perceived throughput.
- Cons: makes the commitment depend on cache hit rate, which is workload-dependent and gameable.

2) Separate “served tokens/sec” vs “billed tokens/sec” vs “computed tokens/sec”
- Served OTPS: what customer receives.
- Computed OTPS: tokens that actually required compute (exclude cached tokens).
- Billed OTPS: what we charge (may follow served).
- SLO would ideally be tied to computed OTPS to represent capacity guarantee.

3) Hybrid for v1
- Dedicated SLO uses served OTPS but only for workloads where caching is either disabled or bounded.
- E.g., “SLO excludes cache-augmented responses beyond X% cache hit rate” is not realistic contract language.

Current leaning
- For Dedicated tiers, we should define the guarantee in terms of served OTPS (because customers care about served throughput) but enforce a routing/capacity model based on computed load (to prevent oversubscription).
- Practically: keep SLO metric simple (served OTPS), but add internal saturation metrics based on compute time / GPU utilization to drive alerting + capacity actions.

Warmup and cold-start behavior
Dedicated deployments may have:
- Scale-to-zero disabled (usually), but can still have cold-ish behavior after idle periods (KV cache cold, compilation cache cold, page cache cold).
- Warm pool behavior may differ by tier (Premium might have warm spares).

SLO question
- Do we exclude the first X minutes after provisioning/rollout?
- Do we exclude minutes immediately following a model reload or node replacement?

Draft approach
- Exclude a limited “warmup window” from SLO evaluation:
  - After deployment creation or model version change: exclude first 15 minutes (or until health criteria met).
  - After node rotation/restart event: exclude first 5 minutes on that node, but not on the deployment aggregate if traffic is served by other nodes.
- Exclusions must be rare and auditable; otherwise the SLO is meaningless.

Model/version constraints
Throughput varies materially by:
- model architecture, quantization profile, context length, decoding params (top_p, temperature, etc.), tool-calling/structured output overhead.

Need a stance for the contract
- If we promise a floor, we must constrain the “supported configuration space.”

Draft constraint language (SRE suggestion)
- Commitment applies when:
  - model is one of the Redwood-supported models for that shape, pinned to an approved version; and
  - request context length <= the published max for the shape; and
  - decoding parameters within documented bounds (e.g., max_output_tokens <= X; no extreme beam search).
- Anything outside bounds is “best effort” and can be throttled.

Windows and aggregation
We need to choose:
- Base window: 1m vs 5m.
- Evaluation window for SLO: 30d typical, but Dedicated contracts might use monthly.

SRE preference
- Compute per-minute eligible OTPS.
- SLO compliance: percentage of eligible minutes where OTPS >= committed_floor.
- Why: handles bursty traffic; avoids a single bad minute being hidden by a high-traffic hour.

Potential issue
- If customer sends one eligible minute per day, the SLO becomes noisy. Might need minimum eligible minutes per day/week to evaluate and/or use a longer aggregation (5m) to reduce variance.

Alerting implications (burn-rate)
- We will likely use burn-rate alerts for the throughput SLO (ENG-19288).
- Burn-rate assumes we have a clean “good/bad event” definition per time bucket.
- For throughput, “bad” could be: eligible_minute AND OTPS < floor.

Tuning note
- Early alert noise expected due to measurement bugs and eligibility gating edge cases.
- We should stage:
  - phase 1: dashboard only (no paging), validate metric integrity.
  - phase 2: page on severe sustained misses.

Edge cases / gaming scenarios to consider
1) Customer sends many tiny requests with low max_output_tokens
- OTPS may look fine; customer might still complain due to overhead/TTFT.
- Not a reason to change OTPS metric, but ensure support playbook references TTFT.

2) Customer sends huge prompts (near max context)
- Output generation slows; OTPS drops.
- If contract is output-only, this could look like a miss even though the bottleneck is prefill.
- Mitigation: shape-specific max context + guidance; optionally define “supported context distribution” (hard to enforce).

3) Cache-heavy workloads
- Could artificially inflate served OTPS.
- Internal planner must not allocate commitments based on served OTPS if cache hit rates are high.

4) Customer-side throttling / retry storms
- If customer retries aggressively, we can see increased queueing and cancellations.
- Exclusion? Probably exclude only if we can attribute clearly (e.g., invalid auth retry loop), otherwise treat as real demand.

5) Streaming clients disconnecting
- If disconnects are due to customer network, not Redwood.
- But we need a robust way to attribute disconnect cause.

Telemetry requirements (what we must emit)
Per request (minimum)
- deployment_id
- route (if present)
- model_id + version + quantization profile
- region / cluster
- request_id
- start_time, first_token_time (if streaming), end_time
- input_tokens, output_tokens
- termination_reason (completed, client_cancel, server_error, timeout)
- cache flags (prefix cache hit? kv cache reuse?) ideally as booleans/counters

Per system (supporting)
- GPU utilization / SM occupancy proxy
- scheduler queue depth
- admitted vs rejected requests (admission control)
- node restart/rotation events
- rollout events (model version, config)

Open questions (to resolve in ADR/PRD)
- Do we anchor on served OTPS or computed OTPS for the customer-facing commitment?
- What is the eligibility gate for “under load” that is both fair and robust?
- How do we handle long-context workloads where prefill dominates and output OTPS understates compute?
- Multi-model deployments: exclude, per-route SLO, or require Premium custom commitments?
- How do we define/limit warmup exclusions so they’re auditable and not overused?
- Do we need a paired latency SLO/guardrail, or is it sufficient to treat as operational KPI?

Next steps (proposed)
- Align with Product (Lauren) on commitment simplicity vs accuracy tradeoffs.
- Validate metric integrity in staging + 1–2 pilot customers:
  - Compare “served OTPS” vs “GPU utilization” vs “queueing” during saturation.
  - Confirm streaming accounting is correct post PR-19422.
- Draft ADR decision:
  - Canonical metric + attribution scope (per deployment).
  - Eligibility gating.
  - Exclusions and warmup windows.
- Feed requirements to telemetry work (ENG-19244) and evaluator/alerting work (ENG-19288).

Personal note
- The biggest risk isn’t picking OTPS vs RPS; it’s creating a metric that is too eligibility-gated and therefore untrustworthy, OR too strict and therefore pages constantly on customer traffic artifacts. We should optimize for a definition that yields fast, confident triage.
text:
Context
- Dedicated is moving toward formal throughput guarantees (tiers + shapes). We need a canonical metric that is: (a) hard to game, (b) stable across model/version + routing changes, (c) aligns with how customers experience “throughput”, and (d) can be enforced/alerted with reasonable operational burden.
- This doc captures SRE exploration (not final). Expect contradictions and open questions.

Goals for the metric
- Customer-relevant: reflects “how much work can I get done” on a Dedicated deployment.
- Comparable over time: changes in batching, caching, kernel selection, or model upgrades shouldn’t break continuity unless we explicitly change the commitment.
- Actionable: when we miss, we can quickly determine if it’s Redwood capacity, model regression, customer request pattern, or measurement artifact.
- Supports tier semantics: different floors, burst behavior, and possibly failover posture by tier.

Non-goals
- Perfectly model-agnostic measurement. Model/version affects tokens/sec. We may need to constrain what is covered under commitment (pinned model version, supported context lengths, etc.).
- Using customer app-level metrics. Commitment must be computed from Redwood-side telemetry.

Candidate metrics considered

Option A: Output tokens/sec (OTPS) per deployment
Definition (candidate):
- Numerator: sum of output tokens produced by the deployment during a window.
- Denominator: elapsed wall-clock time during that window.
- Reported as “output tokens per second” (OTPS).

Pros
- Maps to real GPU work + user-perceived speed (especially for streaming).
- Less sensitive to prompt length variability than total tokens/sec.
- Better for chat/generation where output is the dominant cost driver.

Cons / pitfalls
- Short completions can inflate perceived OTPS if we only count “generation active time” (needs careful denominator).
- If we count total wall-clock, idle time drags metric down and becomes gameable by customer traffic patterns (customer can stop sending traffic; do we then look “bad”?).
- If we count only time while requests are in-flight, it becomes gameable by micro-bursts and can hide tail latency/queueing issues.

Notes
- For Dedicated commitment, we likely want a “when under load” metric rather than “always-on regardless of utilization”. That suggests conditioning the measurement on a minimum concurrency / backlog signal.

Option B: Total tokens/sec (input + output) per deployment
Pros
- Better approximates GPU cost on some workloads where prompt ingestion dominates (long context).

Cons
- Easier to game: customer can send huge prompts and claim low throughput even if we are delivering reasonable output speed.
- Harder to align with what customers call throughput (“my app is slow”) vs “I am sending bigger prompts”.

Option C: Completed requests/sec (RPS) per deployment
Pros
- Easy to reason about for some customers.

Cons
- Highly dependent on request sizes. Not comparable across customers or even the same customer over time.
- Encourages undesirable behavior (optimize for tiny requests).

Option D: “Effective tokens/sec” normalized by request complexity
- E.g., weighted tokens/sec that penalizes long-context or includes a standardization.

Pros
- Tries to address comparability.

Cons
- Complexity and trust: hard to explain in contracts and hard to debug.

Current leaning (not final)
- Canonical throughput unit = output tokens/sec (OTPS), computed Redwood-side, attributed per deployment_id (Dedicated). This matches the direction in ADR-027 draft.

Attribution scope: per-deployment vs per-route
- Per-route can reflect mixed models/routes in the same deployment, but creates complicated contract language (“floor applies to each route?”).
- Per-deployment is simpler for the first version, but must handle multi-model deployments:
  - If a deployment serves multiple models, OTPS varies materially; a single floor can be unfair or impossible.
  - Proposal (draft): SLO commitment applies to deployments pinned to a specific model + version (or a Redwood-managed “compatibility set”). Multi-model deployments are either (a) excluded, (b) use per-route commitments, or (c) require Premium custom language.

Measurement conditioning: “under load” definition
Key question: When do we measure compliance?
We should avoid:
- Penalizing Redwood when customer sends little/no traffic.
- Allowing customers to send tiny trickle traffic and claim the floor is violated.
- Allowing Redwood to ignore prolonged slowdowns by claiming “not under load.”

Candidate “under load” gates (combine 1 + 1):
1) Concurrency gate:
- Measure only when there are >= N in-flight generation requests (or >= N active streams).
2) Backlog/queue gate:
- Measure only when queue depth > 0 OR admission control is non-empty OR scheduler reports “runnable tokens.”
3) Demand gate:
- Measure only when offered load (requested output tokens/sec) exceeds the committed floor by some margin.

Draft gate (for discussion)
- A minute is “eligible” if either:
  - avg_inflight_requests >= 1 AND p50_time_to_first_token (TTFT) is present (streaming active), OR
  - scheduler_queue_depth > 0 for >X% of the minute.

- If we use queue depth, batching can reduce queue depth even during saturation; also queue depth can be 0 while GPU is saturated if we admit tightly.

- Wall-clock OTPS: output_tokens / wall_time (e.g., 60s window). Simple but utilization-sensitive.
- Busy-time OTPS: output_tokens / model_busy_time. Less sensitive to utilization but can hide queueing problems.

  - “final_usage” at request end only, with accurate output token count.
- SRE preference: final_usage is simpler and less error-prone, but it delays visibility for long streams. Observability would like both, but SLO math should pick one.

Pitfall 2: TTFT and partial generation

  - Count tokens actually emitted prior to termination.
  - But track cancellation_rate and client_deadline_rate as separate “exclusion candidate” signals.
  - Contractual exclusion should be narrow; we should not exclude all client cancels because customers may cancel due to Redwood slowness.

Batching impacts

- Higher throughput at the cost of TTFT/latency.
- With continuous batching, the system can preserve high OTPS even when p95 latency is bad.

SLO implication

  - Throughput SLO is primary guarantee.
  - Latency (TTFT / per-token latency) tracked as secondary indicator; if degraded, oncall should still treat as incident even if OTPS is okay.
- For now, measure throughput alone but include “latency regression” triage step in runbooks.

Caching impacts (prefix/KV cache)

- From customer POV, cache hits are good. From “capacity guarantee” POV, cache can make a small deployment look like it meets a higher floor.

- E.g., “SLO excludes cache-augmented responses beyond X% cache hit rate” is not realistic contract language.

- Practically: keep SLO metric simple (served OTPS), but add internal saturation metrics based on compute time / GPU utilization to drive alerting + capacity actions.

Dedicated deployments may have:
- Scale-to-zero disabled (usually), but can still have cold-ish behavior after idle periods (KV cache cold, compilation cache cold, page cache cold).
- Warm pool behavior may differ by tier (Premium might have warm spares).

SLO question
- Do we exclude the first X minutes after provisioning/rollout?
- Do we exclude minutes immediately following a model reload or node replacement?

Draft approach
- Exclude a limited “warmup window” from SLO evaluation:
  - After deployment creation or model version change: exclude first 15 minutes (or until health criteria met).
  - After node rotation/restart event: exclude first 5 minutes on that node, but not on the deployment aggregate if traffic is served by other nodes.
- Exclusions must be rare and auditable; otherwise the SLO is meaningless.

Model/version constraints
Throughput varies materially by:
- model architecture, quantization profile, context length, decoding params (top_p, temperature, etc.), tool-calling/structured output overhead.

  - request context length <= the published max for the shape; and

title: Template: Runtime LTS Release Readiness Review (SRE)
preview:
Purpose
- This is the SRE checklist + sign-off template for releasing a Runtime LTS patch (e.g., 1.18.1-lts) across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.
- Goal: prevent avoidable customer-impacting regressions by verifying (1) monitoring coverage, (2) rollback readiness, (3) load/perf results, (4) customer impact + comms.

When to run this review
- Required for every LTS patch release (scheduled monthly patches and out-of-band security fixes).
- Start: when RC build is cut and perf-canary gates are green (or waiver requested).
- End: prior to publishing artifacts / rollout start.

How to use
- Fill in the “Release metadata” and “Change summary” from the release PR.
- Attach links to perf reports, build artifacts, dashboards, and runbooks.
- Mark each item as PASS / FAIL / N/A and add notes.
- SRE sign-off is required unless explicitly waived by SRE on-call manager (rare; document waiver + rationale).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Release metadata
- Release name/version: ________________________________
- Branch/tag: _________________________________________
- Release type: [ ] Monthly patch  [ ] Out-of-band security  [ ] Hotfix (non-security)
- Intended publish date/time (UTC): _____________________
- Release manager (Release Eng): ________________________
- Runtime DRI: ________________________________________
- SRE DRI (this review): _______________________________
- Impacted deployment modes: [ ] Hosted  [ ] Dedicated  [ ] Private
- Customer cohorts affected: ____________________________
- Change window / maintenance window constraints (if any): __________________________________________

Links (required)
- Release PR / changelog: _______________________________
- Perf-canary summary artifact: __________________________
- CI run links (release branch): _________________________
- Known issues page (LTS): ______________________________
- Release notes draft: __________________________________
- Artifact manifest (images + checksums/provenance): ______
- Rollout plan / Console toggle plan (if applicable): ______

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1) Change summary + risk assessment
1.1 Summary (2–5 bullets, customer-facing language)
- 
- 

1.2 Category of changes (check all that apply)
- [ ] Kernel / attention / quantization path
- [ ] Scheduler / batching / queueing
- [ ] Memory/KV cache / compaction
- [ ] Networking / transport
- [ ] Build/packaging / image base
- [ ] Observability / metrics
- [ ] Security patch
- [ ] Other: _____________________

1.3 Backports included (list PRs)
- PR: ____________  Severity: ____  Notes: ______________________
- PR: ____________  Severity: ____  Notes: ______________________

1.4 Risk score (SRE)
- Risk level: [ ] Low  [ ] Medium  [ ] High
- Rationale (what could break, blast radius, reversibility):
  _______________________________________________________________

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2) Monitoring, alerting, and dashboards
2.1 Monitoring coverage (Hosted/Dedicated)
- [ ] Runtime service dashboards updated/verified for this release line (latency, error rate, throughput, queue depth)
- [ ] LTS-specific SLO dashboards exist and are accessible to on-call (traffic segmented by LTS channel)
- [ ] Error budget views verified (last 7d/30d) for LTS cohorts
- [ ] Canary dashboard exists (or documented manual queries) for rollout detection

2.2 Alerting
- [ ] Paging alerts in place for: elevated 5xx / crashloop / GPU OOM / severe p95 latency regression / queue backpressure
- [ ] Alert thresholds reviewed for LTS (tighter than mainline as needed)
- [ ] Alerts route correctly (SRE on-call, Runtime secondary, Release Eng FYI)
- [ ] Runbook links included in alert descriptions

2.3 Logs/traces
- [ ] Logs include version/commit SHA field (or equivalent) for correlation
- [ ] Tracing hooks (if used) verified for key routes

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3) Performance + load validation
3.1 Perf gates
- [ ] Perf-canary gates passed for required benchmark set(s)
- [ ] Regressions vs baseline within approved thresholds (p50/p95 latency and throughput)
- [ ] Benchmark variance assessed (runner contention/noisy neighbors ruled out)
- [ ] Any waivers documented with owner + follow-up plan

3.2 Coverage
- GPUs covered (check): [ ] H100  [ ] A100  [ ] L40S  [ ] Other: _________
- Workloads covered (check): [ ] short prompts  [ ] mixed lengths  [ ] long context  [ ] streaming  [ ] tool-calling/structured
- Models covered (list): __________________________________________

3.3 Load test / stress results (Dedicated + worst-case)
- [ ] Dedicated-style steady-state load validated (target QPS + context length distribution)
- [ ] Burst / overload behavior validated (backpressure and load-shedding)
- [ ] Tail latency under overload understood + acceptable for LTS

3.4 Perf risk callout (required if Medium/High)
- Expected perf deltas (if any): _________________________________
- Mitigation (config overrides, feature flags, rollback): __________

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4) Rollout strategy + rollback readiness
4.1 Rollout plan
- [ ] Rollout plan documented by environment (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- [ ] Canary stage defined (traffic %, duration, success metrics)
- [ ] Halt criteria defined (what triggers stop/rollback)
- [ ] Dedicated customer scheduling accounted for (no surprise upgrades)

4.2 Rollback
- [ ] Known-good rollback version identified (e.g., previous LTS patch)
- [ ] Rollback procedure tested (at least once per quarter, or for this release if high risk)
- [ ] Rollback artifacts available (images, helm charts, installer bundles)
- [ ] Rollback time estimate documented and acceptable

4.3 Config compatibility
- [ ] Backward compatible with existing configs/flags in LTS line
- [ ] Any deprecated flags documented + validated

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5) Private (installer/Helm) readiness
(Complete if Private is impacted or artifacts are published to Private channels.)
- [ ] Private installer can pin to this exact patch (version string verified)
- [ ] Checksums/provenance available and verification steps documented
- [ ] Helm values support selecting LTS tags + release note URL annotation
- [ ] Upgrade sequencing documented (control plane/runtime, if applicable)

6) Reliability and incident readiness

6.2 Escalation

7) Customer impact assessment + comms

7.2 Release notes quality (LTS “slower-change” requirements)

Appendix: Quick “stop the line” criteria (defaults)
- Sustained p95 latency regression above threshold for LTS cohort during canary

- Any missing/verifiability issue for Private artifacts (checksum mismatch, unsigned artifact)

text:
Purpose
- This is the SRE checklist + sign-off template for releasing a Runtime LTS patch (e.g., 1.18.1-lts) across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.
- Goal: prevent avoidable customer-impacting regressions by verifying (1) monitoring coverage, (2) rollback readiness, (3) load/perf results, (4) customer impact + comms.

When to run this review
- Required for every LTS patch release (scheduled monthly patches and out-of-band security fixes).
- Start: when RC build is cut and perf-canary gates are green (or waiver requested).
- End: prior to publishing artifacts / rollout start.

How to use
- Fill in the “Release metadata” and “Change summary” from the release PR.
- Attach links to perf reports, build artifacts, dashboards, and runbooks.
- Mark each item as PASS / FAIL / N/A and add notes.
- SRE sign-off is required unless explicitly waived by SRE on-call manager (rare; document waiver + rationale).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Release metadata
- Release name/version: ________________________________
- Branch/tag: _________________________________________
- Release type: [ ] Monthly patch  [ ] Out-of-band security  [ ] Hotfix (non-security)
- Intended publish date/time (UTC): _____________________
- Release manager (Release Eng): ________________________
- Runtime DRI: ________________________________________
- SRE DRI (this review): _______________________________
- Impacted deployment modes: [ ] Hosted  [ ] Dedicated  [ ] Private
- Customer cohorts affected: ____________________________
- Change window / maintenance window constraints (if any): __________________________________________

Links (required)
- Release PR / changelog: _______________________________
- Perf-canary summary artifact: __________________________
- CI run links (release branch): _________________________
- Known issues page (LTS): ______________________________
- Release notes draft: __________________________________
- Artifact manifest (images + checksums/provenance): ______
- Rollout plan / Console toggle plan (if applicable): ______

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1) Change summary + risk assessment
1.1 Summary (2–5 bullets, customer-facing language)
- 
- 

1.2 Category of changes (check all that apply)
- [ ] Kernel / attention / quantization path
- [ ] Scheduler / batching / queueing
- [ ] Memory/KV cache / compaction
- [ ] Networking / transport
- [ ] Build/packaging / image base
- [ ] Observability / metrics
- [ ] Security patch
- [ ] Other: _____________________

1.3 Backports included (list PRs)
- PR: ____________  Severity: ____  Notes: ______________________
- PR: ____________  Severity: ____  Notes: ______________________

1.4 Risk score (SRE)
- Risk level: [ ] Low  [ ] Medium  [ ] High
- Rationale (what could break, blast radius, reversibility):
  _______________________________________________________________

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2) Monitoring, alerting, and dashboards
2.1 Monitoring coverage (Hosted/Dedicated)
- [ ] Runtime service dashboards updated/verified for this release line (latency, error rate, throughput, queue depth)
- [ ] LTS-specific SLO dashboards exist and are accessible to on-call (traffic segmented by LTS channel)
- [ ] Error budget views verified (last 7d/30d) for LTS cohorts
- [ ] Canary dashboard exists (or documented manual queries) for rollout detection

2.2 Alerting
- [ ] Paging alerts in place for: elevated 5xx / crashloop / GPU OOM / severe p95 latency regression / queue backpressure
- [ ] Alert thresholds reviewed for LTS (tighter than mainline as needed)
- [ ] Alerts route correctly (SRE on-call, Runtime secondary, Release Eng FYI)
- [ ] Runbook links included in alert descriptions

2.3 Logs/traces
- [ ] Logs include version/commit SHA field (or equivalent) for correlation
- [ ] Tracing hooks (if used) verified for key routes

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3) Performance + load validation
3.1 Perf gates
- [ ] Perf-canary gates passed for required benchmark set(s)
- [ ] Regressions vs baseline within approved thresholds (p50/p95 latency and throughput)
- [ ] Benchmark variance assessed (runner contention/noisy neighbors ruled out)
- [ ] Any waivers documented with owner + follow-up plan

3.2 Coverage
- GPUs covered (check): [ ] H100  [ ] A100  [ ] L40S  [ ] Other: _________
- Workloads covered (check): [ ] short prompts  [ ] mixed lengths  [ ] long context  [ ] streaming  [ ] tool-calling/structured
- Models covered (list): __________________________________________

3.3 Load test / stress results (Dedicated + worst-case)
- [ ] Dedicated-style steady-state load validated (target QPS + context length distribution)
- [ ] Burst / overload behavior validated (backpressure and load-shedding)
- [ ] Tail latency under overload understood + acceptable for LTS

3.4 Perf risk callout (required if Medium/High)
- Expected perf deltas (if any): _________________________________
- Mitigation (config overrides, feature flags, rollback): __________

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4) Rollout strategy + rollback readiness
4.1 Rollout plan
- [ ] Rollout plan documented by environment (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- [ ] Canary stage defined (traffic %, duration, success metrics)
- [ ] Halt criteria defined (what triggers stop/rollback)
- [ ] Dedicated customer scheduling accounted for (no surprise upgrades)

4.2 Rollback
- [ ] Known-good rollback version identified (e.g., previous LTS patch)
- [ ] Rollback procedure tested (at least once per quarter, or for this release if high risk)
- [ ] Rollback artifacts available (images, helm charts, installer bundles)
- [ ] Rollback time estimate documented and acceptable

4.3 Config compatibility
- [ ] Backward compatible with existing configs/flags in LTS line
- [ ] Any deprecated flags documented + validated

PASS/FAIL/N/A: ________   Notes/Follow-ups:
_______________________________________________________________

--------------------------------------------------------------------

(Complete if Private is impacted or artifacts are published to Private channels.)
- [ ] Private installer can pin to this exact patch (version string verified)
- [ ] Checksums/provenance available and verification steps documented
- [ ] Helm values support selecting LTS tags + release note URL annotation
- [ ] Upgrade sequencing documented (control plane/runtime, if applicable)
- [ ] Rollback steps validated for Private

- [ ] Customer-impacting limitations documented (e.g., feature disabled for stability)

6.2 Escalation

- [ ] Support/CS escalation instructions updated (what data to collect)
